blocks:
Ayahs

ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنزَلَ عَلَىٰ عَبْدِهِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَلَمْ يَجْعَل لَّهُۥ عِوَجَا ۜ (١)
All praise belongs to Allah, who has sent down the Book to His servant and did not let any crookedness be in it, (1)
قَيِّمًا لِّيُنذِرَ بَأْسًا شَدِيدًا مِّن لَّدُنْهُ وَيُبَشِّرَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ٱلَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ أَنَّ لَهُمْ أَجْرًا حَسَنًا (٢)
[a Book] upright, to warn of a severe punishment from Him, and to give good news to the faithful who do righteous deeds, that there shall be for them a good reward, (2)
مَّـٰكِثِينَ فِيهِ أَبَدًا (٣)
to abide in it forever, (3)
وَيُنذِرَ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوا۟ ٱتَّخَذَ ٱللَّهُ وَلَدًا (٤)
and to warn those who say, ‘Allah has taken a son.’ (4)
مَّا لَهُم بِهِۦ مِنْ عِلْمٍ وَلَا لِـَٔابَآئِهِمْ ۚ كَبُرَتْ كَلِمَةً تَخْرُجُ مِنْ أَفْوَٰهِهِمْ ۚ إِن يَقُولُونَ إِلَّا كَذِبًا (٥)
They do not have any knowledge of that, nor did their fathers. Monstrous is the utterance that comes out of their mouths, and they say nothing but a lie. (5)

The Virtue of Surah Al-Kahf

Numerous narrations have been cited from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليهم السلام) regarding the virtue of this Surah, which express the extraordinary importance of its content. These include:

  1. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said: "Shall I not introduce to you a Surah which seventy thousand angels escorted during its revelation, and whose greatness filled the heavens and the earth?" The companions replied, "Yes." He said: "It is Surah Al-Kahf. Whoever recites it on Friday, Allah will forgive him until the next Friday (and according to one narration, He will protect him from sin)... and He will grant him a light that shines to the sky, and he will remain protected from the fitna (tribulation) of the Dajjal." [^1]
  2. In another hadith from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), we read: "Whoever memorizes ten verses from the beginning of Surah Al-Kahf, the 'Dajjal' shall not harm him; and whoever memorizes the final verses of the Surah, they shall be a light and radiance for him on the Day of Resurrection." [^2]
  3. We read from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام): "Whoever recites Surah Al-Kahf every Friday night shall die a martyr, will be resurrected with the martyrs, and will stand in the ranks of the martyrs on the Day of Resurrection." [^3]

We have stated many times that the greatness of the Surahs of the Quran and their spiritual effects and ethical blessings are due to their content—that is, faith and acting upon them.

Since one of the most important parts of this Surah is the story of the uprising of a group of distinguished youths against the tyrant and Dajjal of their time—an uprising that endangered their lives and brought them to the brink of death, yet Allah protected them—attention to this reality can ignite the light of faith in ready hearts and protect one against sins, the temptations of Dajjal-like figures, and becoming dissolved in a corrupt environment.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse being discussed. [^2] and [^3]: Same source.

The bone-chilling descriptions of the punishments of Hell found in the verses of this Surah, as well as the grim fate awaiting the arrogant which is extensively reflected in its verses, along with attention to the infinite knowledge of God reflected through an interesting example in this Surah, can all complete this effect.

It protects man from the tribulations of the devils, sheds the light of purity and infallibility into his heart, and ultimately joins him with the martyrs.

Content of Surah Al-Kahf

This Surah begins with the praise and glorification of Allah and ends with monotheism, faith, and righteous deeds.

The content of this Surah, like other "Meccan" Surahs, mostly explains the Origin (Creation) and the Resurrection, glad tidings and warnings. It also points to an important issue that Muslims in those days desperately needed: that a minority, no matter how small, must not surrender to a majority, no matter how seemingly strong and powerful, nor should they be dissolved in the corruption of the environment. Rather, like the small group of the People of the Cave (Ashab al-Kahf), they must separate their account from the corrupt environment and rise against it.

When they have the ability, they should continue the struggle, and in the absence of ability, they should migrate.

Furthermore, among the stories in this Surah is the story of two men: one very wealthy and affluent but faithless, and the other poor and destitute but a believer. However, the believer never lost his dignity and honor before that faithless individual; he advised and guided him as much as he could, and finally declared his disassociation, and victory was with him.

This is so that believers, in conditions like the beginning of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) mission, may know that if the faithless wealthy have some hustle and bustle, it is temporary and destined to be extinguished, just like the poverty and hardship of the people of faith.

Another part of this Surah refers to the story of Moses and Khidr (although the name Khidr is not mentioned in this Surah), showing how Moses (عليه السلام) could not exercise patience before actions that appeared offensive on the surface but were full of wisdom in their hidden reality. However, after Khidr's explanations, he became fully aware of the depth of the matters and regretted his impatience.

This is also a lesson for everyone, not to look only at the appearances of events and occurrences, and to know that beneath these appearances lies a very deep and meaningful inner reality.

Tafsir Nemouneh; Vol. 12; p. 338

Another part of this Surah describes the account of Dhu al-Qarnayn: how he traversed the East and West of the world, encountered various nations with very different customs and traditions, and finally, with the help of a group of people, rose to confront the conspiracy of "Gog" and "Magog" (Yajuj and Majuj) and built an iron dam in their path, cutting off their influence. (A full explanation of all these will come later, God willing). This is so that Muslims, with a broader vision, may prepare themselves to influence the East and West of the world and join hands in unity to fight against the "Gogs" and "Magogs"!

Interestingly, three stories are mentioned in this Surah (the story of the People of the Cave, the story of Moses and Khidr, and the story of Dhu al-Qarnayn) which, unlike most Quranic stories, are not mentioned anywhere else in the Quran (only in Surah Al-Anbiya, verse 96, is the issue of Gog and Magog mentioned without mentioning the name of Dhu al-Qarnayn). This is one of the unique characteristics of this Surah.

In any case, its content is rich from every perspective and is educational.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ‌

الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي أَنْزَلَ عَلى‌ عَبْدِهِ الْكِتابَ وَ لَمْ يَجْعَلْ لَهُ عِوَجاً (1) قَيِّماً لِيُنْذِرَ بَأْساً شَدِيداً مِنْ لَدُنْهُ وَ يُبَشِّرَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ الَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ الصَّالِحاتِ أَنَّ لَهُمْ أَجْراً حَسَناً (2) ماكِثِينَ فِيهِ أَبَداً (3) وَ يُنْذِرَ الَّذِينَ قالُوا اتَّخَذَ اللَّهُ وَلَداً (4)

ما لَهُمْ بِهِ مِنْ عِلْمٍ وَ لا لِآبائِهِمْ كَبُرَتْ كَلِمَةً تَخْرُجُ مِنْ أَفْواهِهِمْ إِنْ يَقُولُونَ إِلاَّ كَذِباً (5)

Commentary

Beginning with the Name of God and the Quran

Surah Al-Kahf, like some other Surahs of the Quran, begins with the praise and glorification of Allah. Since praise and glorification are for an important and worthy action or attribute, here the praise is expressed for the revelation of the Quran, which is free from any crookedness or deviation. It says:

"Praise be to Allah, Who has sent down upon His servant the Book and has not placed therein any crookedness" (الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي أَنْزَلَ عَلى‌ عَبْدِهِ الْكِتابَ وَ لَمْ يَجْعَلْ لَهُ عِوَجاً).

"A Book that is firm and stable, balanced and straight, and both an upholder of human society and a guardian of other heavenly books" (قيما). "To warn the evildoers and the dark-hearted of a severe punishment from Him" (لِيُنْذِرَ بَأْساً شَدِيداً مِنْ لَدُنْهُ‌).

"And to give glad tidings to the true believers who constantly perform righteous deeds, that a great and goodly reward awaits them" (وَ يُبَشِّرَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ الَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ الصَّالِحاتِ أَنَّ لَهُمْ أَجْراً حَسَناً).

"A reward and recompense that is eternal, and they shall abide therein forever" (ماكِثِينَ فِيهِ أَبَداً).

Then, it points to one of the general deviations of the opponents—including Christians, Jews, and polytheists—saying: "Another objective of this heavenly Book is that the Prophet, by means of it, warns those who claimed that Allah has taken a son" (وَ يُنْذِرَ الَّذِينَ قالُوا اتَّخَذَ اللَّهُ وَلَداً). It warns the Christians for their belief that Christ is the son of God, the Jews for their belief in the sonship of Ezra (Uzair), and the polytheists for imagining that the angels were the daughters of God.

Then, it proceeds to a fundamental principle to invalidate such hollow and baseless claims, saying: "They have no knowledge or certainty regarding this statement, and if they follow their fathers, they too were like this" (ما لَهُمْ بِهِ مِنْ عِلْمٍ وَ لا لِآبائِهِمْ‌). "But a very grave and terrifying word comes out of their mouths" (كَبُرَتْ كَلِمَةً تَخْرُجُ مِنْ أَفْواهِهِمْ‌).

God and being a physical body? God and having a child? God and material needs? And finally, God and being limited? What terrifying words?!...

Indeed, "they say nothing but a lie" (إِنْ يَقُولُونَ إِلَّا كَذِباً).

Key Points

1- Beginning the Surah with "Praise" of Allah

Five Surahs of the Quran begin with "الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ". In these five Surahs, after praising Allah, the issue of the creation of the heavens and the earth (or the ownership of the heavens and the earth) or the nurturing of the worlds is mentioned, except in the Surah under discussion, where the revelation of the Quran to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) follows this praise.

In fact, in those four Surahs (Surah Al-An'am, Saba, Fatir, and Al-Hamd), the "Book of Creation" (Takwin) is discussed, but in Surah Al-Kahf, which is under discussion, the "Written Book" (Tadwin) is discussed. We know that each of these two books—namely the "Quran" and the "Universe"—complements the other. This expression also indicates that the Quran has a weight equal to the weight of the entire creation and is a blessing equivalent to the blessing of the universe! Fundamentally, the issue of nurturing the worlds mentioned in the phrase "الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ" is not possible without benefiting from this great heavenly Book.

2- A Firm, Straight, and Guardian Book

"Qayyim" (on the pattern of "Sayyid") is derived from the root "Qiyam" and here means firm, stable, and steadfast. In addition, it means the upholder, protector, and guardian of other books. At the same time, it conveys the meaning of moderation, rectitude, and being free from any crookedness or deviation.

This word, which appears as a description for the Quran after the description of "lack of crookedness" in the above verses, is both an emphasis on the rectitude and moderation of the Quran and its being free from any contradiction, and an indication of the eternity of this great heavenly Book and its being a model for preserving originalities, correcting deviations, and guarding the laws of Allah and human justice and virtue.

This attribute (Qayyim) is actually a derivation from the attribute of "Qayyumiyyah" of the Lord, by virtue of which Allah is the Protector and Guardian of all beings and things in the world. "We are established by You as You are Self-Established!" The Quran, which is the Word of Allah, also possesses this state.

It is noteworthy that the description "Qayyim" appears repeatedly in the verses of the Quran regarding the religion of Islam, and the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was even commanded to "set your face toward the firm and straight religion" (فَأقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ الْقَيِّمِ) (Surah Ar-Rum, verse 43).

What was said above in the interpretation of "Qayyim" is a comprehensive meaning that encompasses all the various interpretations mentioned by commentators: some have interpreted it as a book that is never abrogated, some as the guardian of previous books, some as the upholder of religious affairs, and some as a book in which there is no disagreement or contradiction. However, all these meanings are gathered in that comprehensive concept we mentioned.

Some commentators have considered the phrase "لَمْ يَجْعَلْ لَهُ عِوَجاً" to refer to the eloquence of the Quran's words, while taking "Qayyiman" to mean the rhetoric and rectitude of its content. [^1] However, there is no clear evidence for this distinction, and it seems more likely that these two are reinforcements of each other, with the difference that "Qayyim" has a broader concept—meaning that in addition to the concept of inherent rectitude, it also includes the concept of guarding, reforming, and protecting others. [^1]

[^1]: "Ruh al-Ma'ani," part 15, under the verse being discussed.

  1. In the above verses, after mentioning the issue of warning in a broad and absolute manner, the warning is specifically stated regarding those who claimed a son for Allah. This shows that this deviation has a special significance.

This doctrinal deviation, as we said above, is not exclusive to Christians; rather, the Jews and polytheists also shared in it, and it was considered almost a general belief in the environment where the Quran was revealed. We know that such a complex completely destroys the spirit of monotheism, places God at the level of material and physical beings with human emotions and feelings, attributes to Him a likeness and a partner, and considers Him needy. For this reason, this point has been specifically highlighted.

In Surah Yunus, verse 68, we read: "They say, 'Allah has taken a son.' Glory be to Him! He is the Self-Sufficient (Rich)."

And in Surah Maryam, verses 88 to 91, we read: "And they say, 'The Most Merciful has taken a son.' You have done an atrocious thing. The heavens almost rupture therefrom and the earth splits open and the mountains fall in ruins, that they attribute to the Most Merciful a son."

This extremely intense expression is evidence that the grim consequences of this discordant belief are very vast and extensive. Indeed, it is so, because its result is bringing God down from the peak of His greatness and placing Him at the level of lowly material beings. [^1]

[^1]: Regarding the issue of the Trinity and the belief in the sonship of God in the case of Prophet Jesus, we have spoken in detail in Volume 4 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 220 onwards.

4- Claim Without Evidence

An examination of deviant beliefs shows that most of them originate from claims without evidence, which were sometimes expressed as a false slogan by someone and followed by others, or passed from one generation to another as the tradition of ancestors. The Quran implicitly teaches us to strictly avoid claims without evidence at all times, regardless of who makes them or whom they concern.

In the above verses, Allah has considered such an act as grave and terrifying, and introduced it as the source of falsehood and lies.

This is a principle that if Muslims followed in all their lives—meaning they say nothing without evidence, accept nothing without evidence, and pay no heed to rumors and claims devoid of proof—many of their disorders would be rectified.

5- Righteous Deed as a Continuous Program

In the above verses, when speaking of the believers, "righteous deed" is mentioned as their continuous program, because the phrase "يعملون الصالحات" is in the present continuous tense (mudaari'), and we know that the present continuous tense indicates continuity.

In fact, it must be so, because performing one or a few good deeds might happen by chance or for specific reasons by anyone, and is never evidence of true faith. What is evidence of true faith is continuity in righteous deeds.

6- The final word here is that when the Quran wants to state the revelation of this heavenly Book in the above verses, it says: "Praise be to Allah Who sent down this Book upon His servant," and this is evidence that the expression "servant" ('abd) is the most proud and magnificent description that can be given to a human being—a human who is truly a servant of God, considers everything he has as belonging to Him, has his eyes on His command and his ears for His order, thinks of none but Him, walks no path but His, and whose pride is that he is His devoted servant.

Ayahs

فَلَعَلَّكَ بَـٰخِعٌ نَّفْسَكَ عَلَىٰٓ ءَاثَـٰرِهِمْ إِن لَّمْ يُؤْمِنُوا۟ بِهَـٰذَا ٱلْحَدِيثِ أَسَفًا (٦)
You are liable to imperil your life out of grief for their sake, if they should not believe this discourse. (6)
إِنَّا جَعَلْنَا مَا عَلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ زِينَةً لَّهَا لِنَبْلُوَهُمْ أَيُّهُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلًا (٧)
Indeed We have made whatever is on the earth an adornment for it that We may test them [to see] which of them is best in conduct. (7)
وَإِنَّا لَجَـٰعِلُونَ مَا عَلَيْهَا صَعِيدًا جُرُزًا (٨)
And indeed We will turn whatever is on it into a barren plain. (8)

Commentary

Do Not Grieve; The World is a Field of Trial

Since the previous verses discussed the messengership and leadership of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), the first verse under discussion points to one of the most important conditions of leadership, which is compassion for the Ummah, saying: "Perhaps you will kill yourself with grief, following after them, if they do not believe in this narration [the Quran] out of sorrow?" (فَلَعَلَّكَ باخِعٌ نَفْسَكَ عَلى‌ آثارِهِمْ إِنْ لَمْ يُؤْمِنُوا بِهذَا الْحَدِيثِ أَسَفاً)

Here, several points should be noted

[What is "Bakhi'"]

1- "Bakhi'" (باخع) is derived from the root "Bakh'" (بخع - rhyming with Nakhl), meaning to destroy oneself out of intense grief and sorrow; in other words, "to die of heartbreak."

2- The word "Asafa" (اسفا), which conveys the intensity of grief and sorrow, serves as an emphasis on this matter.

3- "Athar" (آثار) is the plural of "Athar" (اثر), which originally means a footprint, but it is applied to any remaining sign or trace of something.

The choice of this expression in the above verses points to a subtle point: sometimes a person leaves a place, and because the event is recent, their traces remain; but when time passes, the traces also fade. This means: you are so distressed by their lack of faith that even before their traces have faded, you wish to destroy yourself with grief.

It is also possible that "traces" refers to their deeds and behavior.

4- Referring to the Quran as "Hadith" (حديث - narration/discourse) points to the innovations and freshness of this great Heavenly Book. That is, they would not even go to the trouble of examining this new book with its fresh content, and this is evidence of ultimate ignorance—that a person passes by such an important and new matter with indifference.

The Extraordinary Compassion of Divine Leaders

It is clearly understood from the verses of the Quran and history that Divine leaders suffered from the deviation of the people more than can be imagined. They were passionate about their faith; they were pained to see thirsty souls sitting beside a spring of pure water yet crying out in thirst. They shed tears, they prayed, they strove day and night, they preached in secret and in public, they cried out in private and in gatherings, and they grieved because people had left the clear and straight path to wander aimlessly—a soul-crushing grief that sometimes brought them to the brink of death! Truly, unless it is so, "leadership" in its deep sense will not be realized! Sometimes this state of sorrow became so intense that the life of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was endangered, and Allah would comfort him.

In Surah Ash-Shu'ara, verses 3 and 4, we read: لَعَلَّكَ باخِعٌ نَفْسَكَ أَلَّا يَكُونُوا مُؤْمِنِينَ إِنْ نَشَأْ نُنَزِّلْ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنَ السَّماءِ آيَةً فَظَلَّتْ أَعْناقُهُمْ لَها خاضِعِينَ: "Perhaps you will kill yourself because they do not become believers. If We willed, We could send down to them from the sky a sign for which their necks would remain humbled."

The next verse provides a depiction of the state of this world as a field of trial for human beings and an explanation of the human trajectory on this path.

First, It says: "Indeed, We have made what is on the earth as an adornment for it" (إِنَّا جَعَلْنا ما عَلَى الْأَرْضِ زِينَةً لَها).

We created a glamorous world where every corner captivates the heart, occupies the eyes, and awakens various motives within man, so that amidst the conflict of these motives and the brilliance of this glamour and these enchanting faces, man may be placed upon the seat of trial to display the extent of his power of faith, strength of will, spirituality, and virtue.

Therefore, He immediately adds: "That We may test them [as to] which of them is best in deed" (لِنَبْلُوَهُمْ أَيُّهُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلًا).

Some commentators have sought to limit the concept of "what is on the earth" to "scholars" or specifically to "men," saying they are the adornment of the earth, whereas this word has a broad meaning that includes all beings on the earth.

Interestingly, the expression used is "best in deed" (أَحْسَنُ عَمَلًا), not "most in deed," indicating that what holds value in the presence of Allah is the goodness of the deed and its high quality, not its abundance, quantity, or number.

In any case, this is a warning to all humans and all Muslims not to be deceived by the glamour in this field of Divine trial. Instead of becoming attached to these deceptive manifestations, they should think of the goodness of their deeds.

Then He says: These are not lasting and will eventually be erased and destroyed; We will eliminate everything on the earth "and indeed, We will make that which is upon it [into] a barren, dry ground" (وَ إِنَّا لَجاعِلُونَ ما عَلَيْها صَعِيداً جُرُزاً).

"Sa'id" (صعيد) is derived from the root "Su'ud" (صعود) and here means the surface of the earth—a surface where the soil is completely visible.

And "Juruz" (جرز) refers to land where no vegetation grows, as if it "eats" its own plants; in other words, "Juruz" is said of a land where all plants have perished due to drought and lack of rain.

Yes, this beautiful and enchanting scenery that we see in the spring in the heart of the plains and mountains—where flowers laugh, plants dance, leaves whisper, and streams sing songs of joy—does not remain in this state. The season of autumn arrives, branches become bare, streams fall silent, buds wither, leaves fade, and the sound of life sinks into silence.

The glamorous life of humans is the same; these sky-scraping palaces, these colorful clothes, these various blessings, these servants ready to assist, and these posts and positions are not eternal. A day will come when nothing remains of these societies but a dry and silent graveyard, and this is a great lesson of admonition.

Ayahs

أَمْ حَسِبْتَ أَنَّ أَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلْكَهْفِ وَٱلرَّقِيمِ كَانُوا۟ مِنْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا عَجَبًا (٩)
Do you suppose that the Companions of the Cave and the Inscription were among Our wonderful signs? (9)
إِذْ أَوَى ٱلْفِتْيَةُ إِلَى ٱلْكَهْفِ فَقَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَآ ءَاتِنَا مِن لَّدُنكَ رَحْمَةً وَهَيِّئْ لَنَا مِنْ أَمْرِنَا رَشَدًا (١٠)
When the youths took refuge in the Cave, they said, ‘Our Lord! Grant us a mercy from Yourself, and help us on to rectitude in our affair.’ (10)
فَضَرَبْنَا عَلَىٰٓ ءَاذَانِهِمْ فِى ٱلْكَهْفِ سِنِينَ عَدَدًا (١١)
So We put them to sleep in the Cave for several years. (11)
ثُمَّ بَعَثْنَـٰهُمْ لِنَعْلَمَ أَىُّ ٱلْحِزْبَيْنِ أَحْصَىٰ لِمَا لَبِثُوٓا۟ أَمَدًا (١٢)
Then We aroused them that We might know which of the two groups better reckoned the period they had stayed. (12)

Circumstances of Revelation

Commentators have narrated a "Circumstance of Revelation" for the above verses, the summary of which is as follows:

A group from the leaders of the Quraysh sent two of their companions to the Jewish scholars in Medina to investigate the call of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, to see if anything regarding this matter could be found in the previous scriptures.

They came to Medina, contacted the Jewish scholars, and recounted the words of the Quraysh.

The Jewish scholars told them: "Ask Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله three questions; if he provides sufficient answers to all of them, he is a prophet from Allah (and according to some narrations, if he answers two sufficiently and gives a brief, ambiguous answer to one, he is a prophet). Otherwise, he is a liar, and you may decide whatever you wish concerning him."

"First, ask him: What was the story of that group of youths who, in the distant past, separated from their people? For they had a wondrous tale! Also, ask him: Who was the man who traversed the earth and reached the East and the West of the world, and what was his story?"

"And also ask: What is the reality of the Spirit (Ruh)?"

They set out and returned to Mecca, met with the leaders of the Quraysh, and said: "We have found the criteria for measuring the truthfulness or falsehood of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله." Then they recounted their experience, went to the Prophet, and posed their questions.

The Prophet said: "I will answer you tomorrow"—but he did not say "Insha'Allah" (If Allah wills). Fifteen days and nights passed without revelation descending upon the Prophet from Allah, and Jibril (Gabriel) did not come to him. This caused the people of Mecca to spread rumors and say unseemly things regarding the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

This weighed heavily on the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, but finally, Jibril arrived and brought Surah Al-Kahf from Allah, which contained the story of that group of youths and also that world-traveling man. Additionally, he revealed the verse "They ask you about the Spirit..." (يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الرُّوحِ) to the Prophet.

The Prophet asked Jibril: "Why did you delay so long?" He replied: "I do not descend except by the command of your Lord; I did not have permission!" (It is worth noting that two parts of the answers to the three questions appear in this Surah, but the verse regarding the Spirit passed in Surah Bani Israel [Al-Isra]. It is not uncommon in the Quran for a verse to be revealed on a certain occasion and then placed within a specific Surah by the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله command).

Commentary

The Beginning of the Story of the People of the Cave

In the preceding verses, a depiction of the life of this world was presented, along with the nature of this arena of human trial and the path of their lives. Since the Quran often personifies sensitive general issues through parables or examples from past history, it begins here by narrating the story of the "People of the Cave" (Ashab al-Kahf), mentioning them as a "model" and an "example."

A group of intelligent and faithful youths, who lived a life of luxury amidst various comforts, turned their backs on all of it to preserve their faith and struggle against the tyrant of their time. They took refuge in a cave in a mountain that was void of everything, and in this way, they demonstrated their steadfastness and fortitude in the path of faith.

Interestingly, the Quran here employs one of the principles of eloquence and rhetoric by first recounting the story of this group in brief—to prepare the minds of the listeners—within four verses, and then proceeds to detail it within fourteen verses.

First, It says: "Or did you think that the People of the Cave and the Inscription were among Our wondrous signs?" (أَمْ حَسِبْتَ أَنَّ أَصْحابَ الْكَهْفِ وَ الرَّقِيمِ كانُوا مِنْ آياتِنا عَجَباً).

We have more wondrous signs in the heavens and the earth, each of which is an example of the greatness and majesty of creation. Furthermore, in your own life, there are wondrous secrets, each a sign of the truth of your call. Likewise, in this great Heavenly Book, there are many wondrous verses, and certainly, the story of the People of the Cave is not more amazing than them.

The reason this group is named "Ashab al-Kahf" (Companions of the Cave) is that they took refuge in a cave to save their lives, as will be mentioned in the description of their circumstances.

As for "Al-Raqim," it originally comes from the root "raqam," meaning writing [1]. According to the majority of commentators, this is another name for the People of the Cave, because eventually, their names were written on a tablet and fixed at the entrance of the cave.

Some also consider it the name of the mountain in which the cave was located.

Some consider it the name of the land where that mountain was situated.

And some say it is the name of the city and land from which the People of the Cave emerged, but the first meaning seems more correct.

However, the possibility suggested by some that the "People of the Inscription" (Ashab al-Raqim) were a different group from the "People of the Cave"—and for whom a story is narrated in some reports—is not consistent with the apparent meaning of the verse. The apparent meaning of the verse above is that the People of the Cave and the Inscription were one group; hence, after mentioning these two titles, It only proceeds to narrate the story of the People of the Cave and mentions nothing else, which itself is evidence of their unity.

[1]: Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "Raqam (rhyming with zakhm) means bold and thick writing, and some have considered it to mean the dotting of a script (in any case, Raqim means a book or a tablet or a letter in which something is written)."

In the well-known narrations mentioned in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn under this verse regarding three people who were trapped in a cave—each of whom called upon Allah by a sincere deed they had performed and were delivered from that narrow place—there is no mention of the title "Ashab al-Raqim" at all, although this title has appeared in some books of commentary.

In any case, there should be no doubt that these two (Ashab al-Kahf and al-Raqim) refer to one group, and the Circumstances of Revelation of the verses also confirm this fact.

Then It says: "Remember when the youths took refuge in the cave" (إِذْ أَوَى الْفِتْيَةُ إِلَى الْكَهْفِ). Having lost all worldly means, they turned to the threshold of Allah: "and said: 'Our Lord! Grant us mercy from Yourself'" (فَقالُوا رَبَّنا آتِنا مِنْ لَدُنْكَ رَحْمَةً) "'and provide for us a right course in our affair'" (وَ هَيِّئْ لَنا مِنْ أَمْرِنا رَشَداً).

A way that delivers us from this bottleneck, brings us closer to Your pleasure and satisfaction—a way in which there is goodness, felicity, and the fulfillment of duty.

We answered their prayer: "So We cast [a veil of sleep] over their ears in the cave for a number of years" (فَضَرَبْنا عَلَى آذانِهِمْ فِي الْكَهْفِ سِنِينَ عَدَداً).

"Then We raised them up [and awakened them] so that We might know which of the two groups best calculated the duration they had remained" (ثُمَّ بَعَثْناهُمْ لِنَعْلَمَ أَيُّ الْحِزْبَيْنِ أَحْصى‌ لِما لَبِثُوا أَمَداً).

Key Points

  1. The phrase "awa al-fityah" is derived from the root "ma'wa," which means "a place of safety and security," indicating that these youths, fleeing from a corrupt environment, felt a sense of peace when they reached the cave.

  2. "Fityah" is the plural of "fata," which originally means "a fresh and vigorous youth," but it is sometimes applied to older individuals who possess a youthful and vibrant spirit. Usually, this word is accompanied by a type of praise for qualities of chivalry, resistance, courage, and submission to the Truth.

Evidence for this is a hadith narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: The Imam عليه السلام asked one of his companions, "Who is called a 'fata'?" He replied, "We call a young person a 'fata'." The Imam عليه السلام said:

"Do you not know that the People of the Cave were all middle-aged men, yet Allah named them 'fityah' because of their faith?"

Then he added:

"Whoever believes in Allah and practices Taqwa (piety) is a 'fata' (chivalrous youth)" [1]. A similar hadith is narrated in Rawdah al-Kafi from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام [2].

  1. The expression "min ladunka rahmatan" (mercy from Your own presence) indicates that when they took refuge in the cave, they saw themselves cut off from all sides, and all external means and instruments had failed them; they hoped only for the mercy of Allah.

  2. The phrase "darabna 'ala adhanihim" (We struck upon their ears) in the Arabic language is a subtle metaphor for "putting to sleep." It is as if a veil or curtain is cast over a person's ear so they do not hear any speech, and this veil is the veil of sleep.

For this reason, true sleep is that which disables a person's hearing, and also for this reason, when someone wants to wake another, they usually do so through calling out and penetrating their hearing.

  1. The expression "sinina 'adada" (numerous years) indicates that their sleep lasted for many long years, as the explanation will come in the commentary of the future verses, God willing.

  2. The expression "ba'athnahum" (We raised them) regarding their awakening is perhaps because their sleep was so long that it was like death, and their awakening was like the Resurrection and life after death.

  3. The phrase "li-na'lama" (so that We might know...) does not mean that Allah intended to acquire new knowledge here. This expression is frequent in the Quran, and it refers to the realization of Divine knowledge; meaning, We woke them from sleep so that this reality would manifest—that they would question each other about the duration of their sleep.

  4. The expression "ayyu al-hizbayn" (which of the two groups) refers to what will come in the commentary of the future verses: that after awakening, they differed regarding the amount of their sleep; some thought it was a day, and some half a day, while they had slept for many long years.

As for what some have said—that this expression is evidence that the "People of the Inscription" were different from the "People of the Cave"—it is a very far-fetched claim that requires no further explanation [1].

[1] and [2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, pp. 244 and 245. [1]: The above view was mentioned by the author of A'lam al-Quran on page 179 of that book.

Ayahs

نَّحْنُ نَقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ نَبَأَهُم بِٱلْحَقِّ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ فِتْيَةٌ ءَامَنُوا۟ بِرَبِّهِمْ وَزِدْنَـٰهُمْ هُدًى (١٣)
We relate to you their account in truth. They were indeed youths who had faith in their Lord, and We had enhanced them in guidance, (13)
وَرَبَطْنَا عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ إِذْ قَامُوا۟ فَقَالُوا۟ رَبُّنَا رَبُّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ لَن نَّدْعُوَا۟ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ إِلَـٰهًا ۖ لَّقَدْ قُلْنَآ إِذًا شَطَطًا (١٤)
and fortified their hearts, when they stood up and said, ‘Our Lord is the Lord of the heavens and the earth. We will never invoke any god besides Him, for then we shall certainly have said an atrocious lie. (14)
هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ قَوْمُنَا ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ ءَالِهَةً ۖ لَّوْلَا يَأْتُونَ عَلَيْهِم بِسُلْطَـٰنٍۭ بَيِّنٍ ۖ فَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا (١٥)
These—our people—have taken gods besides Him. Why do they not bring any clear authority touching them? So who is a greater wrongdoer than he who fabricates a lie against Allah? (15)
وَإِذِ ٱعْتَزَلْتُمُوهُمْ وَمَا يَعْبُدُونَ إِلَّا ٱللَّهَ فَأْوُۥٓا۟ إِلَى ٱلْكَهْفِ يَنشُرْ لَكُمْ رَبُّكُم مِّن رَّحْمَتِهِۦ وَيُهَيِّئْ لَكُم مِّنْ أَمْرِكُم مِّرْفَقًا (١٦)
When you have dissociated yourselves from them and from what they worship except Allah, then take refuge in the Cave. Your Lord will unfold His mercy for you, and He will help you on to ease in your affair.’ (16)

Commentary

The Detailed Account of the Companions of the Cave

As we mentioned, after the brief overview of this story, the Holy Quran proceeds to its detailed explanation within fourteen verses, beginning the discourse as follows: "We relate to you their story in truth" (نَحْنُ نَقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ نَبَأَهُمْ بِالْحَقِّ).

A narrative free from any superstition, baseless claims, or incorrect statements.

"Indeed, they were youths who believed in their Lord, and We increased them in guidance" (إِنَّهُمْ فِتْيَةٌ آمَنُوا بِرَبِّهِمْ وَ زِدْناهُمْ هُدىً).

The word "fityah" (فتية), as previously noted, is the plural of "fata" (فتى), meaning a fresh and vigorous youth. However, since the body is strong and emotions are fervent and overflowing during youth, and from a spiritual perspective, a young heart is ready to accept the light of truth and is a center for love, generosity, and forgiveness, this word (fata and futuwwah) is often used to signify the collection of these noble qualities, even in those of advanced age—just as we understand these concepts from the word "javanmardi" (chivalry/magnanimity) and "futuwwah" in Persian today.

From the hints in the Quranic verses and detailed historical accounts, it is understood that the Companions of the Cave lived in an environment and era surrounded by idolatry and disbelief. A tyrannical and oppressive government, which typically serves as the guardian and protector of polytheism, disbelief, ignorance, plunder, and crime, had cast its dark shadow over them.

However, this group of chivalrous men, who possessed sufficient intelligence and sincerity, eventually realized the corruption of this creed and decided to rise up, or if unable, to migrate from that polluted environment.

Therefore, following the previous discussion, the Quran says: "And We strengthened their hearts when they stood up and said, 'Our Lord is the Lord of the heavens and the earth'" (وَ رَبَطْنا عَلى‌ قُلُوبِهِمْ إِذْ قامُوا فَقالُوا رَبُّنا رَبُّ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ). "Never will we invoke besides Him any deity" (لَنْ نَدْعُوَا مِنْ دُونِهِ إِلهاً).

If we were to say such a thing and consider anyone other than Him a deity, "then we would certainly have spoken an enormity [far from the truth]" (لَقَدْ قُلْنا إِذاً شَطَطاً).

From the phrase "We strengthened their hearts" (رَبَطْنا عَلى‌ قُلُوبِهِمْ), it is inferred that the thought of Monotheism (Tawhid) first emerged in their hearts, but they lacked the strength to express it. However, Allah fortified their hearts and gave them the power and courage to stand up and openly proclaim the call of Monotheism.

Whether they first made this declaration before the tyrannical king of the time, "Decius" (Deqyanus), or among the masses, or both, or even among themselves, is not entirely clear. However, the apparent meaning of the expression "they stood up" (قاموا) is that they spoke these words among the people or in the presence of the oppressive ruler.

"Shatat" (شطط) means exceeding the limit and extreme distance; hence, words that are very far from the truth are called shatat. And if the banks of large rivers are called "shatt" (شط), it is because they are at a great distance from the water and their walls are high.

In reality, these believing chivalrous men employed a clear logical proof to establish Monotheism and negate false deities (alihah): we clearly see that these heavens and the earth have a Lord, and the existence of the system of creation is proof of His existence; we too are part of this collective existence, therefore, our Lord is the same Lord of the heavens and the earth.

Then they resorted to another proof: "These, our people, have taken besides Him deities" (هؤُلاءِ قَوْمُنَا اتَّخَذُوا مِنْ دُونِهِ آلِهَةً). After all, is belief possible without proof and evidence? "Why do they not bring for them a clear authority?" (لَوْ لا يَأْتُونَ عَلَيْهِمْ بِسُلْطانٍ بَيِّنٍ).

Can mere fancy and imagination, or blind imitation, be a proof for such a belief? What a manifest injustice and great deviation this is!

"Who is more unjust than one who invents a lie about Allah?" (فَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنِ افْتَرى‌ عَلَى اللَّهِ كَذِباً).

This "invention" (iftira) is both an injustice against oneself—because man has entrusted his destiny to the factors of misery and downfall—and an injustice against the society in which he spreads this tune and leads toward deviation, as well as an injustice toward the sanctity of the Lord and an insult to His Great Station.

These monotheistic chivalrous men strove as much as they could to remove the rust of polytheism from hearts and to plant the seedling of Monotheism. However, the clamor of idols and idol-worship in that environment was so loud, and the suffocation caused by the oppression and tyranny of the despotic king had so trapped the breaths of the men of God in their chests, that their monotheistic melodies were lost in their throats.

Inevitably, to save themselves and find a more receptive environment, they decided to "migrate." Thus, they consulted among themselves as to where they should go and in which direction they should move, saying to one another:

"And when you have withdrawn from them and that which they worship other than Allah, then retreat to the cave" (وَ إِذِ اعْتَزَلْتُمُوهُمْ وَ ما يَعْبُدُونَ إِلَّا اللَّهَ فَأْوُوا إِلَى الْكَهْفِ). "So that your Lord may spread out for you of His mercy and prepare for you from your affair a facility [comfort and ease]" (يَنْشُرْ لَكُمْ رَبُّكُمْ مِنْ رَحْمَتِهِ‌ و يهيئ‌لَكُمْ مِنْ أَمْرِكُمْ مِرْفَقاً).

"Yuhayyi'" (يهيئ) is from the root tahiyah, meaning to prepare or make ready. And "mirfaq" (مرفق) means something that is a means of grace, comfort, and gentleness (rifq). Therefore, the entire phrase "prepare for you from your affair a facility" means that Allah will provide the means for your grace and comfort.

It is not unlikely that the "spreading of mercy" mentioned in the first clause refers to Allah's spiritual favors, while the second clause refers to physical aspects, salvation, and material peace.

Key Points

1- Chivalry and Faith The spirit of Monotheism is always accompanied by a series of high human qualities; it both originates from them and has a reciprocal effect on them. For this reason, we read in the story of the Companions of the Cave that they were chivalrous men who believed in their Lord.

For this same reason, some scholars have said: "The head of chivalry (futuwwah) is faith." Others have said: "Chivalry is the bestowing of generosity, the withholding of harm, and the abandonment of complaining [about hardships]." Others have interpreted futuwwah as: "It is the avoidance of the forbidden and the practice of noble virtues."

2- Faith and Divine Assistance In several instances in the above verses, this reality is well-reflected: if a person takes the first steps in the way of "Allah" and stands up for Him, Divine help and assistance will rush to him. In one place it says: "They were youths who believed, and We increased them in guidance." In another place it says: "We strengthened their hearts" and gave them power and ability. And at the end of the verses, we read that they were waiting for the spreading of Divine mercy and finding a way of salvation.

Other verses of the Quran also clearly confirm this reality. Is it not the case that if a person strives for God, God will guide him to the path of truth? "And those who strive for Us—We will surely guide them to Our ways" (Surah Al-Ankabut, last verse). And also in Surah Muhammad, verse 17, we read: "And those who are guided—He increases them in guidance."

We know that the path of truth is a path with many obstacles and numerous difficulties; if the grace of God does not encompass a person, traversing it until reaching the destination is a difficult task. We also know that Allah's grace is too great to leave His truth-seeking and truth-demanding servant all alone on this path.

3- A Sanctuary Named "The Cave" The definite article "Al" in the word "Al-Kahf" (The Cave) perhaps indicates that they had a specific cave in a remote location in mind, so that if their monotheistic propagation did not bear fruit, they could take refuge in that cave to save themselves from that polluted and dark environment.

"Kahf" (Cave) is a word full of meaning that brings to mind a return to the most primitive type of human life—an environment where there is no light or brightness, and whose dark and cold nights remind one of the agonizing pains of the deprived. There is no sign of the glitter of the material world in it, nor of soft beds and a comfortable life!

Especially considering what is narrated in histories—that the Companions of the Cave were high-ranking ministers and officials of the king who rose up against him and his creed—it becomes clear how much courage, sacrifice, ambition, and spiritual vastness it takes to leave that life of luxury and preference cave-dwelling over it!

Yet, in that dark, cold, silent, and potentially dangerous cave (in terms of attacks by vermin), there was a world of light, purity, Monotheism, and spirituality. The lines of Divine mercy were etched upon the walls of such a cave, and the effects of the Lord's grace surged in its atmosphere. There was no news of ridiculous, colorful idols there, and the reach of the storm of the tyrants' oppression did not extend to it. It liberated man from that limited and suffocating atmosphere of the environment of ignorance and crime and immersed him in the vastness of free thought.

Yes, these monotheistic chivalrous men left that polluted world, which despite all its vastness was an agonizing prison for the soul, and turned toward a dry and dark cave whose dimensions seemed like an infinite space.

Just like the chaste Joseph (peace be upon him), who, when they pressured him saying that if he did not submit to the rebellious desires of the beautiful wife of the Aziz of Egypt, a dark and terrifying prison awaited him, increased his steadfastness and finally presented this amazing sentence to the presence of Allah: "My Lord, prison is more dear to me than that to which they invite me. And if You do not avert from me their plan, I might incline toward them" (Yusuf: 33).

Ayahs

۞ وَتَرَى ٱلشَّمْسَ إِذَا طَلَعَت تَّزَٰوَرُ عَن كَهْفِهِمْ ذَاتَ ٱلْيَمِينِ وَإِذَا غَرَبَت تَّقْرِضُهُمْ ذَاتَ ٱلشِّمَالِ وَهُمْ فِى فَجْوَةٍ مِّنْهُ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ ۗ مَن يَهْدِ ٱللَّهُ فَهُوَ ٱلْمُهْتَدِ ۖ وَمَن يُضْلِلْ فَلَن تَجِدَ لَهُۥ وَلِيًّا مُّرْشِدًا (١٧)
You may see the sun, when it rises, slanting toward the right of their cave, and, when it sets, cut across them towards the left, and they are in a cavern within it. That is one of Allah’s signs. Whomever Allah guides is rightly guided, and whomever He leads astray, you will never find for him any guardian or guide. (17)
وَتَحْسَبُهُمْ أَيْقَاظًا وَهُمْ رُقُودٌ ۚ وَنُقَلِّبُهُمْ ذَاتَ ٱلْيَمِينِ وَذَاتَ ٱلشِّمَالِ ۖ وَكَلْبُهُم بَـٰسِطٌ ذِرَاعَيْهِ بِٱلْوَصِيدِ ۚ لَوِ ٱطَّلَعْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ لَوَلَّيْتَ مِنْهُمْ فِرَارًا وَلَمُلِئْتَ مِنْهُمْ رُعْبًا (١٨)
You will suppose them to be awake, although they are asleep. We turn them to the right and to the left, and their dog [lies] stretching its forelegs at the threshold. If you come upon them, you will surely turn to flee from them, and you will surely be filled with a terror of them. (18)

Commentary

The Precise Location of the People of the Cave

In the two aforementioned verses, the Quran addresses the subtle details regarding the extraordinary life of the People of the Cave (Ashab al-Kahf) within that cave. It reveals these details with such precision and delicacy that it is as if a person is sitting before the cave, watching the sleepers with their own eyes.

In these two verses, six characteristics are mentioned:

1- The mouth of the cave opened toward the north, and since it was certainly located in the Northern Hemisphere, sunlight did not shine directly into it. As the Quran says: "And you would have seen the sun, when it rose, inclining away from their cave to the right, and when it set, passing them by on the left" (وَ تَرَى الشَّمْسَ إِذا طَلَعَتْ تَّزاوَرُ عَنْ كَهْفِهِمْ ذاتَ الْيَمِينِ وَ إِذا غَرَبَتْ تَقْرِضُهُمْ ذاتَ الشِّمالِ).

In this way, direct sunlight—the persistence of which might cause decay and wear—did not strike their bodies, yet there was sufficient indirect light.

The expression "tazawaru," which means to incline or lean away, carries the nuance that the sun was, as it were, commissioned to pass by the right side of the cave. Similarly, the expression "taqridu," which means to cut or sever, also implies a sense of mission. Furthermore, "tazawaru," derived from the root "ziyarah" (visiting), is associated with a beginning, which is appropriate for sunrise, while "taqridu" signifies cutting off and ending, which is inherent in the concept of sunset.

The fact that the cave's opening faced north allowed mild and gentle breezes, which usually blow from the north, to easily enter the cave and breathe fresh life into all its corners.

2- They were located in a spacious area within the cave (وَ هُمْ فِي فَجْوَةٍ مِنْهُ).

This indicates that the mouth of the cave, which is typically narrow, was not their resting place; rather, they had chosen the central parts of the cave, which were hidden from the eyes of onlookers and shielded from direct sunlight.

At this point, the Quran interrupts the narrative to provide a spiritual conclusion, as the mention of all these stories is for this very purpose.

It says: "That was from the signs of Allah. He whom Allah guides is the [rightly] guided, but he whom He leaves to stray—never will you find for him a protecting guide" (ذلِكَ مِنْ آياتِ اللَّهِ مَنْ يَهْدِ اللَّهُ فَهُوَ الْمُهْتَدِ وَ مَنْ يُضْلِلْ فَلَنْ تَجِدَ لَهُ وَلِيًّا مُرْشِداً).

Indeed, those who step onto the path of Allah and rise to strive for His sake are encompassed by His grace at every step—not only in the essence of their work but even in the smallest details.

3- Their sleep was not an ordinary or common sleep: "And you would have thought them awake, while they were asleep" (وَ تَحْسَبُهُمْ أَيْقاظاً وَ هُمْ رُقُودٌ).

This indicates that their eyes were fully open, just like an awake person. This exceptional state might have been so that harmful animals would not approach them, as they fear an awake human, or perhaps to create a terrifying sight so that no human would dare approach them, serving as a protective shield for them.

4- So that their limbs would not decay over the long years of this extended sleep: "And We turned them to the right and to the left" (وَ نُقَلِّبُهُمْ ذاتَ الْيَمِينِ وَ ذاتَ الشِّمالِ).

This was so that the blood in their bodies would not concentrate in one place, and so that pressure and weight over a long period would not have a harmful effect on the muscles resting against the ground.

5- Meanwhile, "their dog stretched its forelegs at the entrance" (وَ كَلْبُهُمْ باسِطٌ ذِراعَيْهِ بِالْوَصِيدِ).

"Wasid," as Raghib says in the book Al-Mufradat, originally means a room or storehouse built in the mountains to store property, and here it means the entrance of the cave.

Although the Quranic verses had not mentioned the dog of the People of the Cave until now, the Quran—especially when narrating stories—sometimes uses expressions that clarify other matters. For instance, describing the state of the dog here shows that they had a dog with them that walked alongside them and was, as it were, their guardian and watchman.

As for where this dog joined them—whether it was their hunting dog or a shepherd's dog they encountered on the way—when the shepherd recognized them, he sent the animals back toward the village and, being a seeker of truth and the "Beloved," joined these pure-hearted ones. The dog also did not let go of their trail and continued on its way.

Does this not imply that on the path to reaching the Truth, all lovers of this path can tread, and the doors to the Friend's quarter are closed to no one—from the repentant ministers of a tyrannical king to a shepherd, and even his dog?! Is it not that the Quran says all particles of existence in the earth and heavens, and all trees and living creatures, celebrate the praise of Allah, carrying His love in their heads and His affection in their hearts (Surah Al-Isra, Verse 44)?

6- "Their sight was so terrifying that had you looked at them, you would have turned from them in flight and been filled with terror of them" (لَوِ اطَّلَعْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ لَوَلَّيْتَ مِنْهُمْ فِراراً وَ لَمُلِئْتَ مِنْهُمْ رُعْباً).

This is not the first or last time that Allah creates a protective shield around His faithful servants through awe and fear. In the verses of Surah Al-Imran, Verse 151, we encounter a scene of this same phenomenon where Allah says: "We will cast terror into the hearts of those who disbelieve" (سَنُلْقِي فِي قُلُوبِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا الرُّعْبَ). [^1] In the Dua al-Nudba, we also read regarding the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله: "Then You aided him with terror" (ثم نصرته بالرعب):

"O Allah, You aided Your Prophet by means of the terror and awe You cast into the hearts of his enemies."

However, as to whether this awe and terror that filled the observer upon seeing the People of the Cave was due to their physical appearance or a mysterious spiritual force at work, the Quranic verses do not speak of it. Although commentators have various discussions, since there is no definitive proof for them, we refrain from mentioning them.

Furthermore, the phrase "and you would have been filled with terror of them" (وَ لَمُلِئْتَ مِنْهُمْ رُعْباً) is actually the reason for the phrase "you would have turned from them in flight" (لَوَلَّيْتَ مِنْهُمْ فِراراً). That is, you would have fled because fear and terror would have surrounded your heart; rather, it is as if it emerged from the heart—its primary center—and penetrated every particle of your being, filling everything with horror. In any case, when Allah's will is attached to something, He brings about the most significant result through the simplest means.

[^1]: For further explanation, refer to Tafsir Nemouneh, Volume 3, page 122 onwards, and also Volume 7, page 106 onwards.

Ayahs

وَكَذَٰلِكَ بَعَثْنَـٰهُمْ لِيَتَسَآءَلُوا۟ بَيْنَهُمْ ۚ قَالَ قَآئِلٌ مِّنْهُمْ كَمْ لَبِثْتُمْ ۖ قَالُوا۟ لَبِثْنَا يَوْمًا أَوْ بَعْضَ يَوْمٍ ۚ قَالُوا۟ رَبُّكُمْ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا لَبِثْتُمْ فَٱبْعَثُوٓا۟ أَحَدَكُم بِوَرِقِكُمْ هَـٰذِهِۦٓ إِلَى ٱلْمَدِينَةِ فَلْيَنظُرْ أَيُّهَآ أَزْكَىٰ طَعَامًا فَلْيَأْتِكُم بِرِزْقٍ مِّنْهُ وَلْيَتَلَطَّفْ وَلَا يُشْعِرَنَّ بِكُمْ أَحَدًا (١٩)
So it was that We aroused them [from sleep] so that they might question one another. One of them said, ‘How long have you stayed [here]?’ They said, ‘We have stayed a day, or part of a day.’ They said, ‘Your Lord knows best how long you have stayed. Send one of you to the city with this money. Let him observe which of them has the purest food, and bring you provisions from there. Let him be attentive, and let him not make anyone aware of you. (19)
إِنَّهُمْ إِن يَظْهَرُوا۟ عَلَيْكُمْ يَرْجُمُوكُمْ أَوْ يُعِيدُوكُمْ فِى مِلَّتِهِمْ وَلَن تُفْلِحُوٓا۟ إِذًا أَبَدًا (٢٠)
Indeed should they prevail over you, they will [either] stone you [to death], or force you back into their creed, and then you will never be saved. ’ (20)

Commentary

Awakening After a Long Sleep

By Allah’s will, we shall read in the upcoming verses that the sleep of the Companions of the Cave lasted so long that it reached 309 years. Thus, it was a sleep resembling death, and their awakening was akin to the Resurrection. Therefore, in the verses under discussion, the Quran says: "And similarly, We awakened them" (وَ كَذلِكَ بَعَثْناهُمْ).

That is to say, just as We were capable of plunging them into such a long sleep, We were capable of returning them to wakefulness.

We awakened them from sleep: "that they might question one another. A speaker from among them said, 'How long have you remained [here]?'" (لِيَتَساءَلُوا بَيْنَهُمْ قالَ قائِلٌ مِنْهُمْ كَمْ لَبِثْتُمْ) [^1]. "They said, 'We have remained a day or part of a day'" (قالُوا لَبِثْنا يَوْماً أَوْ بَعْضَ يَوْمٍ).

This hesitation was perhaps because, as a group of commentators have stated, they entered the cave and fell asleep at the beginning of a day and woke up at the end of a day; this caused them to first think they had slept for a full day, but upon seeing the position of the sun, they suggested "part of a day."

However, ultimately, since they could not know exactly how long their sleep had lasted:

"They said, 'Your Lord is most knowing of how long you remained'" (قالُوا رَبُّكُمْ أَعْلَمُ بِما لَبِثْتُمْ).

[^1]: The "Lam" in "Liyatasā'alū" is the so-called "Lam of Consequence" (Lam al-Aqibah), not "Lam of Cause"; meaning the result of their awakening was that they questioned one another about the duration of their sleep.

A group has said that the speaker of these words was the eldest among them, named "Tamlikha," and the use of the plural form "Qālū" (they said) is common in such contexts.

And perhaps this statement was made because they fell into doubt upon seeing the state of their faces, hair, and nails, as well as the style of their clothes, fearing that this might be an extraordinary sleep.

In any case, they felt severely hungry and in need of food because their body's reserves had been depleted. Therefore, their first proposal was: "So send one of you with this silver coin of yours to the city and let him look to which is the purest of food and bring you provision from it" (فَابْعَثُوا أَحَدَكُمْ بِوَرِقِكُمْ هذِهِ إِلَى الْمَدِينَةِ فَلْيَنْظُرْ أَيُّها أَزْكى‌ طَعاماً فَلْيَأْتِكُمْ بِرِزْقٍ مِنْهُ).

"But he must exercise the utmost caution and let no one be aware of you" (وَ لْيَتَلَطَّفْ وَ لا يُشْعِرَنَّ بِكُمْ أَحَداً).

"For indeed, if they come to know of you and catch you, they will stone you or return you to their religion (the religion of idolatry)" (إِنَّهُمْ إِنْ يَظْهەرُوا عَلَيْكُمْ يَرْجُمُوكُمْ أَوْ يُعِيدُوكُمْ فِي مِلَّتِهِمْ). "And in that case, you will never attain success or salvation" (وَ لَنْ تُفْلِحُوا إِذاً أَبَداً).

Key Points

1- The Purest Food

It is interesting that we read in this story that the Companions of the Cave, after awakening—despite presumably being very hungry and having exhausted their bodily reserves during this long period—still advised the one commissioned to buy food not to buy just any food. Rather, he should look among the sellers to see whose food is the purest and select that.

Some commentators have said this refers to slaughtered animals, as they knew there were people in that city who sold contaminated meat or perhaps carrion, or some whose business was fundamentally tainted with the forbidden (Haram). They advised avoiding the purchase of food from such individuals.

However, it appears this phrase has a broad meaning encompassing every kind of outward and inward purity. This is an advice to all wayfarers of the path of Truth: that they should not only think of spiritual food but also be mindful of the purity of their physical food—pure from any contamination. Even in the most critical moments of life, they should not forget this principle.

Today, many people in the world have realized the importance of this instruction in one aspect, striving for their food to be free from any outward contamination. They keep food in covered containers, away from dirty hands and dust. This is certainly a very good practice, but one should not be content with this alone; rather, food must also be pure from contamination by the forbidden, usury, fraud, deception, and any kind of inward impurity.

In Islamic narrations, great emphasis is placed on Halal (lawful) food and its effect on the acceptance of supplication (Dua) and the clarity of the heart.

In a narration, we read that someone came to the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) and said: "I love for my prayer to be answered."

The Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) said: "Purify your food and do not let the forbidden enter your stomach" [^1].

2- Constructive Dissimulation (Taqiyyah)

From the expressions in the above verses, it is clearly understood that the Companions of the Cave were insistent that no one in that environment should learn of their location, lest they be forced to accept the religion of idolatry or be killed in the worst manner—that is, by stoning. They wanted to remain unknown so that they could preserve their strength for future struggles, or at least for the preservation of their own faith.

This is one of the types of "constructive Taqiyyah," for the reality of Taqiyyah is that a person prevents the wasting of resources and preserves their existence by concealing themselves or their belief, so that at the necessary time, they can continue their effective struggles.

Naturally, where the concealment of belief causes the failure of the goal and programs, Taqiyyah is forbidden; everything must be made manifest, "no matter what the cost" (wa law balagha ma balagh).

3- The Center of the Quran is "Grace" (Lutf)

The phrase "wa-lyatalattaf" (and let him be cautious/kind), which according to the famous view is the exact midpoint of the Holy Quran in terms of word count, possesses a special grace and a very subtle meaning. It is derived from the root "Lutf" and "Latafat," which here means to exercise precision and subtlety; meaning the one commissioned to get food should go and return in such a way that no one becomes aware of their situation.

[^1]: Wasa'il, Vol. 4, Chapters on Supplication, Chapter 67, Hadith 4. For further explanation, refer to Volume 1 of the Commentary on Surah Al-Baqarah under verse 186.

Some commentators have said it refers to grace in buying food, such that he should not be harsh in the transaction, nor start a dispute or commotion, and should choose the best quality.

And it is indeed a grace that the middle phrase of the Quran consists of "Grace" and "Acting with Grace" (Lutf and Talattuf) [^1].

[^1]: Now that, by the grace of the Great Lord, after ten full years we reach the midpoint of the commentary of the Quran, we thank Allah that despite all the difficult and painful events and successive storms that passed over us and our country, the light of Islam was not extinguished in this region; rather, its scope expanded. We also thank Allah that no interruption occurred in the work of writing it, but we hope that the remainder of the commentary proceeds with greater speed (Insha-Allah). It is true that ten years is not a short time, but the work done on this commentary so far has also, praise be to Allah, not been a small task.

Ayahs

وَكَذَٰلِكَ أَعْثَرْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ لِيَعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقٌّ وَأَنَّ ٱلسَّاعَةَ لَا رَيْبَ فِيهَآ إِذْ يَتَنَـٰزَعُونَ بَيْنَهُمْ أَمْرَهُمْ ۖ فَقَالُوا۟ ٱبْنُوا۟ عَلَيْهِم بُنْيَـٰنًا ۖ رَّبُّهُمْ أَعْلَمُ بِهِمْ ۚ قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ غَلَبُوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَمْرِهِمْ لَنَتَّخِذَنَّ عَلَيْهِم مَّسْجِدًا (٢١)
So it was that We let them come upon them, that they might know that Allah’s promise is true, and that there is no doubt in the Hour. As they disputed among themselves about their matter, they said, ‘Build a building over them. Their Lord knows them best.’ Those who had the say in their matter said, ‘We will set up a place of worship over them.’ (21)
سَيَقُولُونَ ثَلَـٰثَةٌ رَّابِعُهُمْ كَلْبُهُمْ وَيَقُولُونَ خَمْسَةٌ سَادِسُهُمْ كَلْبُهُمْ رَجْمًۢا بِٱلْغَيْبِ ۖ وَيَقُولُونَ سَبْعَةٌ وَثَامِنُهُمْ كَلْبُهُمْ ۚ قُل رَّبِّىٓ أَعْلَمُ بِعِدَّتِهِم مَّا يَعْلَمُهُمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلٌ ۗ فَلَا تُمَارِ فِيهِمْ إِلَّا مِرَآءً ظَـٰهِرًا وَلَا تَسْتَفْتِ فِيهِم مِّنْهُمْ أَحَدًا (٢٢)
They will say, ‘[They are] three; their dog is the fourth of them,’ and say, ‘[They are] five, their dog is the sixth of them,’ taking a shot at the invisible. They will say, ‘[They are] seven, their dog is the eighth of them.’ Say, ‘My Lord knows best their number, and none knows them except a few.’ So do not dispute concerning them, except for a seeming dispute, and do not question about them any of them. (22)
وَلَا تَقُولَنَّ لِشَا۟ىْءٍ إِنِّى فَاعِلٌ ذَٰلِكَ غَدًا (٢٣)
Do not say about anything, ‘I will indeed do it tomorrow,’ (23)
إِلَّآ أَن يَشَآءَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَٱذْكُر رَّبَّكَ إِذَا نَسِيتَ وَقُلْ عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَهْدِيَنِ رَبِّى لِأَقْرَبَ مِنْ هَـٰذَا رَشَدًا (٢٤)
without [adding], ‘God willing.’ And when you forget, remember your Lord, and say, ‘Maybe my Lord will guide me to [something] more akin to rectitude than this.’ (24)

Commentary

The End of the Story of the Companions of the Cave

Soon, the story of the migration of this group of distinguished men in that environment spread everywhere, and the tyrannical king became severely enraged. He feared that their migration or escape might become a prelude to the awakening and awareness of the people, or that they might travel to regions far and near to preach the doctrine of Monotheism and struggle against polytheism and idol-worship.

Therefore, he ordered special agents to search for them everywhere, and if they found any trace, to pursue them until they were captured and brought to punishment.

However, the more they searched, the less they found, and this itself became a riddle for the people of that environment and a turning point in their way of thinking. Perhaps the very fact that a group of the highest state officials turned their backs on all material positions and accepted various dangers became a source of awakening and awareness for a group of people.

In any case, the mysterious story of this group was recorded in their history and passed down from one generation to the next, and hundreds of years passed in this manner...

Now let us return to the agent sent to buy food and see what happened to him. He entered the city, but his mouth hung open in astonishment. The shape of the buildings had completely transformed, the faces were all unrecognizable, the clothes had taken on a new style, and even the manner of speaking and the customs of the people had changed. The ruins of yesterday had turned into palaces, and the palaces of yesterday into ruins! Perhaps for a brief moment, he thought he was still asleep and what he was seeing was a dream; he rubbed his eyes, but he realized that what he saw was the very essence of a strange and unbelievable reality.

He still thought their sleep in the cave had been for a day or half a day, so why all this transformation? How could all these changes be possible in a single day?

On the other hand, his appearance was also strange and unfamiliar to the people; his clothes, his manner of speaking, and his countenance were all new to them, and perhaps this situation drew the attention of some people toward him, and they began to follow him.

His astonishment reached its peak when he reached into his pocket to pay the price of the food he had bought. The seller’s eyes fell upon a coin that belonged to 300 years ago or more, perhaps bearing the name of "Decius," the tyrannical king of that time. When he asked for an explanation, he replied, "I have recently acquired this coin!"

Gradually, from the circumstances, it became certain to the people that this man was one of the group whose names they had read in the history of 300 years ago, and whose mysterious story had been discussed in many circles.

And he himself also realized into what a deep and long sleep he and his companions had fallen.

This matter echoed through the city like a bomb and spread from tongue to tongue everywhere.

Some historians write that in those days, a righteous and monotheistic ruler governed them, but digesting the issue of physical resurrection and the reviving of the dead after death was difficult for the people of that environment. A group of them could not believe that a human returns to life after dying. However, the event of the sleep of the Companions of the Cave became a crushing proof for those who supported physical resurrection.

Therefore, the Quran says in the first verse:

"And similarly, We caused them to be discovered [by the people] so that they might know that the promise of Allah is true" (وَ كَذلِكَ أَعْثَرْنا عَلَيْهِمْ لِيَعْلَمُوا أَنَّ وَعْدَ اللَّهِ حَقٌّ).

"And that there is no doubt about the Hour [of Resurrection]" (وَ أَنَّ السَّاعَةَ لا رَيْبَ فِيها).

For this long sleep, which lasted for hundreds of years, was not unlike death, and their awakening was like the Resurrection; rather, it can be said that this sleep and awakening were, in some respects, more wondrous than dying and returning to life. For hundreds of years passed over them while their bodies did not decay, and while they neither ate food nor drank water. How did they remain alive during this long period?

Is this not proof of the power of Allah over everything and every action? Life after death, in view of such a scene, is certainly possible.

Some historians have written that the agent sent to buy food quickly returned to the cave and informed his friends of the event. They all fell into deep wonder, and since they felt they had lost all their children, brothers, and friends, and none of their former companions remained alive, enduring this life was hard and unpleasant for them. They asked Allah to close their eyes to this world and transfer them to the proximity of His mercy, and so it happened.

They passed away, and their bodies remained in the cave when the people came looking for them.

"At this point, a dispute and conflict broke out between the supporters of the issue of physical resurrection and their opponents.

The opponents tried to ensure that the matter of the sleep and awakening of the Companions of the Cave would soon be forgotten, to take this crushing proof away from the supporters. Therefore, they suggested that the cave be sealed so that they would remain hidden from the people's sight forever" (إِذْ يَتَنازَعُونَ بَيْنَهُمْ أَمْرَهُمْ فَقالُوا ابْنُوا عَلَيْهِمْ بُنْياناً).

And to silence the people, they said: "Do not speak much of them; they had a mysterious fate, and 'their Lord knows best about them'" (رَبُّهُمْ أَعْلَمُ بِهِمْ).

Therefore, leave their story and leave them to themselves.

While "the true believers who had become aware of this matter and considered it a living document to prove the Resurrection in its true sense, tried to ensure this story would never be forgotten. Thus, they said: 'We will surely build a masjid (place of worship) over them'" so that people would never let their memory fade, and furthermore, they could seek assistance from their pure souls (قالَ الَّذِينَ غَلَبُوا عَلى‌ أَمْرِهِمْ لَنَتَّخِذَنَّ عَلَيْهِمْ مَسْجِداً).

In the commentary of the above verse, several other possibilities have been suggested, some of which we will mention when discussing the Key Points.

The next verse refers to some of the differences that exist among the people regarding the Companions of the Cave, including their number: "A group of people will say they were three, the fourth of them being their dog" (سَيَقُولُونَ ثَلاثَةٌ رابِعُهُمْ كَلْبُهُمْ).

"And a group says they were five, the sixth of them being their dog" (وَ يَقُولُونَ خَمْسَةٌ سادِسُهُمْ كَلْبُهُمْ).

"All these are words without proof, a shot in the dark" (رجماً بالغيب).

"And a group says they were seven, and the eighth of them was their dog" (وَ يَقُولُونَ سَبْعَةٌ وَ ثامِنُهُمْ كَلْبُهُمْ).

"Say: My Lord is most knowing of their number" (قُلْ رَبِّي أَعْلَمُ بِعِدَّتِهِمْ).

"None knows them except a few" (ما يَعْلَمُهُمْ إِلَّا قَلِيلٌ).

Although in the above sentences the Quran has not explicitly stated their number, from the indications in the verse, it can be understood that the third statement is the correct one and corresponds to reality. This is because following the first and second statements, the phrase "رجماً بالغيب" (a shot in the dark)—which points to their groundlessness—is mentioned, but regarding the third statement, not only is such an expression absent, but the expressions "Say: My Lord is most knowing of their number" and "None knows them except a few" are mentioned, which is itself a reason for confirming this statement. In any case, at the end of the verse, it adds: "So do not argue about them except with an argument that is clear and based on logic" (فَلا تُمارِ فِيهِمْ إِلَّا مِراءً ظاهِراً).

"Mira'" (argument/dispute)—as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat—is originally derived from "Maraytu al-naqah," meaning "I took the camel's udder for milking," and was then applied to discussion and debate surrounding something that is subject to doubt and uncertainty.

It is often used in stubborn arguments and the defense of falsehood, but its original root is not limited to this meaning; rather, it includes any type of discussion and debate about any matter that is a place of doubt.

"Zahir" (clear/manifest) means dominant, prevailing, and victorious.

Therefore, the phrase (فَلا تُمارِ فِيهِمْ إِلَّا مِراءً ظاهِراً) means: speak with them so logically and with such evidence that the superiority of your logic becomes manifest.

Some have also suggested in the commentary of the verse that you should not discuss or debate "privately" with stubborn opponents, because whatever you say, they will distort it; rather, speak openly and in the presence of the people so they cannot distort or deny the truth.

However, the first commentary seems more correct. In any case, the meaning of the words is that you must speak with them relying on Divine revelation, for the firmest proof in this regard is this very proof, "and therefore, do not consult any of them [who speak without proof] about the number of the Companions of the Cave" (وَ لا تَسْتَفْتِ فِيهِمْ مِنْهُمْ أَحَداً).

The next verse gives a general instruction to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "And never say of anything, 'I will surely do that tomorrow'" (وَ لا تَقُولَنَّ لِشَيْ‌ءٍ إِنِّي فاعِلٌ ذلِكَ غَداً).

"Except [with the addition], 'If Allah wills'" (إِلَّا أَنْ يَشاءَ اللَّهُ). Meaning, in relation to news of the future and the decision to perform tasks, definitely add the phrase "Insha'Allah."

Firstly, because you are never independent in decision-making, and if Allah does not will it, no one has the power to do anything. Therefore, to prove that your power is from His eternal power and your strength is dependent on His strength, definitely add the phrase "Insha'Allah" (If Allah wills) to your speech.

Secondly: making a definitive announcement for a human whose power is limited and where the possibility of various obstacles arising exists is not correct or logical, and it may turn out to be a lie, unless it is accompanied by the phrase "Insha'Allah."

Some commentators have suggested another possibility in the interpretation of the above verse, which is that the purpose is to negate human independence in actions. The meaning of the verse would be: "You cannot say 'I will do such-and-such tomorrow' unless Allah wills it."

Of course, the implication of this statement is that if we add the phrase "Insha'Allah," the statement will be complete, but this is an implication of the sentence, not the text itself, as stated in the first commentary. [^1] The occasion of revelation that we narrated regarding the above verses supports the first commentary, because the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, without mentioning "Insha'Allah," promised an explanation and answer to those who had asked about the Companions of the Cave and the like. For this reason, Divine revelation was delayed for a while to warn the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in this regard and to serve as a model for all people.

Then, following this sentence, the Quran says: "And remember your Lord when you forget" (وَ اذْكُرْ رَبَّكَ إِذا نَسِيتَ).

This points to the fact that if, due to forgetfulness, you do not add the phrase "Insha'Allah" to statements reporting the future, whenever you remember, immediately compensate and say "Insha'Allah," for this will compensate for the past.

"And say: 'Perhaps my Lord will guide me to a way closer to the right path than this'" (وَ قُلْ عَسى‌ أَنْ يَهْدِيَنِ رَبِّي لِأَقْرَبَ مِنْ هذا رَشَداً).

Key Points

1- "Rajman bi-l-ghayb"

"Rajm" originally means stone or throwing a stone; it was then applied to any type of shooting, and sometimes it is used metaphorically to mean "accusing" or "judging by conjecture and suspicion." The word (bi-l-ghayb) is an emphasis on this meaning, i.e., making a baseless judgment about something in its absence.

This expression is similar to what we say in Persian, "shooting an arrow in the dark." Since shooting an arrow in the dark usually does not hit the target, such judgments usually do not turn out to be correct.

2- The "Waw" in the phrase "wa thamunuhum kalbuhum"

In the above verses, the phrases "rabi'uhum kalbuhum" and "sadisuhum kalbuhum" both appear without the "waw" (and), while the phrase "wa thamunuhum kalbuhum" begins with "waw." Since every expression in the Quran certainly contains a point of wisdom, commentators have spoken extensively about the meaning of this "waw."

Perhaps the best interpretation is that this "waw" refers to the final statement and the last word, just as in modern literature this expression has recently become common: when listing things, all members of that discussion are mentioned without "waw," but the last one must be with "waw." For example, we say "Zayd, Amr, Hasan, and Muhammad came." This "waw" points to the end of the speech and the statement of the final instance and subject.

This view is narrated from the famous commentator Ibn Abbas, and some other commentators have confirmed it, and meanwhile, he wanted to use this very word "waw" as a confirmation that the actual number of the Companions of the Cave was seven, because the Quran, after stating the baseless sayings of others, stated their true number at the end.

Other commentators, such as Fakhr al-Razi and Qurtubi, have narrated another interpretation for this "waw," the summary of which is as follows: The number seven is considered a complete number among the Arabs. For this reason, they bring up to seven without "waw," but as soon as they pass this number, they bring "waw," which is a sign of the beginning of speech and resumption (istinaf). Therefore, in the terminology of Arabic grammarians, it has become known as the "Waw of Eight" (Waw al-Thamaniyah).

In the verses of the Quran, we also frequently encounter this matter. For example, in Surah At-Tawbah, verse 112, when listing the attributes of the mujahideen in the way of Allah, it mentions seven attributes without "waw," but when it reaches the eighth attribute, it says: "وَ النَّاهُونَ عَنِ الْمُنْكَرِ وَ الْحافِظُونَ لِحُدُودِ اللَّهِ" (and those who forbid evil and those who observe the limits of Allah).

And in verse 5 of Surah At-Tahrim, in describing the wives of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, after mentioning seven attributes, it brings the eighth with "waw," saying "ثيبات و ابكارا" (previously married and virgins).

And in Surah Az-Zumar, in verse 71, when speaking of the gates of Hell, He says: "فتحت أبوابها" (its gates are opened), but in two verses later, when speaking of the gates of Paradise, He says: "و فتحت أبوابها" (and its gates are opened). Is this not because the gates of Hell are seven and the gates of Paradise are eight?

Of course, this may not be a universal rule, but in most cases, it is so, and in any case, it shows that even the presence of a single "waw" in the Quran is calculated and intended to express a reality.

3- A Masjid Beside the Tomb

The apparent meaning of the Quranic expression is that the Companions of the Cave eventually passed away and were buried, and the word "alayhim" (over them) is evidence for this claim. Then, those devoted to them decided to build a place of worship beside their tomb.
