The Quran mentions this subject in the above verses with a favorable tone, and this shows that building a place of worship to honor the graves of the greats of religion is not only not forbidden—as the Wahhabis imagine—but is a good and worthy deed.

In principle, commemorative buildings that keep alive the memory of prominent and distinguished individuals have always existed and continue to exist among the people of the world, and a type of appreciation for those of the past and encouragement for those of the future is inherent in that act. Islam has not only not forbidden this act but has permitted it.

The existence of such buildings is a historical document of the existence of these personalities and their program and history. For this reason, prophets and personalities whose graves have remained neglected have also had their history subjected to doubt and questioning.

It is also clear that such buildings have the least conflict with the issue of Monotheism (Tawhid) and the exclusivity of worship to "Allah," because "honor" is one matter, and "worship" and adoration is another matter.

Of course, this subject has much discussion which is not the place for it here.

4- Everything Relying on the Will of Allah

Bringing the phrase "Insha'Allah" when stating decisions related to the future is not only a form of etiquette in the presence of Allah but is also an expression of this important reality: that we have nothing of our own; whatever exists is from His side. Allah is the Independent by Essence, and we are all dependent on Him. Unless His will exists, even if all the blades in the world move from their place, they will not cut even one vein, and if His will exists, everything is quickly realized and He even "keeps the glass in the embrace of the stone."

This is, in fact, the very concept of "Monotheism in Actions" (Tawhid al-Af'ali), which, while acknowledging the existence of human choice and freedom of will, links the existence of everything and every action to the will of Allah.

This expression, by increasing our attention to Allah in our affairs, grants us strength and power, and also invites us to purity and correctness of action.

It is understood from some narrations that if someone says something in relation to the future without "Insha'Allah," Allah leaves him to himself and takes him out from under the umbrella of His support. [^1] In a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: The Imam had ordered a letter to be written. When the letter was finished and given to him, he noticed that "Insha'Allah" was not in it. He said:

"How did you hope that this would be completed while there is no exception (Insha'Allah) in it? Look at every place where there is no exception and put the exception in it."

5- Answer to a Question

In the above verses, we read that Allah says to His Prophet: "And remember your Lord when you forget." [^1] Pointing to the fact that if you did not rely on His will with the phrase "Insha'Allah," whenever you remember, compensate for it.

In several hadiths narrated from the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام in the commentary of the above verse, this issue is also emphasized: that even after a year has passed, if you remember that you did not say "Insha'Allah," compensate for the past. [^2] Now this question arises: is forgetfulness (nisyan) possible for the Prophet, while if forgetfulness found its way into his mind, people could not have full confidence in his words and actions? And this is the reason for the infallibility (ismah) of the prophets and Imams from error and forgetfulness, even in mundane subjects.

However, considering that in many verses of the Quran we have seen the speech addressed to the prophets while the intended audience is the masses of the people, the answer to this question becomes clear. It is according to the Arabic proverb: "I mean you, but listen, O neighbor," meaning "My words are addressed to you who are with me, but you, neighbor, listen." [^3] Some great commentators have given another answer to this question, which we have provided under verse 68 of Surah Al-An'am (Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 5, p. 289).

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 254. [^2]: Refer to Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 254 onwards. [^3]: Its equivalent in Persian is the famous proverb: "I am telling the door so the wall hears."

Ayahs

وَلَبِثُوا۟ فِى كَهْفِهِمْ ثَلَـٰثَ مِا۟ئَةٍ سِنِينَ وَٱزْدَادُوا۟ تِسْعًا (٢٥)
They remained in the Cave for three hundred years, and added nine more [to that number]. (25)
قُلِ ٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا لَبِثُوا۟ ۖ لَهُۥ غَيْبُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ أَبْصِرْ بِهِۦ وَأَسْمِعْ ۚ مَا لَهُم مِّن دُونِهِۦ مِن وَلِىٍّ وَلَا يُشْرِكُ فِى حُكْمِهِۦٓ أَحَدًا (٢٦)
Say, ‘Allah knows best how long they remained. To Him belongs the Unseen of the heavens and the earth. How well does He see! How well does He hear! They have no guardian besides Him, and none shares with Him in His judgement.’ (26)
وَٱتْلُ مَآ أُوحِىَ إِلَيْكَ مِن كِتَابِ رَبِّكَ ۖ لَا مُبَدِّلَ لِكَلِمَـٰتِهِۦ وَلَن تَجِدَ مِن دُونِهِۦ مُلْتَحَدًا (٢٧)
Recite what has been revealed to you from the Book of your Lord. Nothing can change His words, and you will never find any refuge besides Him. (27)

Commentary

The Sleep of the Companions of the Cave

From the indications present in the previous verses, it was gathered in brief that the sleep of the Companions of the Cave was an exceedingly long one. This subject piques the curiosity of every listener, who wishes to know exactly how many years they remained in this long sleep.

In the final verses of this story, which are the verses under discussion, the listener is brought out of doubt as He says:

"And they remained in their cave for three hundred years and exceeded by nine [more]"! (وَ لَبِثُوا فِي كَهْفِهِمْ ثَلاثَ مِائَةٍ سِنِينَ وَ ازْدَادُوا تِسْعاً) [^1] Therefore, the total duration of their stay and sleep in the cave was three hundred and nine years.

A group [of commentators] believes that this expression—stating "three hundred years and adding nine" instead of simply saying 309 years—is an allusion to the difference between solar and lunar years. This is because they stayed for three hundred years according to the solar calendar, which amounts to three hundred and nine years when calculated by the lunar calendar. This is among the subtleties of expression: using a minor phrasing detail to convey another reality that would otherwise require explanation. [^2]

Then, to put an end to the various discussions among the people regarding this matter, He says: "Say, 'Allah is most knowing of how long they remained'" (قُلِ اللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِما لَبِثُوا). For "to Him belongs the [knowledge of the] unseen of the heavens and the earth," and He is more aware of their state than anyone else (لَهُ غَيْبُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ).

How could One who is aware of the hidden and the manifest in the entire universe not be aware of the duration of the stay of the Companions of the Cave?

[^1]: According to grammatical rules, the singular sanah (year) should have been used here instead of the plural sinīn (years). However, it seems that because such a sleep was so long and this number of years was so vast, the plural form was mentioned to express the magnitude and abundance.

[^2]: The difference between solar and lunar years is approximately eleven days. If we multiply this by three hundred and then divide it by the number of days in a lunar year (354 days), the result is exactly nine (of course, a small fraction remains which is negligible as it is less than a year).

"How clearly He sees and how finely He hears!" (أَبْصِرْ بِهِ وَ أَسْمِعْ) [^1] For this reason, the inhabitants of the heavens and the earth "have no protector or guardian besides Him" (ما لَهُمْ مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ وَلِيٍ).

There is discussion among commentators regarding whom the pronoun in "for them" (ma lahum) refers to. A group believes it refers to the inhabitants of the earth and the heavens, while others consider it a reference to the "Companions of the Cave," meaning the Companions of the Cave had no protector or guardian other than Allah; it was He who was with them everywhere in this event and protected them.

However, considering the preceding sentence which speaks of the unseen of the heavens and the earth, the first interpretation seems more correct.

And at the end of the verse, He adds: "and He shares not His legislation with anyone" (وَ لا يُشْرِكُ فِي حُكْمِهِ أَحَداً).

In reality, this is an emphasis on the absolute Guardianship (Wilayah) of Allah—that no other person has guardianship over the worlds, nor is anyone a partner in His guardianship. That is, no other person has influence in the governance of the world, neither independently nor jointly.

In the final verse, the address is turned toward the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, saying: "And recite what has been revealed to you of the Book of your Lord" (وَ اتْلُ ما أُوحِيَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ كِتابِ رَبِّكَ). And pay no heed to the sayings of this person or that, which are mixed with lies, superstitions, and baseless matters; the foundation of your discussion in these affairs must only be Divine revelation.

[^1]: The phrase "أَبْصِرْ بِهِ وَ أَسْمِعْ" is a form of exclamation (sighat al-ta'ajjub), intended to express the greatness of Allah's knowledge. It means He is so seeing and hearing that it leaves man in wonder.

For nothing can change His words, and "there is no changer of His words (and His knowledge)" (لا مُبَدِّلَ لِكَلِماتِهِ).

His word and His knowledge are not like the knowledge and speech of servants, which undergo change and alteration every day due to new discoveries and awareness, and for which reason they cannot be 100% relied upon.

For these reasons, "you will never find besides Him any refuge" (وَ لَنْ تَجِدَ مِنْ دُونِهِ مُلْتَحَداً).

"Multahad" is derived from the root lahd (on the weight of mahd), meaning a hole that deviates from the center toward one of two sides (like the niche made for a grave). For this reason, multahad is used for a place toward which a person inclines, and subsequently, it has come to mean "refuge and sanctuary."

It is noteworthy that the last two verses express Allah's scientific encompassment of all beings in the universe through several ways:

  • First, it says: The unseen of the heavens and the earth belongs to Him, and for this reason, He is aware of them all.
  • Then it adds: How clearly He sees and how finely He hears!
  • Again it says: He alone is the Protector and Guardian, and He is the most knowing of all.
  • It also adds: No one shares in His judgment such that His knowledge would be limited.
  • Then it states: No change or alteration occurs in His knowledge and His word to diminish its value or stability.
  • And in the final phrase, it says: He is the only refuge in the universe, and naturally, He is aware of all His refugees.

Key Points

1- The Story of the Companions of the Cave in Islamic Hadiths

Numerous narrations regarding the Companions of the Cave are found in Islamic sources, some of which are not reliable in terms of their chains of transmission (asnad); for this reason, contradictions and differences exist among some of them.

Among the narrations, the one cited by Ali ibn Ibrahim al-Qummi in his Tafsir seems better in terms of text, content, and harmony with the Quranic verses. Its summary is as follows:

Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام said regarding the "Companions of the Cave and al-Raqim": They lived during the time of a tyrannical and stubborn king who called the people of his country to worship idols, and he would kill anyone who did not respond to his call. This group (the Companions of the Cave) was a community of believers who worshipped the Great Allah (but they kept their faith hidden from the apparatus of the tyrannical king).

The tyrannical king had stationed guards at the gates of the capital, and anyone who wished to leave was forced to prostrate to the idols placed there.

This believing group managed to leave the city—under the pretext of hunting—(and they intended never to return to their city, which was a very polluted environment).

On their path, they encountered a shepherd whom they invited to the One God, but he did not accept. However, strangely, the shepherd's dog followed them and never separated from them. Having fled from the religion of idol-worship, they reached a cave at the end of the day and decided to rest for a while. Allah caused sleep to overcome them, just as He says in the Quran: "We cast [a cover of sleep] over their ears... for a number of years."

They slept for so long that the tyrannical king died, and the people of the city also passed away one after another, and another time and another population took their place.

The Companions of the Cave woke up after this long sleep and asked one another about the duration of their sleep. They looked at the sun and saw it had risen, and said: We have slept for a day or part of a day!

Then they commissioned one of their own, saying: Take this silver coin and enter the city incognito, and obtain some food for us, but be careful that they do not recognize you; for if they become aware of our situation, they will either kill us or return us to their religion.

The man entered the city but observed the scene of the city to be contrary to what he remembered, and the population was different from the people he knew. In fact, he did not properly understand their language, just as they did not properly understand his. They said to him: Who are you and where do you come from?! He eventually unveiled his secrets. The king of that city (who was a God-worshipper at that time) moved toward the cave with his companions along with that man. When they reached the entrance of the cave, they looked inside; some said they are no more than three, the fourth being their dog; some said they are five, the sixth being their dog; and some said they are seven, the eighth being their dog.

At this moment, Allah placed them behind a veil of awe such that no one dared to enter the cave except for the individual who was one of them. When their companion entered the cave, he found them terrified, for they thought the crowd present at the cave's entrance were the companions of "Decius" (Daqyanus), the tyrannical idol-worshipping king. But he informed them of the story of their long sleep and told them that Allah had made them a sign for the people.

They became happy and shed tears of joy, asking God to return them to their previous state.

However, the king of that time said: It is fitting that we build a mosque here, for they were a group of believers.

Here, the Imam added that twice every year they would turn from side to side, and their dog was at the entrance of the cave with its forelegs stretched out (keeping watch). [^1]

In another hadith from Ali عليه السلام, we read a detailed account of the Companions of the Cave, the summary of which is: "They were initially six people whom Decius had chosen as his ministers, and every year he would hold a festival for them.

In one of those years, while it was the day of the festival, the great commanders of the army were on his right and special advisors on his left. One of the commanders informed him that the Persian army had entered the borders. He became so distressed upon hearing this tragic news that he trembled and the crown fell from his head. One of these ministers, named 'Tamlikha,' said in his heart: This man thinks he is God; if so, why did he become so grieved? Furthermore, he possesses all human attributes!"

[^1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, pp. 247-248.

His six ministers would gather every day at the house of one of them, and that day it was "Tamlikha's" turn.

He prepared good food for his friends, yet he appeared distressed (and did not reach for the food; his friends inquired about his state). He said: A matter has fallen into my heart that has deprived me of food, water, and sleep. They asked about the matter.

He said: I have reflected and meditated deeply upon this high-reaching heaven established without pillars, and the One who set the sun and moon in motion within it as two bright signs, and the One who adorned its surface with stars. Then I looked at this earth and said to myself: Who brought it out of the water and spread it out? And who calmed its agitation with mountains? Then I fell into thought about myself and said: Who sent me out from the state of an embryo to the outside of the mother's womb? Who granted me pleasant milk from the mother's breast and nourished me? And finally, who raised me?

From the sum of these matters, I understood that all of this has a Maker, Creator, and Disposer who is certainly other than "Decius"; He is the King of kings and the Ruler over the heavens.

When he uttered these words with clarity and sincerity, what had arisen from his heart settled in the hearts of his companions. Suddenly, they all fell at his feet, kissed them, and said: Allah has invited us from misguidance to guidance through you; now tell us, what shall we do?!

"Tamlikha" stood up, sold some dates from an orchard he owned for three thousand dirhams, took the money, and they mounted their horses and rode out of the city.

When they had traveled three miles, "Tamlikha" said to them: Brothers! Kingship and ministry have passed; the way of God cannot be traversed with these expensive horses. Dismount so that we may walk this path on foot; perhaps Allah will grant an opening in our closed affair.

They left the horses and set out on foot. They walked swiftly for seven leagues (farsakh) that day, but their feet became wounded and blood dripped from them!

A shepherd came to meet them. They said: O shepherd, do you have a draught of milk or water to host us? The shepherd said: I have what you desire, but I see your faces as the faces of kings! Why are you here? I think you have fled from King Decius.

They said: O shepherd! The truth is that we cannot lie, but if we tell the truth, will you not create trouble for us? Then they explained their story.

The shepherd threw himself at their hands and feet, kissed them, and said: Brothers! What has fallen into your hearts has also fallen into mine, but allow me to deliver the sheep to their owners and join you. They waited a while until he delivered the sheep and returned, while his dog was with him...

These youths looked at the dog; some said: There is fear that he might reveal our secret with his noise. But no matter how much they tried to drive him away, he refused, as if saying: Let me protect you from enemies (I too am a traveler on this path!...).

These seven people continued their way while the dog followed them until they climbed a mountain and arrived at a cave. At the entrance of the cave, they found springs and fruit trees; they ate from them and quenched their thirst. The darkness of night arrived.

They took refuge in the cave, and the dog stretched out its forelegs at the entrance and kept watch. At this moment, Allah commanded the Angel of Death to take their souls (and a deep sleep resembling death dominated them). [^1]

Regarding Decius, some commentators say: He was a Roman Emperor who ruled from 249 to 251 AD. He was a fierce enemy of Christians and would harass and torture them, before the Roman state accepted the religion of Jesus.

2- Where was the Cave?

There is much discussion among scholars and commentators regarding which region of the earth the Companions of the Cave lived in and where this cave was located.

Although finding the exact location of this event does not significantly impact the essence of the story, its educational points, or its historical importance—and this is not the only event whose core story we know but lack sufficient information on some details—knowing the location of this incident can certainly help in better understanding its characteristics.

In any case, among the possibilities and opinions existing in this field, two opinions seem more correct:

First, that this incident occurred in the city of "Ephesus" and this cave was located near it. The ruins of this city are currently visible near "Izmir" in "Turkey," and near the village of "Ayasuluk" on Mount "Yanairdagh," a cave is currently seen that is not far from "Ephesus."

[^1]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 2, p. 382 (under the root f-k-r).

This cave is a vast one in which it is said the remains of hundreds of graves are visible, and in the opinion of many, this is the cave of the Companions of the Cave.

As reported by informed sources, the mouth of this cave faces the northeast, and this has caused some great commentators to doubt its authenticity. However, this situation actually supports its authenticity, because the sun being on the right side of the cave at sunrise and on the left at sunset implies that the mouth of the cave faces north or slightly inclined toward the northeast.

The current absence of a mosque or temple next to it is not a reason to deny its authenticity either, as it is possible that after about 17 centuries, the remains of that temple have vanished.

The second cave is a cave located near the capital of "Jordan," the city of "Amman," near a village called "Rajib." At the top of this cave, the remains of a monastery are seen which, according to some indications, dates back to the 5th century AD, and after the Muslim conquest of that area, it was converted into a mosque and has a mihrab and minaret.

3- Educational Aspects of this Story

This strange historical event, which the Quran has presented free from any superstition or baseless and fabricated matters, is—like all stories of the Quran—full of constructive educational points mentioned throughout the commentary of the verses. However, we deem it necessary to mention them here as a summary to get closer to the primary goal of the Quran.

A: The first lesson of this story is breaking the barrier of imitation (taqlid) and separating oneself from conforming to a corrupt environment. As we saw, the chivalrous Companions of the Cave did not lose their intellectual independence in the face of the misguided majority of the environment, and this very matter became the cause of their salvation and success. Fundamentally, a human being should be a "shaper of the environment," not one who "compromises with the environment." Contrary to what weak-willed individuals lacking personality say—"If you don't want to be disgraced, conform to the crowd"—people of faith and possessors of independent thought say: "Conforming to the crowd is the very disgrace!"

B: "Migration" (Hijrah) from polluted environments is another lesson from this exemplary event. They left palatial, comfortable homes full of material blessings and submitted to various deprivations in a cave lacking everything, in order to preserve their faith and not strengthen the apparatus of oppression, injustice, disbelief, and polytheism. [^1]

C: "Taqiyyah" (precautionary dissimulation) in its constructive sense is another lesson of this story. They insisted that their situation not be revealed to the people of the city and remain under the veil of secrets, lest they lose their lives in vain or be forced back into that same corrupt environment. We know that taqiyyah is nothing other than a person concealing their true position in a place where disclosure is fruitless, in order to preserve their strength for the time of struggle and striking the enemy. [^2]

D: The lack of distinction among humans on the path of Allah and the placement of a "minister" alongside a "shepherd," and even the guardian dog that followed their path, is another lesson in this regard. This clarifies that the privileges of the material world and its various ranks have the least effect on separating the ranks of the travelers of the path of truth; for the path of truth is the path of Monotheism (Tawhid), and the path of Monotheism is the path of the unity of all humans.

[^1]: Regarding the importance of migration in Islam and its philosophy, we have provided a detailed discussion in Volume 4, page 90 onwards.

[^2]: Regarding "taqiyyah" as a defensive shield, we have discussed it in detail in Volume 2 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 373 onwards, and provided its jurisprudential documents in the book Al-Qawa'id al-Fiqhiyyah.

E: The wondrous Divine aids during the onset of crises is another result taught to us. We saw how Allah caused the Companions of the Cave to fall into a deep sleep for years to save them from those undesirable social conditions, and woke them at a favorable time—a time when they were appreciated as a group of heroes of the path of Monotheism. We also saw how during this period He preserved their bodies from the harm of incidents and made awe and terror a shield to protect them against intruders.

F: In this story, they taught us the lesson of "purity of nourishment" even in the most difficult conditions, because the food of the human body has a deep effect on the soul, thought, and heart of man, and becoming polluted with forbidden and impure food distances man from the path of God and piety.

G: The necessity of relying on the will of God, seeking help from His grace, and saying "Insha'Allah" (If Allah wills) in news we give about the future was another lesson we learned within this story.

H: We saw that the Quran refers to them as "youths" (fityah), while according to some narrations they were not young in terms of age. If we accept that they were initially ministers of the tyrannical king, it can also be accepted that they were of some age. This shows that the logic of the Quran regarding youth is the observance of the principles of chivalry (jawanmardi)—namely purity, forgiveness, courage, and bravery.

I: The necessity of logical discussion in dealing with opponents is another educational lesson of this story, for when they wanted to criticize the polytheistic religion of their environment, they resorted to logical reasons, examples of which we read in verses 15 and 16 of this same Surah. Fundamentally, the basis of the work of all prophets and Divine leaders in dealing with opponents was free and logical discussion, and the resort to force—and that too only to extinguish the fire of sedition—was limited to cases where logical discussion was not effective or where they prevented logical discussions.

J: Finally, the issue of the possibility of physical resurrection and the return of humans to a new life at the time of the Resurrection: this is the tenth and final lesson this event gives us, the explanation of which we will study extensively in future discussions.

We do not say the educational points of this story are limited to these, but even one of these "ten educational lessons" seems sufficient for the narration of such a story, let alone all of them. In any case, the goal is not entertainment or storytelling; the goal is building resistant, faithful, aware, and brave human beings, one of the ways for which is showing authentic models throughout the eventful history of humanity.

Is the Story of the Companions of the Cave Scientific?

The account of the Companions of the Cave was certainly not in any of the previous Heavenly Books (including the original books and the current distorted ones) and should not be, because according to historical reports, this incident relates to the centuries after the appearance of Christ عليه السلام.

This event relates to the time of "Decius" (the Arabized form of which is "Daqyanus"), in whose era Christians were under severe torture. According to European historians, this incident occurred between the years 249 and 251 AD. These historians consider the duration of their sleep to be 157 years and know them as the "Seven Sleepers of Ephesus," [^1] while among us they are known as the "Companions of the Cave."

Now let us see where "Ephesus" is and who were the first scholars to write books regarding the story of these sleepers, and in which century: "Ephesus" or "Ephesos" was one of the cities of Asia Minor (modern-day Turkey, which was part of the old Eastern Rome) and was located near the Cayster River about 40 miles southeast of "Izmir," and was considered the capital of the "Ionian" kings.

"Ephesus" also has global fame because of the famous temple and idol-house of "Artemis," which was one of the seven wonders of the world. [^2] It is said that the story of the Companions of the Cave was explained for the first time in the 5th century AD by a Christian scholar named "James" (Jacob), who was a bishop of the Syrian church, in a treatise written in the Syriac language. Then another person named "Gregory" translated that treatise into Latin and titled it "The Glory of the Martyrs." [^3] This itself shows that this incident was famous among Christians a century or two before the appearance of Islam and was of interest to church circles.

Of course, as mentioned, some of its specifics—including the duration of their sleep—differ from what has come in Islamic sources, for the Quran explicitly mentions the duration of their sleep as 309 years.

On the other hand, according to the report of "Yaqut al-Hamawi" in the book Mu'jam al-Buldan (Vol. 2, p. 806), "Ibn Khordadbeh" in the book al-Masalik wa al-Mamalik (pp. 106 to 110), and "Abu Rayhan al-Biruni" in the book al-Athar al-Baqiyah (p. 290), a group of ancient travelers found a cave in the city of "Abas" containing several dried bodies, and they consider it probable that this matter relates to this very story.

From the tone of the Quranic verses in Surah al-Kahf, and the occasions of revelation (shan al-nuzul) entered in Islamic sources for the said verses, it is understood that the said incident was also famous as a historical event among Jewish scholars. Thus, it becomes certain that the account of this long sleep has appeared in the historical sources of various nations. [^1]

Regarding the long sleep of the sleepers of the city of Ephesus (the Companions of the Cave) which lasted for many years, some individuals might doubt and not consider it consistent with scientific standards, and therefore assume it to be in the rank of "myths and legends" because:

First: Such a long life of several hundred years is unlikely for awake individuals, let alone for those asleep! Second: If we accept that such a life is possible in wakefulness, it is impossible for someone who is asleep, because the problem of food and water arises—how is it possible for a human to remain alive for such a duration without food and water? If we consider, for example, one kilogram of food and one liter of water for each day, for the life of the Companions of the Cave, more than one hundred tons of food and one hundred thousand liters of water would be required, the storage of which within the body itself makes no sense. Third: If we overlook all of this, the objection still arises that the body remaining in uniform conditions for such a long duration would damage its organism and cause extensive waste.

These objections might, at first glance, visualize dead ends and insurmountable obstacles in the path of this issue, while it is not so because:

First: The issue of a long-term life is not an unscientific issue, for we know that the lifespan of no living being—from a scientific perspective—has a fixed and definite measure such that death is certain upon its arrival. In other words, it is true that the physical forces of man, whatever they may be, are ultimately limited and exhaustible, but this statement does not mean that the body of a human, or another living being, does not have the ability to live more than the normal amount. For example, just as in nature when water reaches one hundred degrees of heat it boils and at zero degrees it freezes, it is not the case that when a human reaches one hundred or one hundred and fifty years, his heart must necessarily stop and his death arrive. Rather, the measure of the lifespan of living beings depends heavily on their living conditions and is completely changeable with the change of conditions. A living proof of this statement is that, on one hand, we see that none of the world's scientists have determined a specific measure for human life, and on the other hand, they have been able in laboratories to sometimes increase the lifespan of some living beings to two or several times, and sometimes to 12 times and more. Even today, they give us hope that in the future, with the discovery of "new scientific methods," the human lifespan will increase to several times the current one. This is regarding the principle of the issue of longevity.

Second: Regarding water and food in this long sleep, if it were a normal and ordinary sleep, one could grant the objector that this matter is not consistent with scientific principles, because the metabolism of the body during even ordinary sleeps is slightly less than the state of wakefulness, but overall for many consecutive years, it would be very high. However, it must be noted that there are sleeps in the world of nature in which the body's food consumption is very negligible, such as hibernation.

Hibernation

There are many living creatures that fall into sleep throughout the winter and have what is scientifically called "hibernation." In this type of sleep, vital activities almost stop, and only a very weak flame of it remains lit. The "heart" almost stops beating, or more accurately, its beat becomes so faint that it is not felt at all. In such cases, the body can be compared to massive furnaces that, when turned off, have a "pilot light" still burning. It is clear that the amount of fuel that the furnace requires in one day of oil (for example) to send its massive flames to the sky might be the fuel for tens or hundreds of years while the very small pilot light is burning (of course, this depends on the massive flames of the furnace's awake state and the state of its pilot light).

Scientists say regarding the hibernation of some creatures: "If we take a toad that is in hibernation out of its place, it appears dead; there is no air in its lungs, its heartbeat is so weak that it cannot be detected. Among cold-blooded animals that have hibernation, many butterflies, insects, land snails, and reptiles can be named. Some (warm-blooded) mammals also have hibernation. During the period of hibernation, vital activities become very slow, and the stored fat of their bodies is gradually consumed." [^1]

[^1]: A'lam al-Quran, p. 153.

[^2]: Adapted from the book Qamus-e Muqaddas (Holy Dictionary), p. 87.

[^3]: A'lam al-Quran, p. 154.

[^1]: Resurrection and the World After Death, pp. 163 to 165.

[^1]: Adapted from the book Farhangnameh (New Persian Encyclopedia), under the entry "Hibernation."

The point is that we have a type of sleep in which the need for food is reduced extraordinarily and vital activities reach near zero, and incidentally, this very matter helps prevent the wear and tear of organs and the longevity of such creatures. Fundamentally, hibernation is a very precious opportunity for these animals that are likely unable to obtain their food in winter.

Another Example: The Burial of Yogis

Regarding yogis (murtadan), it has also been seen that some of them, before the astonished eyes of a number of skeptical individuals, have been placed in a coffin and sometimes buried under the earth for a period of one week. After the said period ended, they were taken out and given massage and artificial respiration until they gradually returned to a normal state. If the issue of the need for food during this period is not important, the issue of the need for atmospheric oxygen is very important. For we know the sensitivity of brain cells, especially toward oxygen, and their need for this vital substance is so great that if they are deprived of it for a few minutes, they are ruined. Now, how is it that the yogi tolerates the lack of oxygen, for example, for a period of about one week? The answer to this question is not very difficult given the explanation we provided. During this period, the vital activity of the yogi's body "almost" stops; therefore, the cells' need for oxygen and its consumption is extraordinarily reduced, such that during this period, the same air in the coffin enclosure is sufficient for the nourishment of his body's cells for one week!

Freezing the Body of a Living Human

Regarding the freezing of the bodies of living creatures and even the human body (to prolong their lives), there are many theories and discussions today, part of which has been put into practice. According to these theories, it is possible by placing the body of a human or animal in sub-zero cold according to a specific method to stop his life and living without him actually dying, and after a period as necessary, place him in suitable heat and return him again to a normal state! For space travels to distant planets which likely take hundreds or thousands of years, plans have been proposed, one of which is this very plan: to place the astronaut's body in a special enclosure and freeze it, and after many years upon approaching the intended planets, with an automatic system, normal heat returns to the enclosure and they return to a normal state without having actually wasted any life! In one of the scientific journals, this news was published that in recent years, a book regarding the freezing of the human body for the sake of a long life was published by "Robert Nelson," which had a wide and far-reaching reflection in the world of science. In an article prepared in the said journal in this regard, it was specified that recently a specific scientific branch among the branches of science has also come into existence under this same title. In the said article, we read: "Eternal life has always been among the golden and long-standing dreams of man throughout history, but now this dream has turned into reality, and this matter is indebted to the wondrous progress of a new science called 'Cryonics' (the science that takes man to frozen worlds and maintains him like a frozen body, in the hope of the day when scientists return him to life again). Is this logic believable? Many prominent and distinguished scientists think about this issue from other aspects, and publications such as 'Life' and 'Esquire' as well as newspapers across the world have intensely engaged in discussion regarding this important matter, and most importantly, a program is currently (in this regard) under implementation." [^1] A while ago, it was also announced in the newspapers that among the polar ice which, according to the testimony of its layers, related to several thousand years ago, a frozen fish was found which, after placing it in mild water, resumed life!! And before the astonished eyes of observers, it began to move! It is clear that even in the state of freezing, vital systems do not completely stop as in the state of death, for in that case, a return to life would not be possible; rather, they become extraordinarily slow. From the sum of these discussions, we conclude that stopping or extraordinarily slowing down life is possible, and various scientific studies have confirmed its possibility from various aspects. In this state, the body's food consumption reaches almost zero, and the negligible reserve present in the body can be sufficient for its slow life over many years. Let it not be mistaken: we never wish to deny the miraculous aspect of the sleep of the Companions of the Cave with these words; rather, we wish to bring it closer to the mind from a scientific perspective. For certainly, the sleep of the Companions of the Cave was not an ordinary and normal sleep like our nightly sleeps; it was a sleep that had an exceptional aspect. Therefore, it is no surprise that they (by the will of Allah) fell into a long sleep, neither suffering from a lack of food nor their body's organism being damaged! Interestingly, it is understood from the verses of Surah al-Kahf regarding their account that the manner of their sleep differed greatly from normal sleeps:

وَ تَحْسَبُهُمْ أَيْقاظاً وَ هُمْ رُقُودٌ ...لَوِ اطَّلَعْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ لَوَلَّيْتَ مِنْهُمْ فِراراً وَ لَمُلِئْتَ مِنْهُمْ رُعْباً (Verse 18). "And you would have thought them awake, while they were asleep... If you had looked at them, you would have turned from them in flight and been filled with terror of them."

This verse is proof that they did not have an ordinary sleep, but rather a sleep resembling the state of a dead person—with open eyes! Furthermore, the Quran says: "The sunlight did not shine into their cave," and considering that their cave was likely in one of the heights of Asia Minor in a cold region, the exceptional conditions of their sleep become clearer. On the other hand, the Quran says: "وَ نُقَلِّبُهُمْ ذاتَ الْيَمِينِ وَ ذاتَ الشِّمالِ" (Kahf, Verse 18) "And We turned them to the right and to the left," and this shows that they were not in a state of complete uniformity, and mysterious factors that remain unknown to us! (likely once every year) would turn them to the right and left so that no damage would be caused to their body's organism. Now that this scientific discussion has become sufficiently clear, the conclusion from it regarding the issue of the Resurrection does not need much discussion, for waking up after that long sleep is not unlike being brought to life after death, and it brings the possibility and realization of the Resurrection closer to the mind. [^1]

Ayahs

وَٱصْبِرْ نَفْسَكَ مَعَ ٱلَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ رَبَّهُم بِٱلْغَدَوٰةِ وَٱلْعَشِىِّ يُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَهُۥ ۖ وَلَا تَعْدُ عَيْنَاكَ عَنْهُمْ تُرِيدُ زِينَةَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَلَا تُطِعْ مَنْ أَغْفَلْنَا قَلْبَهُۥ عَن ذِكْرِنَا وَٱتَّبَعَ هَوَىٰهُ وَكَانَ أَمْرُهُۥ فُرُطًا (٢٨)
Content yourself with the company of those who supplicate their Lord morning and evening, desiring His Face, and do not lose sight of them, desiring the glitter of the life of this world. And do not obey him whose heart We have made oblivious to Our remembrance, and who follows his own desires, and whose conduct is [mere] profligacy. (28)
وَقُلِ ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّكُمْ ۖ فَمَن شَآءَ فَلْيُؤْمِن وَمَن شَآءَ فَلْيَكْفُرْ ۚ إِنَّآ أَعْتَدْنَا لِلظَّـٰلِمِينَ نَارًا أَحَاطَ بِهِمْ سُرَادِقُهَا ۚ وَإِن يَسْتَغِيثُوا۟ يُغَاثُوا۟ بِمَآءٍ كَٱلْمُهْلِ يَشْوِى ٱلْوُجُوهَ ۚ بِئْسَ ٱلشَّرَابُ وَسَآءَتْ مُرْتَفَقًا (٢٩)
And say, ‘[This is] the truth from your Lord: let anyone who wishes believe it, and let anyone who wishes disbelieve it.’ Indeed, We have prepared for the wrongdoers a Fire whose curtains will surround them [on all sides]. If they cry out for help, they will be helped with a water like molten copper, which will scald their faces. What an evil drink, and how ill a resting place! (29)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ إِنَّا لَا نُضِيعُ أَجْرَ مَنْ أَحْسَنَ عَمَلًا (٣٠)
As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds—indeed We do not waste the reward of those who are good in deeds. (30)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ جَنَّـٰتُ عَدْنٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهِمُ ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ يُحَلَّوْنَ فِيهَا مِنْ أَسَاوِرَ مِن ذَهَبٍ وَيَلْبَسُونَ ثِيَابًا خُضْرًا مِّن سُندُسٍ وَإِسْتَبْرَقٍ مُّتَّكِـِٔينَ فِيهَا عَلَى ٱلْأَرَآئِكِ ۚ نِعْمَ ٱلثَّوَابُ وَحَسُنَتْ مُرْتَفَقًا (٣١)
For such there will be the gardens of Eden with streams running in them. They will be adorned therein with bracelets of gold and wear green garments of silk and brocade, reclining therein on couches. How excellent a reward, and how good a resting place! (31)

Circumstances of Revelation

Commentators have recorded the following regarding the circumstances of revelation for a portion of the above verses: A group of arrogant wealthy men and self-satisfied nobles of the Arabs came to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله). Pointing toward men of faith such as Salman, Abu Dharr, Suhayb, Khabbab, and their like, they said: "O Muhammad! If you sit at the head of the assembly and distance yourself from these individuals—whose scent offends one's senses and who wear coarse woolen garments—(in short, if your assembly becomes one befitting nobles and personalities!), we will come to you, sit in your gathering, and benefit from your words. But what can we do? As long as this group is present, there is no place for us!"

At this moment, the above verses were revealed, commanding the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) never to surrender to these hollow, deceptive words and to always remain throughout his life with the faithful and pure-hearted individuals like the Salmans and Abu Dharrs, even if their hands are empty of worldly wealth and their clothes are made of wool.

Following the revelation of these verses, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) went in search of this group (it seems they had become saddened upon hearing those words and had gone to a corner of the mosque to worship their Lord). He finally found them at the back of the mosque engaged in the remembrance of Allah. He said: "Praise be to Allah that I did not die until He gave me such a command to be with the likes of you. Indeed, living with you is pleasant, and dying with you is pleasant!" (Ma'akum al-mahya wa ma'akum al-mamat) [1].

Commentary

The Pure-Hearted Barefoot!

Among the lessons taught to us by the story of the People of the Cave was that the criterion for human value is not outward rank, position, or wealth; rather, where the path of Allah is concerned, the minister and the shepherd stand in the same rank. The verses under discussion essentially pursue this same important matter and command the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله): "Stay with those who call upon their Lord morning and evening, seeking only His Pure Essence."

[1] Majma' al-Bayan and Qurtubi, under the commentary of the verses.

(وَ اصْبِرْ نَفْسَكَ مَعَ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ رَبَّهُمْ بِالْغَداةِ وَ الْعَشِيِّ يُرِيدُونَ وَجْهَهُ‌).

The expression wasbir nafsaka ("keep yourself patient") refers to the reality that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was under pressure from arrogant enemies and tainted nobles to drive away the group of poor believers. Therefore, Allah commands him to practice patience and steadfastness against this increasing pressure and never to surrender to them.

The expression "morning and evening" indicates that they are in remembrance of Allah at all times and throughout their entire lives.

The phrase yuriduna wajhahu ("seeking His Face/Essence") is evidence of their sincerity. It indicates that they desire Allah Himself from Allah; they do not worship Him even for the sake of Paradise (though its blessings are great and precious) or out of fear of Hell and its punishments (though its torments are painful). Rather, they worship Him solely for His Pure Essence, for "We have no desire from You other than You!" This is the highest degree of obedience, servitude, love, and faith in Allah [1].

Then, as an emphasis, it continues: "Never turn your eyes away from this group of believers—who are outwardly poor—and do not cast your gaze toward these arrogant, oblivious ones for the sake of the glitz of the world" (وَ لا تَعْدُ عَيْناكَ عَنْهُمْ تُرِيدُ زِينَةَ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا) [2].

Again, for further emphasis, it adds: "And do not obey those whose hearts We have made heedless of Our remembrance" (وَ لا تُطِعْ مَنْ أَغْفَلْنا قَلْبَهُ عَنْ ذِكْرِنا).

"Those who followed their own desires" (وَ اتَّبَعَ هَواهُ‌).

"And those whose every deed is excessive, beyond moderation, and coupled with extravagance" (وَ كانَ أَمْرُهُ فُرُطاً) [3].

It is interesting that the Quran has placed the attributes of these two groups in contrast to one another: The true but impoverished believers have hearts overflowing with the love of Allah, are always in His remembrance, and seek Him. However, the arrogant wealthy are completely heedless of Allah's remembrance, seek nothing but their own desires, and everything about them is beyond the limit of moderation, moving in the path of excess and extravagance.

[1] Regarding the meaning of Wajh (Face) and that it sometimes means "Essence" and sometimes "Face," and the reason for its choice in such cases, we have discussed in detail in Volume 2, page 263. [2] La ta'du is from the root 'ada ya'du, meaning to overstep. Thus, the meaning of the sentence is: "Do not take your eyes off them to look at others." [3] Furut means exceeding the limit, and anything that goes beyond its bounds and turns toward extravagance is called furut.

The importance of this subject is such that in the next verse, the Quran explicitly tells the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله): "Say, this is my program and this is the truth from your Lord; whoever wills, let him believe and accept this truth, and whoever wills, let him disbelieve" (وَ قُلِ الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّكُمْ فَمَنْ شاءَ فَلْيُؤْمِنْ وَ مَنْ شاءَ فَلْيَكْفُرْ).

But know that these world-worshipping oppressors, who with their comfortable lives, glitz, and ornaments, cast a mocking smile at the woolen clothes of the Salmans and Abu Dharrs, have a dark and wretched end, for: "We have prepared for the wrongdoers a Fire whose canopy surrounds them from every side" (إِنَّا أَعْتَدْنا لِلظَّالِمِينَ ناراً أَحاطَ بِهِمْ سُرادِقُها).

Yes, in this worldly life, whenever they became thirsty, they would call out, and servants would present various drinks before them. "But in Hell, when they plead for water, they will be given water like molten metal! Which, if it nears the face, scalds the faces!" (وَ إِنْ يَسْتَغِيثُوا يُغاثُوا بِماءٍ كَالْمُهْلِ يَشْوِي الْوُجُوهَ‌) [1]. "What a wretched drink it is!" (بِئْسَ الشَّرابُ‌). "And what an evil resting place is Hell!" (وَ ساءَتْ مُرْتَفَقاً) [2].

Think about it: water which, if brought near the face, its heat scalds the face—is it drinkable? This is because in the world they drank various pleasant and cool beverages while setting fire to the hearts of the deprived and the oppressed; this is that same fire which has manifested here in this form!

It is remarkable that the Quran here assigns to the wealthy, oppressive, and faithless world-worshippers a set of formalities in Hell similar to the formalities of this world, but with the difference that in the world they have suradiq, meaning high tents ("suradiq" is originally derived from the Persian word sarapardeh) and noble quarters where the poor are not allowed and which are places of pleasure and wine-drinking. But there, their massive tents are "made of the flames of the scorching fire of Hell!" Here, in their pavilions, there are various drinks; as soon as they call the cupbearer, chalices of colorful wines are presented to them. In Hell, they also have a cupbearer and a bringer of drinks, but what water? Water like molten metal! Water as hot as the burning tears of orphans and the fiery sighs of the needy! Yes, everything there is a manifestation of what is here! (We seek refuge in Allah).

Since the method of the Quran is an educational and comparative one, after describing the attributes and the punishment of the selfish world-worshippers, it proceeds to describe the state of the true believers and their exceptionally valuable rewards. First, briefly and then relatively in detail, it says: "Those who believed and did righteous deeds, We shall not let the reward of those who do good be lost," whether it be little or much, general or specific, from anyone, at any age, and under any circumstances (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ إِنَّا لا نُضِيعُ أَجْرَ مَنْ أَحْسَنَ عَمَلًا).

"They are the ones for whom are the Gardens of Eternity" (أُولئِكَ لَهُمْ جَنَّاتُ عَدْنٍ‌). "Gardens of Paradise where rivers flow beneath their trees and palaces" (تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهِمُ الْأَنْهارُ). "They are adorned therein with bracelets of gold" (يُحَلَّوْنَ فِيها مِنْ أَساوِرَ مِنْ ذَهَبٍ‌) [1] "and they wear fine garments of green color, made of thin and thick silk" (وَ يَلْبَسُونَ ثِياباً خُضْراً مِنْ سُنْدُسٍ وَ إِسْتَبْرَقٍ‌). "While they recline therein upon couches and thrones" (مُتَّكِئِينَ فِيها عَلَى الْأَرائِكِ) [2]. "Oh, what a wonderful reward!" (نِعْمَ الثَّوابُ‌). "And what a beautiful gathering of friends!" (وَ حَسُنَتْ مُرْتَفَقاً).

[1] Muhl (rhyming with qufl), as Raghib says in al-Mufradat, means the dregs of oil, which is usually something contaminated, filthy, and foul-tasting. However, a group of other commentators have interpreted it as any kind of molten metal, and the outward meaning of the phrase "scalds the faces" here supports the second meaning. [2] Murtafaq is from the root rifq and rafiq, meaning a gathering place of friends. [1] Asawir is the plural of aswirah, which in turn is the plural of siwar, originally derived from the Persian word dastvar, meaning bracelet. After being Arabized, Arabic verbs were also derived from it. [2] Ara'ik is the plural of arikah, referring to a throne surrounded on all sides like a canopy. Originally, as Raghib says, it is derived from arak, the name of a well-known tree, because Arabs sometimes made special canopies from this tree, or it is from aruk, meaning to reside and stay.

Key Points

1- Class Consciousness: A Major Problem of Societies

Not only do the above verses combat the division of society into two groups of nobles and the poor, but this point is emphasized in many Quranic verses that we have previously passed or will reach later.

Fundamentally, a society where one group (which will naturally be a minority) enjoys the most luxurious life, wallowing in blessings and drowned in extravagance and waste, while simultaneously being tainted by various vices—while another group, forming the majority, is deprived of the most basic and simple means of human life—such a society is neither one that Islam approves of nor does it bear the color of a human society.

Such a community will never see peace. Oppression, suppression, the deprivation of freedom, colonialism, and arrogance will surely cast their shadow upon it. Bloody wars have often arisen from societies with such a fabric, and unrest in such societies never ends.

Fundamentally, why should all these divine blessings be placed without reason at the disposal of a limited few, while the majority struggles amidst various deprivations, pain, suffering, hunger, and disease?

Such a society is certainly filled with hatred, enmity, jealousy, pride, arrogance, oppression, tyranny, and all factors of ruin. If we see that all the great Prophets, especially the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), fought a vigorous and continuous battle against such a system, it is for this very reason.

In these types of aristocratic societies, the gatherings of the nobles have always been separate from the gatherings of the poor. Their neighborhoods are separate, their centers of recreation and assembly are separate (if the poor even have a recreational center), their customs and traditions are completely different, and even their graveyards have been separate!

This separation, which is contrary to the spirit of humanity and the spirit of all heavenly laws, was not and is not tolerable for any man of God. In the pagan Arab society, this situation ruled intensely, to the point that they sometimes considered the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) greatest flaw to be that the barefoot and impoverished Salmans and Abu Dharrs (but with hearts full of faith, love for God, courage, and sacrifice) surrounded him.

In the pagan society of the time of Noah (عليه السلام), the idol-worshipping nobles raised the same objection to Noah: why have the "aradhil" (the lowliest)—as they called them—followed you? (Aradhil meaning the lowly! Because these blind-hearted people thought the measure of greatness and lowliness was dirhams and dinars) (فَقالَ الْمَلَأُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ قَوْمِهِ ما نَراكَ إِلَّا بَشَراً مِثْلَنا وَ ما نَراكَ اتَّبَعَكَ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ هُمْ أَراذِلُنا—Surah Hud, Verse 27).

And we saw how a group of these faithless self-worshippers refused to sit beside the faithful poor, even for a few moments. We have also read in Islamic history how the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), by setting aside the first group and giving room to the second, built a society that was "monotheistic" (tawhidi) in the true sense of the word—a society where latent talents blossomed and the criteria for value and personality were genius, human values, piety, knowledge, faith, Jihad, and righteous deeds.

Today, as well, until we strive to build such a society and, by taking the program of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) as a model, remove class-based thinking from minds through education, the formulation of correct laws, and their precise implementation—even if global arrogance dislikes it and rises in opposition—we will never have a healthy and human society.

2- Comparison of Life in This World and the Next

We have said many times that the "embodiment of deeds" is one of the most important issues related to the Resurrection. We must know that what exists in that world is a vast, extensive, and evolved reflection of this world. Our deeds, thoughts, social methods, and various moral traits will be embodied in that world and will always be with us.

The above verses are a vivid depiction of this very reality. The oppressive and monopolistic wealthy who reclined in pavilions in this world, intoxicated by wines, and tried to keep everything of theirs separate from the impoverished believers, will also have a suradiq (canopy/pavilion) there, but made of scorching fire! For oppression is, in reality, a scorching fire that incinerates the harvest of the lives and hopes of the oppressed.

There, they also have drinks that are a manifestation of the inner reality of worldly wine and drinks prepared from the heart's blood of the deprived people. The drink gifted to these oppressors in that world not only burns the entrails but is like molten metal that, before being drunk, roasts their faces when brought near the mouth and face!

Conversely, those who, to maintain purity and observe the principles of justice, turned their backs on these luxuries and contented themselves with a simple life, and endured the deprivations of this world for the sake of implementing the principles of justice for Allah, will find gardens of Paradise with rivers of flowing water, the best clothes and ornaments, and the most soul-stirring gatherings awaiting them. This is a manifestation of their pure intention, as they wanted blessings for all of Allah's servants.

3- The Relationship Between Self-Worship and Heedlessness of Allah

The human soul is filled either by Allah or by desire (hawa), and combining the two is impossible. Self-worship is the source of heedlessness of Allah and Allah's creation. Self-worship is the factor of alienation from all moral principles, and ultimately, self-worship sinks a person into themselves and distances them from all the realities of the world.

A self-worshipping person thinks of nothing but the saturation of their own lusts; awareness, forgiveness, sacrifice, and spirituality have no meaning for them.

The relationship between these two is well-expressed in the above verses where it says: (وَ لا تُطِعْ مَنْ أَغْفَلْنا قَلْبَهُ عَنْ ذِكْرِنا وَ اتَّبَعَ هَواهُ وَ كانَ أَمْرُهُ فُرُطاً) Here, first, heedlessness of Allah is mentioned, followed by the following of desire, and interestingly, the result is mentioned as "excess" (furut) in an absolute sense.

Why is the self-worshipper always caught in excess? Perhaps one reason is that human nature in material pleasures always moves toward seeking more. Someone who yesterday became intoxicated by a certain amount of narcotics will not be intoxicated by that same amount today and must gradually increase the amount. Someone who yesterday was satisfied by a well-equipped palace of several thousand meters finds it an ordinary matter today. In the same way, in all branches of desire and whim, they constantly step toward excess until they ruin and destroy themselves.

4- Ornamental Clothing in the Other World

A question may arise for many: Allah has criticized the glitz and glamour of the world in the Holy Quran, yet He promises such things to the believers in the other world—gold ornaments, silk fabrics (thin and thick), beautiful couches and thrones, and the like.

In response to this question, we first consider it necessary to note that we never interpret these verses like those apologists who consider all these terms as metaphors for spiritual concepts. For we have learned from the Quran itself that the Resurrection has both a "spiritual" and a "physical" aspect. Accordingly, the pleasures of that world must be in both sectors, though undoubtedly its spiritual pleasures are incomparable to the physical ones.

Yet, at the same time, one cannot hide the fact that we see only a distant shadow of the blessings of that world and hear words by way of allusion. For that world, compared to this world, is like this world compared to the mother's womb and the fetal state. Just as a "mother," if she could establish a connection with her fetus, could only describe the beauties of this world—the shining sun, the glowing moon, springs, gardens, flowers, and the like—to the child in her womb through allusions, because she does not possess sufficient words to express these concepts in a way the child could understand; similarly, it is not possible to fully recount the material and spiritual blessings of the Resurrection for us who are besieged in the womb of this world.

With this introduction clarified, we turn to the answer to the question: If Allah has criticized the glitz and glamour of this world, it is because the limitations of this world cause the acquisition of such a life to be coupled with various types of oppression, and its utilization to be coupled with heedlessness and ignorance.

The discriminations that arise from this path are the source of hatreds, jealousies, enmities, and ultimately bloodshed and wars.

But in that world, where everything is vast, neither does the acquisition of these ornaments create difficulty, nor does it cause discrimination or the deprivation of anyone, nor does it provoke hatred or resentment. In that environment filled with spirituality, it does not make one heedless of Allah, nor does it require the trouble of guarding and protecting, nor does it create jealousy in rivals, nor is it a source of pride and arrogance, nor does it cause distance from Allah's creation or Allah Himself.

Why should the people of Paradise be deprived of such blessings, which are a physical pleasure alongside great spiritual blessings, without any undesirable reaction?

5- Approaching the Wealthy for Their Wealth

Another point that the above verses teach us is that we should not refrain from guiding and directing this or that group just because they are wealthy or have a comfortable life, and so to speak, "cross them out." Rather, what is blameworthy is that we go to them for the sake of benefiting from their material world and, in the words of the Quran, become an instance of "seeking the glitz of the life of the world" (turidu zinata al-hayati al-dunya). However, if the goal is their guidance and direction, or even utilizing their resources for positive and valuable social activities, contact with them is not only not blameworthy but is necessary and obligatory.

Ayahs

۞ وَٱضْرِبْ لَهُم مَّثَلًا رَّجُلَيْنِ جَعَلْنَا لِأَحَدِهِمَا جَنَّتَيْنِ مِنْ أَعْنَـٰبٍ وَحَفَفْنَـٰهُمَا بِنَخْلٍ وَجَعَلْنَا بَيْنَهُمَا زَرْعًا (٣٢)
Draw for them the parable of two men for each of whom We had made two gardens of vines, and We had surrounded them with date palms, and placed crops between them. (32)
كِلْتَا ٱلْجَنَّتَيْنِ ءَاتَتْ أُكُلَهَا وَلَمْ تَظْلِم مِّنْهُ شَيْـًٔا ۚ وَفَجَّرْنَا خِلَـٰلَهُمَا نَهَرًا (٣٣)
Both gardens yielded their produce without stinting anything of it. And We had set a stream gushing through them. (33)
وَكَانَ لَهُۥ ثَمَرٌ فَقَالَ لِصَـٰحِبِهِۦ وَهُوَ يُحَاوِرُهُۥٓ أَنَا۠ أَكْثَرُ مِنكَ مَالًا وَأَعَزُّ نَفَرًا (٣٤)
He had abundant fruits, so he said to his companion, as he conversed with him: ‘I have more wealth than you, and am stronger with respect to numbers.’ (34)
وَدَخَلَ جَنَّتَهُۥ وَهُوَ ظَالِمٌ لِّنَفْسِهِۦ قَالَ مَآ أَظُنُّ أَن تَبِيدَ هَـٰذِهِۦٓ أَبَدًا (٣٥)
He entered his garden while he wronged himself. He said, ‘I do not think that this will ever perish, (35)
وَمَآ أَظُنُّ ٱلسَّاعَةَ قَآئِمَةً وَلَئِن رُّدِدتُّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّى لَأَجِدَنَّ خَيْرًا مِّنْهَا مُنقَلَبًا (٣٦)
and I do not think that the Hour will ever set in. And even if I am returned to my Lord I will surely find a resort better than this.’ (36)

Commentary

A Depiction of the Arrogant’s Stance Against the Oppressed

In the preceding verses, we observed how world-worshippers strive to distance themselves in every aspect from the men of Truth who are destitute, and we subsequently read of their ultimate fate in the Hereafter.

The verses under discussion, by referring to the story of two friends or two brothers—each serving as a model for one of these two groups—clarify the way of thinking, speech, action, and the stance of these two groups.

First, it says: O Prophet, "set forth to them the parable of two men: for one of them We provided two gardens of grapevines, and We surrounded them with date-palms, and between the two We placed a field of crops" (وَ اضْرِبْ لَهُمْ مَثَلًا رَجُلَيْنِ جَعَلْنا لِأَحَدِهِما جَنَّتَيْنِ مِنْ أَعْنابٍ وَ حَفَفْناهُما بِنَخْلٍ وَ جَعَلْنا بَيْنَهُما زَرْعاً).

It was a garden and a farm where everything was perfectly arranged; it contained both grapes and dates, as well as wheat and other grains—a complete and self-sufficient estate.

"Both these gardens were perfect in terms of agricultural produce; the trees had borne fruit, and the crops had formed ears of grain, and neither garden failed in any way" (كِلْتَا الْجَنَّتَيْنِ آتَتْ أُكُلَها وَ لَمْ تَظْلِمْ مِنْهُ شَيْئاً). Most importantly, water—which is the source of life for everything, especially gardens and crops—was available to them in abundance, for "We caused a river to gush forth in the midst of them" (وَ فَجَّرْنا خِلالَهُما نَهَراً).

In this manner, "the owner of this garden and farm had every kind of fruit and income at his disposal" (وَ كانَ لَهُ ثَمَرٌ). However, since the world was going according to his desires, and a person of low capacity and lacking in character is seized by pride when everything conforms to his wishes, he began to rebel and transgress. The first stage of this is seeking superiority and showing arrogance toward others. The owner of these two gardens turned to his friend and, in the course of a conversation, "said to him: I am greater than you in wealth, and more honorable in status and power, and stronger in followers" (فَقالَ لِصاحِبِهِ وَ هُوَ يُحاوِرُهُ أَنَا أَكْثَرُ مِنْكَ مالًا وَ أَعَزُّ نَفَراً).

Therefore, I possess abundant human resources, vast wealth and property, as well as influence and social standing; what do you have to say against me? And what logical argument do you possess?!

Gradually, these thoughts—as is common—reached a peak within him, and he reached a point where he imagined the world to be eternal and his wealth, property, and grandeur to be everlasting: "Arrogantly, while he was in fact wronging himself, he entered his garden (he cast a look at the lush trees whose branches were bent under the weight of fruit, and the grain-filled ears that leaned in every direction, and listened to the murmur of the river that roared and moved forward to irrigate the trees, and out of negligence and ignorance) he said: I do not think that this will ever perish or come to an end" (وَ دَخَلَ جَنَّتَهُ وَ هُوَ ظالِمٌ لِنَفْسِهِ قالَ ما أَظُنُّ أَنْ تَبِيدَ هذِهِ أَبَداً).

He went even further than this, and since the eternity of this world contradicts the establishment of the Resurrection, he thought of denying the Day of Judgment and said: "I do not think the Hour will ever come" (وَ ما أَظُنُّ السَّاعَةَ قائِمَةً). These are words that a group has woven together merely to comfort themselves.

Then he added that even if there were a Resurrection, with all this status and position, "if I am brought back to my Lord, I will surely find a place of return better than this" (وَ لَئِنْ رُدِدْتُ إِلى‌ رَبِّي لَأَجِدَنَّ خَيْراً مِنْها مُنْقَلَباً).

He was immersed in these crude fantasies, and every moment he added new absurdities to his past ones, until his believing companion began to speak and said those things which we shall read in the following verses.

Ayahs

قَالَ لَهُۥ صَاحِبُهُۥ وَهُوَ يُحَاوِرُهُۥٓ أَكَفَرْتَ بِٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكَ مِن تُرَابٍ ثُمَّ مِن نُّطْفَةٍ ثُمَّ سَوَّىٰكَ رَجُلًا (٣٧)
His companion said to him, as he conversed with him: ‘Do you disbelieve in Him who created you from dust, then from a drop of [seminal] fluid, then fashioned you as a man? (37)
لَّـٰكِنَّا۠ هُوَ ٱللَّهُ رَبِّى وَلَآ أُشْرِكُ بِرَبِّىٓ أَحَدًا (٣٨)
But I [say], ‘‘He is Allah, my Lord,’’ and I do not ascribe any partner to my Lord. (38)
وَلَوْلَآ إِذْ دَخَلْتَ جَنَّتَكَ قُلْتَ مَا شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَا قُوَّةَ إِلَّا بِٱللَّهِ ۚ إِن تَرَنِ أَنَا۠ أَقَلَّ مِنكَ مَالًا وَوَلَدًا (٣٩)
Why did you not say, when you entered your garden, ‘‘[This is] as Allah has willed! There is no power except by Allah!’’ If you see that I have lesser wealth than you and children, (39)
فَعَسَىٰ رَبِّىٓ أَن يُؤْتِيَنِ خَيْرًا مِّن جَنَّتِكَ وَيُرْسِلَ عَلَيْهَا حُسْبَانًا مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ فَتُصْبِحَ صَعِيدًا زَلَقًا (٤٠)
maybe my Lord will give me [something] better than your garden, and He will unleash upon it bolts from the sky, so that it becomes a bare plain. (40)
أَوْ يُصْبِحَ مَآؤُهَا غَوْرًا فَلَن تَسْتَطِيعَ لَهُۥ طَلَبًا (٤١)
Or its water will sink down, so that you will never be able to obtain it.’ (41)

Commentary

This is the Response of the Oppressed

These verses are a refutation of the baseless fabrications of that arrogant, self-satisfied, and faithless wealthy man, which we hear from the tongue of his believing friend:

He, who until then had remained silent and listened to the words of this shallow-minded man so that he might pour out whatever was inside him before responding all at once, entered the conversation "and said to him: 'Do you disbelieve in Him who created you from dust, then from a sperm-drop, then fashioned you into a complete man?!'" (قالَ لَهُ صاحِبُهُ وَ هُوَ يُحاوِرُهُ أَ كَفَرْتَ بِالَّذِي خَلَقَكَ مِنْ تُرابٍ ثُمَّ مِنْ نُطْفَةٍ ثُمَّ سَوَّاكَ رَجُلًا).

A question arises here: in the words of that arrogant man mentioned in the previous verses, there was nothing explicitly seen regarding the denial of God's existence, yet the apparent response given by the monotheist man—and the first thing for which he reproaches him—is the issue of denying God.

Therefore, he directs his attention toward the All-Knowing and All-Powerful God through the issue of human creation, which is one of the most prominent proofs of Monotheism (Tawhid).

The God who created man initially from dust; the nutrients present in the earth were absorbed by the roots of trees, and the trees, in turn, became food for animals, and man utilized those plants and the meat of those animals, and his sperm was formed from these. The sperm underwent stages of evolution in the mother's womb until it turned into a complete human being—a human who is superior to all earthly creatures, who thinks, reflects, makes decisions, and subjects everything to his will. Yes, the transformation of worthless dust into such a wondrous being with all those complex systems in his body and soul is one of the great proofs of Monotheism.

In response to this question, commentators have various interpretations:

  1. A group has said that since this arrogant man explicitly denied or doubted the Resurrection (Ma'ad), it necessitates the denial of God. This is because those who deny the physical Resurrection actually deny God's power and do not believe that decomposed dust can once again wear the garment of life. Therefore, the man of faith, by mentioning the "initial creation" of man from dust, then a sperm-drop, and other stages, directs his attention to the infinite power of the Lord, so he may know that we constantly see the issue of Resurrection with our own eyes in the scenes of this very life.

  2. Others have said that his polytheism (shirk) and disbelief (kufr) were because he claimed independent ownership for himself and imagined his ownership to be eternal.

  3. A third possibility also does not seem unlikely: that in a portion of his speech which the Quran has not fully recounted, he had risen to deny God, which becomes clear by the context of the believer's words. Thus, in the next verse, we observe the man of faith saying: "If you have denied God and followed the path of polytheism, I shall never do so."

In any case, these three possibilities mentioned in the interpretation of the above verse are not unrelated to one another, and the words of the monotheist man could be an allusion to all of them.

Then, this man of faith, in order to shatter his disbelief and pride, said: "But as for me, Allah is my Lord," and I take pride and glory in this belief (لكِنَّا هُوَ اللَّهُ رَبِّي) [1]. "You take pride in having gardens, crops, fruits, and abundant water, but I take pride that my Lord is Allah; He is my Creator and my Provider. You boast of your world, and I boast of my creed, faith, and monotheism! 'And I do not associate anyone with my Lord'" (وَ لا أُشْرِكُ بِرَبِّي أَحَداً).

After referring to the issue of Monotheism and Polytheism, which is the most vital and destiny-shaping matter, he reproaches him again, saying: "Why, when you entered your garden, did you not say: 'This is what Allah has willed'?" Why did you not consider all of this to be from God and offer gratitude for His blessing?! (وَ لَوْ لا إِذْ دَخَلْتَ جَنَّتَكَ قُلْتَ ما شاءَ اللَّهُ) [2]. "Why did you not say: 'There is no power or strength except from Allah'?" (لا قُوَّةَ إِلَّا بِاللَّهِ).

If you have tilled the earth, sown seeds, planted saplings, made the trees bear fruit, and attended to everything in its time until it reached this stage, all of this was achieved by using God-given powers and the means and facilities that God placed at your disposal. You have nothing of your own, and without Him, you are nothing!

Then he added: "If you see me as less than you in wealth and children (it is not an important matter) (إِنْ تَرَنِ أَنَا أَقَلَّ مِنْكَ مالًا وَ وَلَداً).

"My Lord may well give me something better than your garden" (فَعَسى‌ رَبِّي أَنْ يُؤْتِيَنِ خَيْراً مِنْ جَنَّتِكَ).

Not only might He give me better than what you have, but: "God may send a thunderbolt from heaven upon your garden, and in a short time, turn this lush and green land into a barren and slippery ground" (وَ يُرْسِلَ عَلَيْها حُسْباناً مِنَ السَّماءِ فَتُصْبِحَ صَعِيداً زَلَقاً).

Or He may command the earth to shake, "and this gushing spring and stream will sink into its depths, such that you will never have the power to seek it out" (أَوْ يُصْبِحَ ماؤُها غَوْراً فَلَنْ تَسْتَطِيعَ لَهُ طَلَباً).

Husban (on the pattern of Luqman) is originally derived from the root hisab (calculation). Later, it came to mean arrows that are counted when being shot, and it also means a punishment that overtakes individuals based on a calculation, which is the intended meaning in the above verse.

Sa'id means the surface layer of the earth (originally derived from the root su'ud, meaning ascent).

Zalaq means a land that is smooth and devoid of any vegetation, such that a person's foot slips upon it. (It is noteworthy that today, to stabilize shifting sands and prevent settlements from being buried under sandstorms, they try to grow plants, vegetation, and trees in them so that they lose that zalaq and slippery state and become controlled).

In reality, the man of faith and monotheist warned his arrogant friend not to set his heart on these blessings, for none of them are reliable.

In effect, he says: You have seen with your own eyes, or at least heard with your ears, that heavenly thunderbolts sometimes, in a brief moment, turn gardens, houses, and crops into a heap of dust or a dry, waterless, and grassless land.

And you have also heard or seen that sometimes a severe earthquake causes underground canals to collapse and springs to dry up, such that they cannot be repaired or restored.

Since you know these things, what is this pride and arrogance for?! Since you have seen these scenes, why this much attachment?! Why do you say, "I do not believe these blessings will ever perish, but rather they will remain forever"? What ignorance and folly is this?!

[^1]: The word Lakinnā was originally Lākin ana, then they were merged and took this form.

[^2]: The phrase Mā shā’ Allāh has an omitted part, and its implied meaning is: Hādhā mā shā’ Allāh (This is what Allah has willed) or Mā shā’ Allāh kā’in (Whatever Allah wills, happens).

Ayahs

وَأُحِيطَ بِثَمَرِهِۦ فَأَصْبَحَ يُقَلِّبُ كَفَّيْهِ عَلَىٰ مَآ أَنفَقَ فِيهَا وَهِىَ خَاوِيَةٌ عَلَىٰ عُرُوشِهَا وَيَقُولُ يَـٰلَيْتَنِى لَمْ أُشْرِكْ بِرَبِّىٓ أَحَدًا (٤٢)
And ruin closed in on his produce, and he began to wring his hands for what he had spent on it, as it lay fallen on its trellises. He was saying, ‘I wish I had not ascribed any partner to my Lord.’ (42)
وَلَمْ تَكُن لَّهُۥ فِئَةٌ يَنصُرُونَهُۥ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَمَا كَانَ مُنتَصِرًا (٤٣)
He had no party to help him, besides Allah, nor could he help himself. (43)
هُنَالِكَ ٱلْوَلَـٰيَةُ لِلَّهِ ٱلْحَقِّ ۚ هُوَ خَيْرٌ ثَوَابًا وَخَيْرٌ عُقْبًا (٤٤)
There, all authority belongs to Allah, the Real. He is best in rewarding, and best in requiting. (44)

Commentary

And This Was the End of Their Affair

Finally, the conversation between these two men came to an end without the monotheistic man being able to penetrate the depths of that arrogant and faithless wealthy man's soul, and he returned to his home with that same spirit and mindset.

He was unaware that the Divine command for the destruction of his gardens and lush crops had been issued, and he had to face the punishment for his arrogance and polytheism in this very world, so that his fate might become a lesson for others.

Perhaps at the very moment when the black veils of night had covered everything, the Divine punishment descended—in the form of a deadly lightning bolt, a crushing and terrifying storm, or a devastating and horrific earthquake. Whatever it was, in a few short moments, it crushed and ruined these vibrant gardens, towering trees, and fruiting crops: "And his fruits were encompassed [by ruin] from every side by the command of Allah" (وَ أُحِيطَ بِثَمَرِهِ).

"Uhit" (أُحِيطَ) is derived from the root "Ihatat" (احاطه), and in such contexts, it means an "all-encompassing punishment" that results in total destruction. In the morning, when the owner of the garden set out toward it with a series of dreams to inspect and benefit from the garden's produce, as soon as he drew near, he was faced with a horrific sight—so much so that his mouth hung open in astonishment, his eyes lost their luster, and he stood frozen.

He did not know if he was seeing this scene in a dream or while awake! The trees had all fallen to the ground, the crops were overturned, and there was hardly a trace of life to be seen there.

It was as if a lush garden and green lands had never existed there, and the mournful cries of owls echoed in its ruins. His heart began to pound, the color drained from his face, his mouth went dry, and all the pride and arrogance that weighed heavily on his heart and mind collapsed at once! It was as if he had awakened from a deep and long sleep: "He began to wring his hands [in grief] over what he had spent on it, while it lay collapsed on its trellises" (فَأَصْبَحَ يُقَلِّبُ كَفَّيْهِ عَلى‌ ما أَنْفَقَ فِيها وَ هِيَ خاوِيَةٌ عَلى‌ عُرُوشِها).
