It was exactly at this moment that he regretted his vain and false words and thoughts, "and he said: 'Oh, I wish I had not associated anyone with my Lord,' and I wish I had never followed the path of polytheism" (وَ يَقُولُ يا لَيْتَنِي لَمْ أُشْرِكْ بِرَبِّي أَحَداً).

Even more tragic was that in the face of all this calamity and disaster, he was all alone: "And he had no group to help him against Allah" (وَ لَمْ تَكُنْ لَهُ فِئَةٌ يَنْصُرُونَهُ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ). And since this was his entire capital and he had nothing else to replace it with, "nor could he defend himself" (وَ ما كانَ مُنْتَصِراً).

In truth, all his arrogant assumptions collapsed and were proven false in this event. On one hand, he used to say, "I do not think this great wealth and capital will ever perish," but he witnessed its demise with his own eyes.

On the other hand, he used to show pride and superiority over his monotheistic and faithful companion, saying: "I am greater than you in wealth and stronger in followers," but after this incident, he saw that no one was his helper or supporter.

Thirdly, he relied on his own power and strength and considered his might to be unlimited, but after this incident and being cut off from everything, he realized his great mistake, for he possessed nothing that could compensate for even a fraction of that great loss.

In principle, the "helpers" and "supporters" who surround a person like flies around sweets for the sake of wealth and money—whom a person sometimes considers a pillar for his days of misfortune—all scatter at once when that blessing disappears. This is because their friendship was not a spiritual bond; it had a material backing that vanished when the material vanished.

But whatever it was, it was too late. This kind of forced awakening, which occurs during the descent of heavy calamities even for the likes of Pharaohs and Nimrods, is worthless. For this reason, it yielded no result for him.

It is true that here he uttered the phrase "لَمْ أُشْرِكْ بِرَبِّي أَحَداً" (I did not associate anyone with my Lord)—the same phrase his faithful friend had said before—but the friend said it in health, while he said it in the midst of calamity!

"And it was at this time that this truth was once again proven: that authority, guardianship, and power belong to Allah, the One who is the Absolute Truth" (هُنالِكَ الْوَلايَةُ لِلَّهِ الْحَقِ). Yes, here it became perfectly clear that all blessings are from Him, and whatever He wills shall be, and nothing can be achieved except by relying on His grace.

Indeed, "He is best in reward and best in providing a final end for the obedient" (هُوَ خَيْرٌ ثَواباً وَ خَيْرٌ عُقْباً).

Therefore, if a person wants to attach his heart to someone, rely on something, and hope for someone's reward, how much better it is that his support be Allah, and his attachment and hope be in the grace and kindness of the Lord.

Key Points

1- The Arrogance of Wealth

In this story, we see a vivid depiction of what we call the "arrogance of wealth," and we become acquainted with the end of this pride, which concludes in polytheism and disbelief.

People of small capacity, when they reach a position and find a slight superiority over others in terms of status and wealth, often fall victim to the affliction of arrogance. First, they try to flaunt their resources to others and use them as a means of seeking superiority. They take the gathering of fly-like sycophants around this sweetness as evidence of their own influence in hearts; this is what the Quran mentions with the phrase "أَنَا أَكْثَرُ مِنْكَ مالًا وَ أَعَزُّ نَفَراً" (I am greater than you in wealth and stronger in followers) in the verses above.

Their love and affection for the world gradually visualize the illusion of its eternity in their eyes, and they cry out: "ما أَظُنُّ أَنْ تَبِيدَ هذِهِ أَبَداً" (I do not think this will ever perish)! Since belief in the eternity of the material world is in clear contradiction with the Resurrection, they consequently rise to deny the Hereafter, saying: "وَ ما أَظُنُّ السَّاعَةَ قائِمَةً" (And I do not think the Hour will ever come).

Their self-centeredness and vanity cause them to consider material superiority as evidence of proximity to the Lord's threshold, and they claim an extraordinary status for themselves with Him, saying: "If we return to Allah and there is a Resurrection and Hereafter, our place there will be even better than here!!" (وَ لَئِنْ رُدِدْتُ إِلى‌ رَبِّي لَأَجِدَنَّ خَيْراً مِنْها مُنْقَلَباً).

These four stages are seen more or less in the lives of world-worshipping power-holders with some variations. In fact, their deviant path begins with world-worship and ends in polytheism, idolatry, disbelief, and denial of the Resurrection, because they worship material power like an idol and consign everything else to oblivion.

2- This lesson-filled story, despite its brevity, teaches us other lessons as well:

A- No matter how vast and extensive the blessings of the material world may be, they are unreliable and unstable. A flash of lightning can, in one night or even a few short moments, turn gardens and crops—the product of many years of a person's life—into a heap of dust and ash and dry, slippery ground.

A minor earthquake can swallow the gushing springs that were the source of all this life and blessing, such that they cannot even be repaired.

B- Friends who circle around a person out of love for material gain are so unreliable and unfaithful that they bid him farewell at the very moment these blessings are separated from him, as if they had made that decision beforehand: "وَ لَمْ تَكُنْ لَهُ فِئَةٌ يَنْصُرُونَهُ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ".

Such events, examples of which we have heard or seen many times, prove that one cannot set one's heart on anything but Allah. A person's true and faithful friends are only those with whom spiritual bonds establish a connection; these are friends in times of wealth, poverty, old age and youth, health and sickness, and honor and humiliation. Even their bonds of affection continue after death.

C- Awakening after calamity is often futile

We have said many times that a forced awakening is neither evidence of an internal revolution and a change in a person's path, nor a sign of regret for past actions. Rather, every human being, when his eyes fall upon the gallows or the waves of calamity and storm, is temporarily affected. In those short moments—whose lifespan is no longer than the lifespan of that calamity—he decides to change his path. But because it has no roots in the depths of his soul, as soon as that storm is extinguished, this thought is also extinguished, and he returns exactly to the starting line.

If we read in Surah An-Nisa, verse 18, that the doors of repentance are closed to a person upon seeing the signs of death, this is the reason. And if we see the Quran saying about Pharaoh in the verses of Surah Yunus (verses 90 and 91) that when his drowning drew near and he was rolling in the waves, he cried out: "I believe in the One God, the God of the Children of Israel," but this repentance was never accepted from him—that too is for this same reason.

D- Neither poverty is proof of humiliation, nor wealth proof of honor

This is another lesson that the above verses teach us.

Of course, in materialist societies and schools of thought, the illusion often arises that poverty and wealth are evidence of humiliation and honor. For this reason, we see the polytheists of the Age of Ignorance wondering at the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله being an orphan or poor, saying: "Why was this Quran not revealed to one of the wealthy men of Mecca or Taif?" (لَوْ لا نُزِّلَ هذَا الْقُرْآنُ عَلى‌ رَجُلٍ مِنَ الْقَرْيَتَيْنِ عَظِيمٍ - Surah Az-Zukhruf, verse 31).

E- The way to break arrogance

When the whispers of arrogance due to wealth and status sprout in the depths of a free person's heart, he must cut its root by considering the history of his own origin: that day when he was worthless dust, that day when he was a weak drop of fluid, that day when he was born from his mother as a weak infant unable to move. Just as the Quran, in the verses above, takes that faithless wealthy man back to his past to break his arrogance, saying through the tongue of that man of faith: "أَ كَفَرْتَ بِالَّذِي خَلَقَكَ مِنْ تُرابٍ ثُمَّ مِنْ نُطْفَةٍ ثُمَّ سَوَّاكَ رَجُلًا" (Do you disbelieve in Him who created you from dust, then from a drop of fluid, then fashioned you into a man?).

F- The world of nature teaches us

Interestingly, in the verses above, when we read the description of those blessed gardens, it says: "وَ لَمْ تَظْلِمْ مِنْهُ شَيْئاً" (And they did not fail [wrong] in anything [of their produce]). Those gardens committed no "wrong" or "injustice" in offering their fruits to the world of humanity. But regarding the owner of that garden, it says: "وَ دَخَلَ جَنَّتَهُ وَ هُوَ ظالِمٌ لِنَفْسِهِ" (And he entered his garden while he was unjust to himself).

This means: O human, look at the world of creation; see how these fruitful trees and blessed crops place whatever they have on the tray of sincerity and offer it to you. There is no sign of monopolization, nor any trace of stinginess or envy. The world of creation is a scene of altruism and giving. The earth offers whatever it possesses to plants and animals altruistically, and trees and plants place all their blessings at the disposal of humans and other living beings. The sun's disk becomes "thinner" day by day as it radiates light, clouds rain, and breezes blow, spreading waves of life everywhere. This is the system of creation.

But you, O human, how do you wish to be the "crown of creation" in this world while trampling its clearest laws?! To become a mismatched patch for the world of creation, dedicating all blessings to yourself and snatching the rights of others?!

Ayahs

وَٱضْرِبْ لَهُم مَّثَلَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا كَمَآءٍ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ فَٱخْتَلَطَ بِهِۦ نَبَاتُ ٱلْأَرْضِ فَأَصْبَحَ هَشِيمًا تَذْرُوهُ ٱلرِّيَـٰحُ ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ مُّقْتَدِرًا (٤٥)
Draw for them the parable of the life of this world: [It is] like the water We send down from the sky. Then the earth’s vegetation mingles with it. Then it becomes chaff, scattered by the wind. And Allah has power over all things. (45)
ٱلْمَالُ وَٱلْبَنُونَ زِينَةُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَٱلْبَـٰقِيَـٰتُ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتُ خَيْرٌ عِندَ رَبِّكَ ثَوَابًا وَخَيْرٌ أَمَلًا (٤٦)
Wealth and children are an adornment of the life of the world, but lasting righteous deeds are better with your Lord in reward and better in hope. (46)

Commentary

The Beginning and End of Life in a Living Portrait

In the preceding verses, the discussion centered on the instability of the blessings of the material world. Since perceiving this reality over a long lifespan of 60 or 80 years is not easy for ordinary individuals, the Quran, in the verses under discussion, vividly portrays this scene through a living and eloquent parable. This is done so that the arrogant and heedless—by observing what has been and continues to be repeated many times in their lives—may awaken from their pride and negligence.

It says: "And present to them the example of the life of this world, [its being] like water which We send down from the sky" (وَ اضْرِبْ لَهُمْ مَثَلَ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا كَماءٍ أَنْزَلْناهُ مِنَ السَّماءِ).

These life-giving drops fall upon mountains and plains. The ready seeds hidden within fertile lands come to life with this rainfall and begin their evolutionary movement.

The hard and resistant shell of the seed softens against the gentleness of the rain, allowing the plant sprout to pass through. Finally, the fresh sprout emerges from the heart of the earth; the sun shines, the breeze blows, and the nutrients of the soil assist it. This young sprout, drawing strength from all these factors of life, continues its growth and development such that "after a short time, the vegetation of the earth mingles and intertwines" (فَاخْتَلَطَ بِهِ نَباتُ الْأَرْضِ).

The face of the mountain and the plain becomes a single tapestry of movement and life. Buds, flowers, and fruits adorn the branches one after another; it is as if they are all laughing, shouting with joy, and dancing in ecstasy.

But this enchanting scene does not last long. The autumn winds begin to blow, scattering the dust of death upon them. The air turns cold, water diminishes, "and it is not long before that lush, green, and smiling plant turns into withered and lusterless branches and leaves" (فَأَصْبَحَ هَشِيماً) [1]. Those leaves that in the spring season clung so tightly to the branches that no storm could detach them become so weak and lifeless that "every wind and breeze detaches them and carries them away in every direction" (تَذْرُوهُ الرِّياحُ) [2].

[1]: "Hashim" is derived from the root "hashm," meaning to break, and here it refers to dried plants that have been broken apart.

[2]: "Tadhruhu" is from the root "dhraw," meaning to scatter.

"Indeed, Allah has been and is Capable over all things" (وَ كانَ اللَّهُ عَلى‌ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ مُقْتَدِراً).

The next verse defines the status of wealth and human resources—the two main pillars of worldly life—within this context, stating: "Wealth and children are the adornment of the life of this world" (الْمالُ وَ الْبَنُونَ زِينَةُ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا). They are like the buds and flowers that appear on the branches of this tree; they are fleeting, short-lived, and if they do not acquire the color of eternity by being placed in the path of "Allah," they are highly unreliable.

In truth, this verse points to two of the most important capitals of worldly life upon which everything else depends: "economic power" and "human power." To achieve any material objective, these two forces are essential. For this reason, those who sit upon the throne of power strive to accumulate these two forces. Especially in ancient times, anyone with more children felt more powerful, as they constituted one of the two main pillars of his strength. In the previous verses, we also saw that the faithless wealthy man boasted of his wealth and manpower to others, saying: "I am greater than you in wealth and mightier in [numbers of] men" (أَنَا أَكْثَرُ مِنْكَ مالًا وَ أَعَزُّ نَفَراً).

Thus, emphasis is placed on "banun," which is the plural of "ibn" (son), because they considered sons to be the capital and active human force, rather than daughters.

In any case, as we saw how the most stable forms of wealth—gardens, agricultural land, and springs—were destroyed in moments, regarding children, besides the fact that their life and health are always vulnerable, they sometimes turn into enemies who, instead of being helpers, become stubborn obstacles! Then it adds: "But the enduring good deeds (al-baqiyat al-salihat) are better with your Lord for reward and better for hope" (وَ الْباقِياتُ الصَّالِحاتُ خَيْرٌ عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ ثَواباً وَ خَيْرٌ أَمَلًا).

Although some commentators have sought to limit the concept of "al-baqiyat al-salihat" to a specific scope, such as the five daily prayers or the dhikr of "Subhan Allah, wal-Hamdu lillah, wa la ilaha illallah, wallahu Akbar," and the like, it is clear that the concept of this expression is so vast and expansive that it includes every righteous thought, idea, word, and deed that naturally endures and whose effects and blessings remain available to individuals and societies.

If we see in some narrations that it is interpreted as the Night Prayer (Salat al-Layl) or the love (mawaddah) for the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, it is undoubtedly intended to state clear examples, not to restrict the concept to these matters—especially since in some of these narrations, the word "min" (from/among), which denotes partitive meaning, is used.

For instance, in a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that he said: "Do not belittle our love, for it is among the enduring good deeds (al-baqiyat al-salihat)."

And in another hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that he said: "Do not neglect saying the four tasbihat, for they are among the enduring good deeds."

Even those unstable riches and children—who are sometimes a trial and a means of testing—if placed in the path of Allah, they too take on the color of "al-baqiyat al-salihat," because the Pure Essence of Allah is eternal, and anything that is for Him and in His way will be eternal.

Key Points

1- Unstable Glitz and Glamour

Once again, in the above verses, we encounter the constructive role of parables in visualizing meanings. We see how the Holy Quran brings deep intellectual truths—which might not be easily understood by many—to the threshold of their senses by mentioning a living and clear example.

It tells humans: the beginning and end of your life are repeated before your eyes every year. If you have lived sixty years, you have watched this scene sixty times.

In the spring, step into the plain and behold that beautiful and enchanting scene where signs of life and vitality are evident from every corner. In the autumn, step into that same green plain of the spring season and see how signs of death appear from every corner.

Yes, you too were once a child like a newly opened bud; then you became a youth like a fresh flower; then you become old and weak, like withered, dried flowers and yellow, depressed leaves. Then the storm of death harvests you, and after a few days, your decayed dust is scattered in every direction by the help of the winds.

However, this event sometimes occurs unnaturally, and in the middle of life's journey, a lightning bolt or a storm ends it, as mentioned in verse 24 of Surah Yunus: "The example of [this] worldly life is but like rain which We have sent down from the sky that the plants of the earth absorb—[those] from which men and livestock eat—until, when the earth has taken on its adornment and is beautified and its people suppose that they have capability over it, there comes to it Our command by night or by day, and We make it as a harvest, as if it had not flourished yesterday" (إِنَّما مَثَلُ الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا كَماءٍ أَنْزَلْناهُ مِنَ السَّماءِ فَاخْتَلَطَ بِهِ نَباتُ الْأَرْضِ مِمَّا يَأْكُلُ النَّاسُ وَ الْأَنْعامُ حَتَّى إِذا أَخَذَتِ الْأَرْضُ زُخْرُفَها وَ ازَّيَّنَتْ وَ ظَنَّ أَهْلُها أَنَّهُمْ قادِرُونَ عَلَيْها أَتاها أَمْرُنا لَيْلًا أَوْ نَهاراً فَجَعَلْناها حَصِيداً كَأَنْ لَمْ تَغْنَ بِالْأَمْسِ).

But it often happens that events in the middle of life do not cause its destruction, and it follows its natural course, yet its end is also withering, scattering, and annihilation, as pointed out in the verse under discussion.

Therefore, whether worldly life follows its natural path or not, sooner or later, the hand of annihilation will seize its hem.

2- Pride-Shattering Factors

We said that many people, upon finding material possibilities and attaining wealth and status, become arrogant, and this pride is a great enemy to human happiness. In the above verses, we saw how pride leads to polytheism and disbelief. For this reason, the Quran, which is a supreme educational book, uses various methods to shatter this pride:

Sometimes it visualizes the annihilation, non-existence, and instability of material capitals (as in the verses above).

Sometimes it warns that these very capitals of yours might become the enemy of your life (as in verse 55 of Surah At-Tawbah).

Sometimes, by mentioning the fate of the arrogant ones of history, such as the Qaruns and Pharaohs, it gives a wake-up call to humanity.

And sometimes it takes the hand of man and leads him to the past of his life—the time when he was a worthless drop of fluid or insignificant dust—or visualizes his future, which is the same, before his eyes, so he may know that between these two states of weakness and inability, pride is a foolish act (as in verse 6 of Surah At-Tariq, verse 8 of Surah As-Sajdah, and verse 38 of Surah Al-Qiyamah).

In this way, it utilizes every means to shatter this satanic trait which, throughout history, has been the source of great crimes.

However, it is certain that people of faith, high capacity, and realism never fall prey to this ugly trait upon reaching a position or wealth. Not only do they not become arrogant, but not even the slightest change appears in their life program. They consider all these matters as borrowed adornments that fall away with the blowing of a single breeze!

Ayahs

وَيَوْمَ نُسَيِّرُ ٱلْجِبَالَ وَتَرَى ٱلْأَرْضَ بَارِزَةً وَحَشَرْنَـٰهُمْ فَلَمْ نُغَادِرْ مِنْهُمْ أَحَدًا (٤٧)
The day We shall set the mountains moving and you will see the earth in full view, We shall muster them, and We will not leave out anyone of them. (47)
وَعُرِضُوا۟ عَلَىٰ رَبِّكَ صَفًّا لَّقَدْ جِئْتُمُونَا كَمَا خَلَقْنَـٰكُمْ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍۭ ۚ بَلْ زَعَمْتُمْ أَلَّن نَّجْعَلَ لَكُم مَّوْعِدًا (٤٨)
They will be presented before your Lord in ranks: ‘Certainly you have come to Us just as We created you the first time. But you maintained that We shall not appoint a tryst for you.’ (48)
وَوُضِعَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبُ فَتَرَى ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ مُشْفِقِينَ مِمَّا فِيهِ وَيَقُولُونَ يَـٰوَيْلَتَنَا مَالِ هَـٰذَا ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَا يُغَادِرُ صَغِيرَةً وَلَا كَبِيرَةً إِلَّآ أَحْصَىٰهَا ۚ وَوَجَدُوا۟ مَا عَمِلُوا۟ حَاضِرًا ۗ وَلَا يَظْلِمُ رَبُّكَ أَحَدًا (٤٩)
The Book will be set up. Then you will see the guilty apprehensive of what is in it. They will say, ‘Woe to us! What a book is this! It omits nothing, big or small, without enumerating it.’ They will find present whatever they had done, and your Lord does not wrong anyone. (49)

Commentary

Woe to us! What a book is this!

Since the previous verses spoke of the self-centered and arrogant individual who, due to his pride, denied the Resurrection and the Day of Judgment, the verses under discussion provide a detailed description of the nature of the Resurrection in three stages:

  1. The stage before the resurrection of human beings, the stage of the resurrection itself, and part of the stage following it.

First, it says: "Remember the day when (the system of the universe collapses as a prelude to a new system) We shall set the mountains in motion, and all obstacles on the surface of the earth will vanish, such that you will see the earth as a leveled plain and everything upon it will be made manifest" (وَ يَوْمَ نُسَيِّرُ الْجِبالَ وَ تَرَى الْأَرْضَ بارِزَةً).

This part of the verses refers to the events that occur on the threshold of the Resurrection. These events are numerous and are frequently mentioned, especially in the short Surahs at the end of the Quran, and are referred to as Ashrat al-Sa'ah (the Signs of the Hour).

The collection of these signs indicates that our current world will be completely destroyed and transformed; mountains will disintegrate, trees and buildings will collapse, the earth will become flat and leveled, and then earthquakes will shatter it. The sun will lose its brilliance, the moon its light, and the stars will be extinguished. Then, a new world—a new heaven and a new earth—will be built upon its ruins, and the new life of humanity will begin in that new world.

Then it adds: "We shall gather them all at this time, such that We shall not leave out even a single person" (وَ حَشَرْناهُمْ فَلَمْ نُغادِرْ مِنْهُمْ أَحَداً).

"Nughadir" is derived from the root ghadr, meaning to leave something behind. For this reason, someone who breaks and leaves his covenant or pledge is said to have committed ghadr. The reason water pits are called ghadir is that a portion of rainwater is left or abandoned in them.

In any case, the above sentence is an emphasis on the fact that "Resurrection" is a universal and all-encompassing decree, and no one will be exempt from it.

The next verse speaks of the manner in which humans are resurrected: "They shall all be presented before your Lord in a row" (وَ عُرِضُوا عَلى‌ رَبِّكَ صَفًّا). This expression may indicate that every group of people who share a single belief or a similar action will be placed in one row, or that everyone will be placed in a single row without any distinction or privilege.

And it will be said to them: "You have all come to Us just as We created you the first time" (لَقَدْ جِئْتُمُونا كَما خَلَقْناكُمْ أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ).

There is no sign of wealth or riches, no gold or ornaments, no material privileges or statuses, no colorful garments, and no friend or helper—exactly as you were at the beginning of creation, in that same initial state!

"But you claimed that We would never appoint a tryst for you" (بَلْ زَعَمْتُمْ أَلَّنْ نَجْعَلَ لَكُمْ مَوْعِداً).

And this was when the pride of material possibilities overwhelmed you, and the inclination toward the immortality of the world made you heedless of the thought of the Hereafter, which is latent in the nature (fitrah) of every human being.

Then, moving to other stages of this great Resurrection, it says: "The Book, which is the record of the deeds of all humans, shall be placed there" (وَ وُضِعَ الْكِتابُ).

"The criminals, when they become aware of its contents, will be terrified, such that you will see the signs of terror on their faces" (فَتَرَى الْمُجْرِمِينَ مُشْفِقِينَ مِمَّا فِيهِ‌).

At this moment, they will cry out and say: "Woe to us! What a book is this?! It leaves out no small or great deed but has counted and numbered it" (وَ يَقُولُونَ يا وَيْلَتَنا ما لِهذَا الْكِتابِ لا يُغادِرُ صَغِيرَةً وَ لا كَبِيرَةً إِلَّا أَحْصاها).

It has accounted for everything down to the last detail, recorded it, and omitted nothing. Truly, how terrifying is it? We had consigned all these deeds to oblivion, to the extent that we sometimes thought no wrongdoing had ever been committed by us; but today we see that the burden of our responsibility is very heavy, and our destiny is dark!

In addition to this written document, fundamentally, "they shall find all they did present" (وَ وَجَدُوا ما عَمِلُوا حاضِراً).

The good and the bad, the oppressions and the acts of justice, the debaucheries and the betrayals—all and everything will be personified before them!

In reality, they are caught by their own deeds: "And your Lord does not wrong anyone" (وَ لا يَظْلِمُ رَبُّكَ أَحَداً).

What overtakes them are the actions they performed in this world; therefore, who can they complain to except themselves?

Key Points

1- The Mountains and the Secret of Their Destruction

We mentioned that on the threshold of the Resurrection, the system of the material world will collapse; among these, the mountains will disintegrate. However, regarding the disintegration of the mountains, various expressions are found in the Quran:

In the verses under discussion, we read: "We shall set the mountains in motion" (nusayyiru al-jibal). The same expression is found in Surah al-Naba, verse 20, and Surah al-Takwir, verse 3.

However, in Surah al-Mursalat, verse 10, we read: "وَ إِذَا الْجِبالُ نُسِفَتْ‌" (And when the mountains are uprooted and scattered like dust by a strong wind).

While in Surah al-Haqqah, verse 14, we read: "وَ حُمِلَتِ الْأَرْضُ وَ الْجِبالُ فَدُكَّتا دَكَّةً واحِدَةً" (And the earth and the mountains are lifted and crushed with a single blow).

And in Surah al-Muzzammil, verse 14, we read: "يَوْمَ تَرْجُفُ الْأَرْضُ وَ الْجِبالُ وَ كانَتِ الْجِبالُ كَثِيباً مَهِيلًا" (The day the earth and the mountains shall quake, and the mountains shall become a heap of shifting sand)!

And verse 5 of Surah al-Waqi'ah says: "وَ بُسَّتِ الْجِبالُ بَسًّا فَكانَتْ هَباءً مُنْبَثًّا" (And the mountains are crumbled to pieces, becoming scattered dust). And finally, verse 5 of Surah al-Qari'ah says: "وَ تَكُونُ الْجِبالُ كَالْعِهْنِ الْمَنْفُوشِ‌" (And the mountains shall be like carded wool [scattered in every direction]).

It is clear that there is no contradiction between these verses; rather, they represent different stages of the collapse of the world's mountains, which are the firmest and most stable parts of the earth. It begins with the movement of the mountains and continues until they are transformed into dust whose color alone is visible in the atmosphere.

What is the cause of this massive and terrifying movement? Certainly, it is not known to us. It is possible that the property of gravity will be temporarily removed, and the rotational movement of the earth will cause the mountains to be crushed and then escape into the depths of space, or such a massive and terrifying movement might occur due to giant atomic explosions in the earth's core.

In any case, all of this is evidence that the Resurrection involves a massive revolutionary aspect both in the world of inanimate matter and in the renewal of human life, all of which initiate a new world on a higher level—a world where, although "spirit" and "body" govern, its fabric will be more expansive and perfect in every respect.

This Quranic expression also reminds us humans of the reality that if great mountains disintegrate, then gardens and springs are even more fragile. Thus, the mark of annihilation is inscribed on the forehead of all beings in this world, even the greatest of them!

2- The Record of Deeds

In Tafsir al-Mizan, under the above verses, we read: From the totality of Quranic verses, it is understood that in the realm of the Resurrection, there are three types of books (records of deeds) for humans:

First, there is a single book placed for the accounting of everyone's deeds, and in fact, all the deeds of the first and the last are recorded in it, as we read in the above verses: "وَ وُضِعَ الْكِتابُ" (And the Book shall be placed), the apparent meaning of which is that a single book is set for the accounting of all humans.

Second is the book that every nation has, meaning the deeds of a nation are recorded in it, as stated in Surah al-Jathiyah, verse 28: "كُلُّ أُمَّةٍ تُدْعى‌ إِلى‌ كِتابِهَا" (Every nation shall be called to its record).

Third is the book that exists for every human being individually, as we read in Surah al-Isra, verse 13: "وَ كُلَّ إِنسانٍ أَلْزَمْناهُ طائِرَهُ فِي عُنُقِهِ وَ نُخْرِجُ لَهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ كِتاباً ..." (And every man's fate We have fastened to his own neck, and We shall bring out for him on the Day of Resurrection a book...) [^1]. It is obvious that there is no contradiction between these verses, for there is no obstacle to a person's deeds being recorded in different books, just as we see similar things in today's world programs; for the precise organization of a country's administration, there is a system and account for every unit, and then those units find a new account within larger units.

However, considering the point that the record of human deeds in the Resurrection is not like the ordinary notebooks and books of this world; it is an articulate, undeniable collection that is perhaps the natural product of human deeds themselves.

In any case, the verses under discussion show that in addition to the recording of human deeds in special books, the deeds themselves are personified and present there: "وَ وَجَدُوا ما عَمِلُوا حاضِراً" (And they shall find all they did present).

Deeds that have vanished from sight in this world in the form of scattered energies have not actually been destroyed (and modern science has also proven that no matter or energy is ever lost, but rather constantly changes form).

On that day, these lost energies, by the command of Allah, will be transformed into matter and personified in appropriate forms; good deeds will appear in beautiful and attractive forms, and bad deeds in ugly and disgraceful faces, and these deeds will be with us. For this reason, in the final sentence of the above verses, He says: "وَ لا يَظْلِمُ رَبُّكَ أَحَداً" (And your Lord does not wrong anyone), because the rewards and punishments are the product of their own deeds.

Of course, some commentators have considered the sentence "وَ وَجَدُوا ما عَمِلُوا حاضِراً" as an emphasis on the issue of the record of deeds, saying the meaning of the sentence is that they find all their actions present and recorded in that book [^1]. Others have assumed the word "recompense" (jaza) to be implied in this verse, saying its meaning is that on that day "they see the recompense of their deeds present" [^2]. However, the first interpretation is more consistent with the apparent meaning of the verses.

Regarding the personification of deeds, we had a detailed discussion in Volume 2 under verse 30 of Al-Imran (Volume 2, page 377), and God willing, we will have more discussion under appropriate verses in the future.

3- Belief in the Resurrection and its Role in Human Education

Truly, the Quran is a marvelous educational book. When it depicts the scene of the Resurrection for humans, it says: the day when all people are presented in organized rows before the court of the Lord's justice, while the harmony of their beliefs and actions is the criterion for their division into different rows.

Their hands are empty of everything; they have left all worldly attachments behind. In the midst of the crowd, they are alone, and in their loneliness, they are part of a crowd. The records of deeds are spread open.

Everything begins to speak and recounts the small and great deeds of humans, and even higher than that, the deeds and thoughts themselves take life, become personified, and each person is surrounded by their personified deeds. People are so occupied with themselves that a mother completely forgets her child, and a child forgets their parents.

The heavy shadow of terror from this divine court of justice and the great punishments awaiting the evildoers envelops everyone; breaths are held in chests, and eyes cease to wander.

Truly, how effective is belief in such a court in educating a human and controlling his desires? And how much awareness, awakening, and attention to responsibilities does it grant to a human?

In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read:

"When the Day of Resurrection comes, a book is handed to the person, then it is said to him: 'Read.' I (the narrator) said: 'Does he recognize what is in it?' He replied: 'He remembers it; there is not a moment, nor a word, nor a step taken, nor anything he did but that he remembers it as if he had done it that very hour. And for that reason they said: "Woe to us! What a book is this that leaves out no small or great deed but has counted it"'" [^1]!

The effective educational role of belief in such a reality is self-evident. Truly, is it possible for a person to have firm faith in such a scene and still commit a sin?!

[^1]: Al-Mizan, Volume 13, page 348... With great regret, during these days when we are writing this section of the Tafsir, we suffered the loss of this great Allamah, commentator of the Holy Quran, high-ranking philosopher, and great scholar of ethics, Ayatollah Allamah Tabataba'i.

A great man who, during his blessed life, rendered very valuable services to the Islamic society, far from any ostentation or self-display, created a transformation in the Qom Seminary and the thoughts of contemporary scholars, trained many prominent students, and left behind very valuable works.

Especially this precious work of his (Tafsir al-Mizan) opened a new chapter in the commentary of the Holy Quran and was a turning point in this important Islamic science. May his soul be drowned in mercy and his memory be forever honored (Date of his passing: November 15, 1981, corresponding to 18 Muharram 1402).

[^1] and [^2]: Fakhr al-Razi in Tafsir al-Kabir, and Qurtubi in Tafsir al-Jami'.

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Volume 3, page 267.

Ayahs

وَإِذْ قُلْنَا لِلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ ٱسْجُدُوا۟ لِـَٔادَمَ فَسَجَدُوٓا۟ إِلَّآ إِبْلِيسَ كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْجِنِّ فَفَسَقَ عَنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّهِۦٓ ۗ أَفَتَتَّخِذُونَهُۥ وَذُرِّيَّتَهُۥٓ أَوْلِيَآءَ مِن دُونِى وَهُمْ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّۢ ۚ بِئْسَ لِلظَّـٰلِمِينَ بَدَلًا (٥٠)
When We said to the angels, ‘Prostrate before Adam,’ they prostrated, but not Iblis. He was one of the jinn, so he transgressed against his Lord’s command. Will you then take him and his offspring for guardians in My stead, though they are your enemies? How evil a substitute for the wrongdoers! (50)
۞ مَّآ أَشْهَدتُّهُمْ خَلْقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا خَلْقَ أَنفُسِهِمْ وَمَا كُنتُ مُتَّخِذَ ٱلْمُضِلِّينَ عَضُدًا (٥١)
I did not make them witness to the creation of the heavens and the earth, nor to their own creation, nor do I take those who mislead others as assistants. (51)
وَيَوْمَ يَقُولُ نَادُوا۟ شُرَكَآءِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ زَعَمْتُمْ فَدَعَوْهُمْ فَلَمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا۟ لَهُمْ وَجَعَلْنَا بَيْنَهُم مَّوْبِقًا (٥٢)
The day He will say [to the polytheists], ‘Call those whom you maintained to be My partners,’ they will call them, but they will not respond to them, for We shall set an abyss between them. (52)
وَرَءَا ٱلْمُجْرِمُونَ ٱلنَّارَ فَظَنُّوٓا۟ أَنَّهُم مُّوَاقِعُوهَا وَلَمْ يَجِدُوا۟ عَنْهَا مَصْرِفًا (٥٣)
The guilty will sight the Fire and know that they are about to fall in it, for they will find no way to escape it. (53)

Commentary

Do Not Take Satans as Your Guardians!

In various verses of the Quran, the story of the creation of Adam, the prostration of the angels before him, and the defiance of Iblis has been mentioned repeatedly. However, as we have previously noted, these repetitions always contain specific points, and in each instance, a particular dimension has been intended.

In other words, a significant event may have various dimensions, and each time it is mentioned, the focus is on one of these aspects.

Since the preceding discussions—through a concrete external example—visualized the stance of the arrogant and proud wealthy against the oppressed poor and their ultimate fate, and since pride has been the primary factor of deviation, disbelief, and rebellion from the very first day, the verses under discussion speak of the matter of Iblis and his refusal to prostrate before Adam. This is so we may know that from the beginning, pride has been the source of disbelief and rebellion.

Furthermore, this story clarifies that deviations originate from satanic whisperings. How foolish it is to surrender to the whisperings of one who has been girded with enmity toward us from the start?

First, it says: "And [remember] when We said to the angels, 'Prostrate before Adam,' and they all prostrated except Iblis" (وَ إِذْ قُلْنا لِلْمَلائِكَةِ اسْجُدُوا لِآدَمَ فَسَجَدُوا إِلَّا إِبْلِيسَ‌).

This exception might create the illusion that Iblis was of the same genus as the angels, whereas angels are infallible; so how could he pursue the path of rebellion and disbelief?! Therefore, it immediately adds: "He was of the jinn, and he departed from the command of his Lord" (كانَ مِنَ الْجِنِّ فَفَسَقَ عَنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّهِ‌).

He was not of the angels, but due to his servitude, obedience, and proximity to the Lord, he took a place in the ranks of the angels, and perhaps was even their teacher. However, for the sake of one hour of pride and arrogance, he fell so far that he lost all his spiritual capital and became the most driven-away and detested being in the presence of God.

Then it says: "Will you then take him and his progeny as your guardians instead of Me?!" (أَ فَتَتَّخِذُونَهُ وَ ذُرِّيَّتَهُ أَوْلِياءَ مِنْ دُونِي‌).

"While they are enemies to you" (وَ هُمْ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ).

A fierce and sworn enemy who has decided upon the misguidance and misery of all of you, and who openly declared his enmity toward your father, Adam, from the first day.

"Evil is the exchange for the wrongdoers" (بِئْسَ لِلظَّالِمِينَ بَدَلًا) ^1. Truly, how ugly it is for a human to abandon the All-Knowing, Aware, Compassionate, Merciful, and Grace-bestowing God, and to accept Satan and his gang in His place. This is the ugliest of choices. What rational person accepts an enemy—who from the first day has been bent on his destruction and has taken an oath to that effect—as a guardian, leader, guide, and support?!

^1: "Badalan" (بَدَلًا) is grammatically a tamyiz (specification), and the subject of "bi'sa" (بِئْسَ) is Satan and his gang, or the worship of Satan and his gang is the subject.

The next verse provides another reason for the invalidity of this false notion, saying: "I did not make them witnesses to the creation of the heavens and the earth, nor even to the creation of their own selves" (ما أَشْهَدْتُهُمْ خَلْقَ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ لا خَلْقَ أَنْفُسِهِمْ‌).

...so that We might seek their help in the creation of the world or that they might become aware and informed of the secrets of creation.

Therefore, one who had no involvement in the creation of the world or even his own kind, and is in no way aware of the secrets and mysteries of creation—how can he be worthy of guardianship or worship? Fundamentally, what power does he have, and what role can he play? He is a weak and helpless being, even unaware of his own matters; how can he lead others? Or rescue them from problems and afflictions?! And at the end, it adds: "And I would never take the misleaders as assistants" (وَ ما كُنْتُ مُتَّخِذَ الْمُضِلِّينَ عضُداً).

That is to say, creation is established upon truth, righteousness, and guidance. A being whose program is misguidance and corruption can have no place in the administration of this system, for he is exactly in the opposite direction of the system of creation and existence. He is a saboteur and a destroyer, not a reformer or a creator of perfection.

The last verse under discussion warns again: "And [remember] the Day He will say, 'Call those whom you claimed to be My partners' [to come to your aid]" (وَ يَوْمَ يَقُولُ نادُوا شُرَكائِيَ الَّذِينَ زَعَمْتُمْ‌).

For a lifetime you spoke of them and prostrated at their thresholds; now that the waves of punishment and retribution have surrounded you, cry out so that they may hasten to your aid for at least an hour.

Those who seemingly still have the residues of this world's thoughts in their brains cry out and "call upon them, but those [imaginary deities] do not even respond to their call," let alone hasten to their aid (فَدَعَوْهُمْ فَلَمْ يَسْتَجِيبُوا لَهُمْ‌). "And We have placed between them a place of destruction" (وَ جَعَلْنا بَيْنَهُمْ مَوْبِقاً) ^1.

The final verse under discussion describes the end of the followers of Satan and the polytheists as follows: "On that day, the criminals shall see the Fire" (وَ رَأَى الْمُجْرِمُونَ النَّارَ). And the Fire which they had never believed in becomes manifest before their eyes.

Here, they realize their past mistakes: "And they become certain that they are to fall into it—and the Fire into them!" (فَظَنُّوا أَنَّهُمْ مُواقِعُوها).

"And they will find no way of escape from it" (وَ لَمْ يَجِدُوا عَنْها مَصْرِفاً).

Neither do their fabricated deities come to their rescue, nor is the intercession of intercessors effective for them, nor can they escape the clutches of the Hellfire—a fire ignited by their own deeds—through lies, falsehoods, or recourse to "wealth" and "power."

It is necessary to note that although the word "zannu" (ظَنُّوا) comes from the root "zann" (conjecture), here and in many other instances, it is used to mean "certainty" (yaqin). Thus, in Surah al-Baqarah, verse 249, when speaking of the true believers and steadfast mujahideen who rose with Talut to fight the tyrant Jalut, it uses the expression: قالَ الَّذِينَ يَظُنُّونَ أَنَّهُمْ مُلاقُوا اللَّهِ كَمْ مِنْ فِئَةٍ قَلِيلَةٍ غَلَبَتْ فِئَةً كَثِيرَةً بِإِذْنِ اللَّهِ‌:

"Those who had firm faith in the Resurrection said: 'How often a small group (but with faith) has overcome a large group by Allah's command.'"

Additionally, the word "muwaqi'uha" (مُواقِعُوها) from the root "muwaqa'ah" means falling upon one another. It refers to the fact that they fall into the Fire and the Fire falls upon them; the Fire penetrates them and they are in the Fire, for we have read in other verses of the Quran that "the criminals themselves are the fuel of the Fire" (Surah al-Baqarah, verse 24).

Key Points

1- Was Satan an Angel?

We know that angels are pure and infallible, and the Quran has testified to their purity and infallibility where it says: "Nay, they are honored servants. They do not precede Him in speech, and they act by His command" (Surah al-Anbiya, verses 26 and 27).

Fundamentally, since their essence is intellect and not lust, pride, arrogance, selfishness, and generally the motives for sin do not exist within them.

On the other hand, the exception of "Iblis" in the above verses (and some other verses of the Quran) from the assembly of "angels" creates the notion that Iblis was an angel. Given his disobedience and rebellion, the question arises: how could such major sins be committed by an angel?! Especially since it is mentioned in some sermons of Nahj al-Balagha:

ما كان اللَّه سبحانه ليدخل الجنة بشرا بامر اخرج به منها ملكا:

"It is never possible for Allah, the Glorified, to admit a human into Paradise for an act because of which He expelled an angel from it" (referring to the pride of Iblis) ^1. The above verses solve this problem by saying: "He was of the jinn" (كانَ مِنَ الْجِنِ‌). They are beings hidden from our eyes, possessing intellect, consciousness, anger, and lust.

And we know that whenever the word "jinn" is used in the Quran, it refers to this group. However, those commentators who believe Iblis was an angel interpret the above verse in its literal linguistic sense, saying that "He was of the jinn" means Iblis was hidden from sight, like other angels, whereas this meaning is completely contrary to the apparent sense.

Among the clear proofs that establish our claim is that the Quran says on one hand: "And He created the jinn from a smokeless flame of fire" (Surah ar-Rahman, verse 15).

And on the other hand, when Iblis refused to prostrate before Adam, his logic was: "You created me from fire and created him from clay [and fire is superior to clay]" (Surah al-A'raf, verse 12).

Furthermore, the above verses attribute "progeny" (children) to Iblis, while we know that angels do not have progeny.

The sum of what has been said, combined with the essential nature of angels, clearly testifies that Iblis was never an angel. However, because he was in their ranks and had worshipped Allah so much that he occupied the position of the near-stationed angels of Allah, he was included in the address regarding the prostration to Adam. His defiance was expressed as an exception in the Quranic verses, and in the Qasi'ah sermon, the name "angel" was applied to him metaphorically. (Reflect on this).

In the book Uyun al-Akhbar, we read from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام:

"The angels are all infallible and protected from disbelief and ugliness by the grace of the Lord." The narrator of the hadith says: I said to the Imam, "Was Iblis not an angel?" He replied: "No, he was of the jinn. Have you not heard the word of Allah where He says: 'And [remember] when We said to the angels, "Prostrate before Adam," and they all prostrated except Iblis; he was of the jinn'?" ^1 In another hadith narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, one of his close companions says: "I asked the Imam about Iblis, whether he was of the angels. He said: 'No, he was of the jinn, but he was with the angels, such that they thought he was of their kind (due to his worship and proximity to the Lord), but Allah knew he was not of them. When the command for prostration was issued, what We know occurred'" (the veils were removed and the nature of Iblis was revealed) [^2]. Regarding Iblis and Satan in general, we have provided detailed discussions under verses 11 to 18 of Surah al-A'raf (Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 6, p. 98 onwards), and in Surah al-An'am under verse 112 (Vol. 5, p. 406), and in Surah al-Baqarah under verse 34 (Vol. 1).

^1: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 192 (al-Qasi'ah). ^1 and [^2]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 267.

2- The Misguided Should Not Be Invited as Assistants

Although the above verses speak of Allah and the denial of any helper for Him from among the misguided—and we know that fundamentally Allah has no need for an assistant or helper, whether misguided or not—this is a great lesson for everyone. In collective endeavors, one should always seek the help of those who are themselves on the correct path of truth and justice and who invite others to such a path.

We have often seen pure individuals who, due to a lack of care in choosing their assistants, became entangled in various deviations, problems, failures, and miseries. A group of misguided and misguiding people surrounded them, brought their system of work to ruin, and ultimately blew away all their human and social capital.

In the history of Karbala, we read that Imam Husayn عليه السلام, the Master of Martyrs, encountered Ubaydullah ibn Hurr on the way. The Imam عليه السلام went to see Ubaydullah, and he showed great respect. However, when the Imam invited him to help, he swore that he had not left Kufa except to avoid this war. Then he added, "I know that if you fight this group, you will be the first to be killed, but I offer you this sword and my horse." The Imam turned his face away from him and said: "When you are stingy with your own life, we have no need for your wealth." Then he recited this verse: "And I would never take the misleaders as assistants" (وَ ما كُنْتُ مُتَّخِذَ الْمُضِلِّينَ عَضُداً), referring to the fact that "you are misguided and a misleader, and not worthy of being an assistant" ^1. In any case, having no helper is better than a person seeking help from contaminated and impure individuals and gathering them around himself.

^1: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 268.

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ صَرَّفْنَا فِى هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانِ لِلنَّاسِ مِن كُلِّ مَثَلٍ ۚ وَكَانَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ أَكْثَرَ شَىْءٍ جَدَلًا (٥٤)
We have certainly interspersed this Quran with every kind of parable for the people. But man is the most disputatious of creatures. (54)
وَمَا مَنَعَ ٱلنَّاسَ أَن يُؤْمِنُوٓا۟ إِذْ جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْهُدَىٰ وَيَسْتَغْفِرُوا۟ رَبَّهُمْ إِلَّآ أَن تَأْتِيَهُمْ سُنَّةُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ أَوْ يَأْتِيَهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ قُبُلًا (٥٥)
Nothing has kept these people from believing and pleading to their Lord for forgiveness when guidance came to them, except [their demand] that the precedent of the ancients come to pass for them, or that the punishment come to them, face to face. (55)
وَمَا نُرْسِلُ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ إِلَّا مُبَشِّرِينَ وَمُنذِرِينَ ۚ وَيُجَـٰدِلُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِٱلْبَـٰطِلِ لِيُدْحِضُوا۟ بِهِ ٱلْحَقَّ ۖ وَٱتَّخَذُوٓا۟ ءَايَـٰتِى وَمَآ أُنذِرُوا۟ هُزُوًا (٥٦)
We do not send the apostles except as bearers of good news and as warners, but those who are faithless dispute fallaciously to refute thereby the truth, having taken My signs and what they are warned of in derision. (56)

Commentary

As If They Only Await Punishment!

These verses serve as a conclusion to the entirety of the preceding discussions and also provide an indication of the topics to come.

The first verse states: "And We have certainly explained for the people in this Qur'an every [kind of] example" (وَ لَقَدْ صَرَّفْنا فِي هذَا الْقُرْآنِ لِلنَّاسِ مِنْ كُلِّ مَثَلٍ‌).

We have brought various examples from the shocking history of predecessors; We have recited into the ears of the people the painful events of their lives and the bitter and sweet memories of history. We have turned matters over and over so that hearts ready to accept may embrace the Truth, and so that the proof is completed for others, leaving no room for ambiguity.

Yet, despite this, a group of rebellious and defiant people never believed, because "man is, more than anything else, quarrelsome" (وَ كانَ الْإِنْسانُ أَكْثَرَ شَيْ‌ءٍ جَدَلًا). The word "صرفنا" (sarrafna) is derived from the root "tasrif," meaning to change, transform, or turn something from one state to another. The purpose of this expression in the above verse is that We have spoken to the people in various garbs, through different countenances, and in every language that had the potential to influence them.

"Jadal" (جدل) refers to a conversation conducted on the basis of conflict and the desire to dominate another. Therefore, "mujadalah" means two people rising against each other in debate and dispute. This word—as Raghib states—is originally derived from "jadaltu al-khayl" (I twisted the rope tightly). It is as if the person speaking such words intends to forcibly twist the opponent away from his thoughts.

Some have also said that the root of "jidal" means wrestling and throwing another to the ground, which is also used in verbal disputes. In any case, the reference to "man" here is to unrefined and uneducated human beings; similar instances are frequent in the Qur'an. We have provided a detailed discussion on this subject under verse 12 of Surah Yunus (Tafsir Nemouneh, Volume 8, page 239).

The next verse says that despite all these various examples, shocking statements, and diverse logics that should penetrate any ready human being, a large group of people still did not believe: "And nothing prevented the people from believing when guidance came to them and from asking forgiveness of their Lord except that there [should] befall them the precedent of the former peoples" (وَ ما مَنَعَ النَّاسَ أَنْ يُؤْمِنُوا إِذْ جاءَهُمُ الْهُدى‌ وَ يَسْتَغْفِرُوا رَبَّهُمْ إِلَّا أَنْ تَأْتِيَهُمْ سُنَّةُ الْأَوَّلِينَ‌).

"Or that the punishment should come [directly] before them" so they see it with their own eyes (أَوْ يَأْتِيَهُمُ الْعَذابُ قُبُلًا) [1]. In reality, this verse indicates that this stubborn and arrogant group will never believe of their own will and volition. They will only believe in two situations:

First, when the painful punishments that encompassed previous nations overtake them. Second, that they at least witness the divine punishment with their own eyes—though this forced faith will, of course, be worthless.

It is also necessary to note that such misguided nations were never actually "waiting" for such a fate. However, since this fate was certain for them, the Qur'an describes it as an expectation. This is a beautiful form of metaphor, just as we might say to a rebellious individual, "You only want to be punished," meaning that punishment is your certain destiny, such that it is as if you are waiting for it.

In any case, the state of a rebellious and arrogant person sometimes reaches a point where neither heavenly revelation, nor the continuous preaching of the Prophets, nor observing the lessons of social life, nor studying the history of the predecessors has any effect on him. Only the "rod of God" can bring him to his senses; but what use is it, for when the punishment descends, the doors of repentance are closed, and there is no way to return or seek forgiveness.

Then, to comfort the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in the face of the persistence and stubbornness of the opponents, He says: "Your duty is only to bring glad tidings and warnings; We do not send the messengers except as bringers of good tidings and warners" (وَ ما نُرْسِلُ الْمُرْسَلِينَ إِلَّا مُبَشِّرِينَ وَ مُنْذِرِينَ‌).

He then adds that it is not a new matter for such individuals to rise in opposition and mockery; rather, "those who disbelieve dispute by [using] falsehood to refute thereby the Truth and have taken My signs and what they are warned of in ridicule" (وَ يُجادِلُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِالْباطِلِ لِيُدْحِضُوا بِهِ الْحَقَّ وَ اتَّخَذُوا آياتِي وَ ما أُنْذِرُوا هُزُواً) [2]. In fact, this verse is similar to verses 42 to 45 of Surah Al-Hajj, which say: "And if they deny you, [know that] already before them the people of Noah and 'Aad and Thamud denied [their prophets]..."

There is also the possibility in the interpretation of the verse that Allah is saying the work of the Prophets is not coercion or compulsion; rather, their duty is glad tidings and warning. However, the final decision rests with the people themselves, so they may think correctly, observe the consequences of disbelief and faith, and believe out of will and decision—not that they see the divine punishment before their eyes and profess faith out of necessity.

But unfortunately, this freedom and choice, which is a means of perfection, has often been misused. The partisans of falsehood have risen to dispute against the Truth; sometimes through fallacy and sometimes through mockery, they have sought to eliminate the true religion. Yet, there have always been ready hearts to accept the Truth and rise in its support. This struggle between Truth and Falsehood has existed throughout history and continues still.

[1]: "Qubulan" (قُبُلًا) means confrontation; that is, they see the divine punishment right in front of them. A group of commentators, such as Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan, Abu al-Futuh in Rawh al-Jinan, and Alusi in Rawh al-Ma'ani, have also suggested the possibility that "qubul" is the plural of "qabil," referring to various types of punishment, but the first meaning seems more correct.

[2]: "Yud-hidu" (يُدْحِضُوا) is from the root "id-had," meaning to nullify and eliminate, and is originally derived from "dahd," meaning to slip or slide.

Ayahs

وَمَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّن ذُكِّرَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ رَبِّهِۦ فَأَعْرَضَ عَنْهَا وَنَسِىَ مَا قَدَّمَتْ يَدَاهُ ۚ إِنَّا جَعَلْنَا عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ أَكِنَّةً أَن يَفْقَهُوهُ وَفِىٓ ءَاذَانِهِمْ وَقْرًا ۖ وَإِن تَدْعُهُمْ إِلَى ٱلْهُدَىٰ فَلَن يَهْتَدُوٓا۟ إِذًا أَبَدًا (٥٧)
Who is a greater wrongdoer than he who is reminded of the signs of his Lord, whereat he disregards them and forgets what his hands have sent ahead? Indeed We have cast veils on their hearts lest they should understand it, and a deafness into their ears; and if you invite them to guidance they will never [let themselves] be guided. (57)
وَرَبُّكَ ٱلْغَفُورُ ذُو ٱلرَّحْمَةِ ۖ لَوْ يُؤَاخِذُهُم بِمَا كَسَبُوا۟ لَعَجَّلَ لَهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابَ ۚ بَل لَّهُم مَّوْعِدٌ لَّن يَجِدُوا۟ مِن دُونِهِۦ مَوْئِلًا (٥٨)
Your Lord is the All-forgiving dispenser of mercy. Were He to take them to task because of what they have committed, He would have surely hastened their punishment. But they have a tryst, [when] they will not find a refuge besides Him. (58)
وَتِلْكَ ٱلْقُرَىٰٓ أَهْلَكْنَـٰهُمْ لَمَّا ظَلَمُوا۟ وَجَعَلْنَا لِمَهْلِكِهِم مَّوْعِدًا (٥٩)
Those are the towns that We destroyed when they were wrongdoers, and We appointed a tryst for their destruction. (59)

Commentary

Divine Punishment is Not Hastened

Since the preceding verses discussed a group of dark-hearted and prejudiced disbelievers, the above verses continue that same discussion.

First, it says: "And who is more unjust than he who is reminded of the signs of his Lord, yet turns away from them and forgets what his hands have sent forth?" (وَ مَنْ أَظْلَمُ مِمَّنْ ذُكِّرَ بِآياتِ رَبِّهِ فَأَعْرَضَ عَنْها وَ نَسِيَ ما قَدَّمَتْ يَداهُ‌).

The use of the term tadhkir (reminding) seems to indicate that the teachings of the Prophets are a form of reminding humans of truths that exist instinctively in the depths of the human soul, and the task of the Prophets is to lift the veil from them.

This meaning is also found in the first sermon of Nahj al-Balagha, where it says:

ليستادوهم ميثاق فطرته و يذكروهم منسى نعمته و يحتجوا اليهم بالتبليغ و يثيروا لهم دفائن العقول:

"The purpose of the mission of the Prophets was to urge people to fulfill the covenant of their primordial nature (fitrah), to remind them of God's forgotten blessings, to establish the proof against them through preaching, and to uncover for them the hidden treasures of the intellects!"

Interestingly, in this verse, He gives a lesson of awakening to these blind-hearted individuals through three avenues:

First, that these truths are completely familiar to your nature, conscience, and soul; second, that they have come from your own Lord; and third, do not forget that you have committed errors which the program of the Prophets is designed to wash away.

Yet, despite all this, this group will never believe, "For We have placed veils over their hearts lest they understand it, and heaviness in their ears [so they do not hear the voice of truth]" (إِنَّا جَعَلْنا عَلى‌ قُلُوبِهِمْ أَكِنَّةً أَنْ يَفْقَهُوهُ وَ فِي آذانِهِمْ وَقْراً). [^1] "And so, if you call them to guidance, they will never be guided" (وَ إِنْ تَدْعُهُمْ إِلَى الْهُدى‌ فَلَنْ يَهْتَدُوا إِذاً أَبَداً).

It perhaps needs no reminding that if Allah has taken away their sense of discernment and their power of understanding and hearing, it is because of "what their hands have sent forth" and the deeds they previously committed. This punishment is the direct effect of their own actions; or rather, in another sense, those very ugly and shameful deeds have turned into "veils" and "heaviness" (akinnah and waqr) upon their hearts and ears. This is a reality spoken of in many verses of the Quran; for example, in Surah An-Nisa, verse 155, we read: (بَلْ طَبَعَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْها بِكُفْرِهِمْ فَلا يُؤْمِنُونَ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا): "Nay, Allah has set a seal upon them because of their disbelief, so they believe not, except for a few."

However, those who exploit every excuse to prove the school of determinism (jabr)—without considering the other phrases of this verse or placing other verses of the Quran that interpret it alongside it—have clung to the literal appearance of the above expression and used it to support their school of thought, while the answer to it, as we have said, is perfectly clear.

[^1]: As we have stated previously, "akinnah" is the plural of "kinan" (on the pattern of kitab), meaning a veil or anything that conceals, and "waqr" means heaviness of the ear.

And since Allah's educational program for His servants is such that He gives them opportunity until the final stage and never immediately proceeds to punishment like the tyrants of the age, but rather His "vast mercy" always necessitates giving the maximum opportunity to sinners, He says in the next verse: "And your Lord is the Forgiving, Full of Mercy" (وَ رَبُّكَ الْغَفُورُ ذُو الرَّحْمَةِ).

"If He were to call them to account for what they have earned, He would have hastened the punishment for them" (لَوْ يُؤاخِذُهُمْ بِما كَسَبُوا لَعَجَّلَ لَهُمُ الْعَذابَ‌).

"But for them is an appointed time from which they will find no escape" (بَلْ لَهُمْ مَوْعِدٌ لَنْ يَجِدُوا مِنْ دُونِهِ مَوْئِلًا). [^1] His forgiveness necessitates that He forgives the repentant, and His mercy requires that He does not hasten the punishment of others either, so that they might perhaps join the ranks of the repentant. However, His justice also requires that when rebellion and transgression reach the ultimate degree, He settles their account. Fundamentally, the survival of such corrupt and corrupting individuals, for whose reform there is no hope, has no meaning in terms of the wisdom of creation; they must be destroyed, and the earth must be cleansed of the filth of their existence.

Finally, for a final reminder and warning in this series of verses, He recalls the bitter and painful fate of previous oppressors, saying: "And these are the townships whose ruins are before your eyes; We destroyed them when they committed injustice, and yet We did not hasten their punishment, but rather appointed a time for their destruction" (وَ تِلْكَ الْقُرى‌ أَهْلَكْناهُمْ لَمَّا ظَلَمُوا وَ جَعَلْنا لِمَهْلِكِهِمْ مَوْعِداً).

[^1]: "Maw'il" is from the root "wa'ala" (on the pattern of sarv), meaning a refuge, a sanctuary, and a means of salvation.

Ayahs

وَإِذْ قَالَ مُوسَىٰ لِفَتَىٰهُ لَآ أَبْرَحُ حَتَّىٰٓ أَبْلُغَ مَجْمَعَ ٱلْبَحْرَيْنِ أَوْ أَمْضِىَ حُقُبًا (٦٠)
When Moses said to his lad, ‘I will go on [journeying] until I have reached the confluence of the two seas, or have spent a long time [travelling].’ (60)
فَلَمَّا بَلَغَا مَجْمَعَ بَيْنِهِمَا نَسِيَا حُوتَهُمَا فَٱتَّخَذَ سَبِيلَهُۥ فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ سَرَبًا (٦١)
So when they reached the confluence between them, they forgot their fish, which found its way into the sea, sneaking away. (61)
فَلَمَّا جَاوَزَا قَالَ لِفَتَىٰهُ ءَاتِنَا غَدَآءَنَا لَقَدْ لَقِينَا مِن سَفَرِنَا هَـٰذَا نَصَبًا (٦٢)
So when they had passed on, he said to his lad, ‘Bring us our meal. We have certainly encountered much fatigue on this journey of ours.’ (62)
قَالَ أَرَءَيْتَ إِذْ أَوَيْنَآ إِلَى ٱلصَّخْرَةِ فَإِنِّى نَسِيتُ ٱلْحُوتَ وَمَآ أَنسَىٰنِيهُ إِلَّا ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ أَنْ أَذْكُرَهُۥ ۚ وَٱتَّخَذَ سَبِيلَهُۥ فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ عَجَبًا (٦٣)
He said, ‘Did you see?! When we took shelter at the rock, indeed I forgot about the fish—and none but Satan made me forget to mention it!—and it made its way into the sea in an amazing manner!’ (63)
قَالَ ذَٰلِكَ مَا كُنَّا نَبْغِ ۚ فَٱرْتَدَّا عَلَىٰٓ ءَاثَارِهِمَا قَصَصًا (٦٤)
He said, ‘That is what we were after!’ So they returned, retracing their footsteps. (64)

Commentary

The Amazing Story of Khidr and Moses

Commentators have narrated regarding the occasion of revelation for the above verses that a group from the Quraysh came to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and asked about the scholar whom Moses (عليه السلام) was commanded to follow; subsequently, these verses were revealed.

Fundamentally, there are three accounts in this Surah (Surah al-Kahf) that are harmonious from one perspective: the story of the People of the Cave (Ashab al-Kahf) mentioned previously, the story of Moses and Khidr, and the story of Dhul-Qarnayn which follows.

All three accounts take us beyond the horizon of our limited lives—that which we are accustomed to—and demonstrate that neither is the world limited to what we see, nor is the true face of events always what we perceive at first encounter.

In any case, the story of the People of the Cave spoke of chivalrous youths who turned their backs on everything to preserve their faith, eventually attaining such a wondrous life that became a lesson for all people. In the account of Moses and Khidr—or in other words, the scholar and sage of his time—we encounter an amazing scene showing that even an Arch-Prophet (Ulul-'Azm), who is the most knowledgeable person in his environment, still finds the scope of his knowledge limited in certain aspects. He seeks out a teacher to learn from, who teaches him lessons, each more wondrous than the last. Indeed, many profound points are hidden within the collective details of this story.

In the first verse, it says: "And remember when Moses said to his young companion, 'I will not give up until I reach the junction of the two seas, even if I travel for a long period'" (وَ إِذْ قالَ مُوسى‌ لِفَتاهُ لا أَبْرَحُ حَتَّى أَبْلُغَ مَجْمَعَ الْبَحْرَيْنِ أَوْ أَمْضِيَ حُقُباً).

The "Moses" mentioned in the above verse is undoubtedly the famous Arch-Prophet Moses son of Imran, although some commentators have suggested the possibility of it being another Moses. We will later mention that this possibility arose primarily because they could not resolve some of the difficulties present in this story, thus necessitating the assumption of another Moses. However, wherever the Quran speaks of Moses, it refers to Moses son of Imran.

The term "his young companion" (fatahu) here, according to many commentators and numerous narrations, refers to "Joshua son of Nun" (Yusha' ibn Nun), the brave, gallant, and faithful man of the Israelites. The expression "youth" (fata) may be due to these outstanding qualities, or because of his service to Moses and his companionship and journeying with him.

"The junction of the two seas" (Majma' al-Bahrayn) means the place where two seas meet. There is discussion among commentators as to which two seas are referred to, and overall, three well-known opinions exist:

  1. It refers to the meeting point of the Gulf of Aqaba and the Gulf of Suez (we know that the Red Sea has two extensions in the north: one toward the northeast and the other toward the northwest; the first forms the Gulf of Aqaba and the second the Gulf of Suez, and these two gulfs meet in the southern part and connect to the Red Sea).

  2. It refers to the meeting point of the Indian Ocean and the Red Sea, which join at the Bab al-Mandab Strait.

  3. The meeting point of the Mediterranean Sea (also known as the Sea of Rome or the White Sea) and the Atlantic Ocean, namely the Strait of Gibraltar near the city of Tangier.

However, the third interpretation seems very unlikely, as the distance between Moses' residence and Gibraltar is so great that traveling there by the normal means of that time might have taken months.

The second possibility, while involving a shorter distance, is still significant in its own right, as there is a relatively large distance from Levant (Sham) to southern Yemen.

But the first possibility, which has the shortest distance to Moses' place of residence (the path from Levant to the Gulf of Aqaba is not long), seems the most plausible of all interpretations. This is because it can be generally inferred from the verses that Moses did not travel an immense distance, even though he was prepared to travel anywhere to reach this goal (note carefully).

Some narrations also contain references to this meaning.

The word huqub means a long period, which some have interpreted as "eighty years." Moses' intention in mentioning this word was that he would not cease his effort and striving to find his objective, even if he had to continue this journey for years.

From the sum of what has been said above, it is clearly evident that Moses was seeking an important "lost goal" and was searching for it everywhere. He had made a firm resolve and a steadfast decision not to rest until he found his objective.

The goal Moses was commanded to find had a great impact on his destiny and opened a new chapter in his life.

Yes, he was looking for a learned man and scholar who could pull back the veils and curtains from before Moses' eyes, show him new truths, and open the doors of sciences and knowledge to him.

And we will soon see that he possessed a sign to find the location of this great scholar and was moving in pursuit of that sign.

"But when they reached the junction of the two [seas], they forgot their fish" (فَلَمَّا بَلَغا مَجْمَعَ بَيْنِهِما نَسِيا حُوتَهُما). "And it took its course into the sea, slipping away" (فَاتَّخَذَ سَبِيلَهُ فِي الْبَحْرِ سَرَباً) [1]. There is much discussion among commentators as to whether this fish—which they had apparently prepared as food—was roasted, salted, or a fresh fish that miraculously came to life, jumped into the water, and moved.

In some books of commentary, there is also mention of a "Spring of Life" in that region, and that some of its water splashed onto the fish, bringing it to life. However, the possibility also exists that the fish was not yet completely dead, as there are fish that remain in a semi-conscious state for a considerable time after being taken out of water, and if they fall into water during this time, they resume their normal life.

[1]: Sarab (with the weight of jarab), as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat, means walking on a downward slope, and sarab (with the weight of harab) means a downward path.

"Finally, Moses and his companion passed the junction of the two seas (Majma' al-Bahrayn). The length of the journey and the fatigue of the road caused hunger to overcome them. At this moment, Moses remembered that they had brought food with them. He said to his companion: 'Bring us our meal; we have certainly suffered much fatigue from this journey of ours'" (فَلَمَّا جاوَزا قالَ لِفَتاهُ آتِنا غَداءَنا لَقَدْ لَقِينا مِنْ سَفَرِنا هذا نَصَباً).

Ghada' refers to food eaten at the beginning of the day or the middle of the day (breakfast or lunch). However, from the expressions used in lexicons, it is inferred that in ancient times, ghada' was only used for food eaten at the beginning of the day (as it is derived from ghadwah, meaning the start of the day), whereas in modern Arabic, ghada' and taghadda mean lunch and eating lunch.

In any case, this sentence shows that Moses and Joshua traversed a path that qualified as a "journey," yet these expressions also indicate that this journey was not excessively long.

At this moment, "his companion informed him: 'Did you see when we took refuge by the rock (and rested)? I forgot to mention the fish there, and none but Satan made me forget to mention it; and it took its way into the sea in an amazing manner'" (قالَ أَ رَأَيْتَ إِذْ أَوَيْنا إِلَى الصَّخْرَةِ فَإِنِّي نَسِيتُ الْحُوتَ وَ ما أَنْسانِيهُ إِلَّا الشَّيْطانُ أَنْ أَذْكُرَهُ وَ اتَّخَذَ سَبِيلَهُ فِي الْبَحْرِ عَجَباً [1]).

[1]: The phrase "and none but Satan made me forget to mention it" is a parenthetical sentence occurring in the middle of the speech. Since this sentence actually explains the cause of the forgetfulness, it is mentioned in the middle of the speech. This is common, especially regarding those who are addressed or rebuked by a superior; they mention the primary cause within their speech as a parenthesis so they may face less objection.

And since this matter served as a sign for Moses regarding finding that great scholar: "Moses said: 'That is what we were seeking'" and looking for (قالَ ذلِكَ ما كُنَّا نَبْغِ‌).

"And at that moment, they retraced their footsteps, following the path back" (فارْتَدَّا عَلى‌ آثارِهِما قَصَصاً).

Here a question arises: Is it possible for a Prophet like Moses to be afflicted with forgetfulness, such that the Quran says "they forgot their fish" (nasiya hutahuma)? Furthermore, why does Moses' companion attribute his own forgetfulness to Satan?

The answer is that there is no obstacle to a Prophet experiencing forgetfulness in matters that have no connection to divine laws and missionary duties—that is, in ordinary matters of daily life (especially in a case intended as a trial, as has been said regarding Moses here, the details of which we will explain later).

As for attributing the forgetfulness of his companion to Satan, it may be because the incident of the fish was connected to finding that learned man. Since Satan is a deceiver, he wanted to delay their meeting with that scholar through this act. Perhaps the preliminaries of this also began with Joshua himself, who did not exercise the necessary care and attention in this regard.

Ayahs

فَوَجَدَا عَبْدًا مِّنْ عِبَادِنَآ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ رَحْمَةً مِّنْ عِندِنَا وَعَلَّمْنَـٰهُ مِن لَّدُنَّا عِلْمًا (٦٥)
[There] they found one of Our servants whom We had granted a mercy from Ourselves, and taught him a knowledge from Our own. (65)
قَالَ لَهُۥ مُوسَىٰ هَلْ أَتَّبِعُكَ عَلَىٰٓ أَن تُعَلِّمَنِ مِمَّا عُلِّمْتَ رُشْدًا (٦٦)
Moses said to him, ‘May I follow you for the purpose that you teach me some of the probity you have been taught?’ (66)
قَالَ إِنَّكَ لَن تَسْتَطِيعَ مَعِىَ صَبْرًا (٦٧)
He said, ‘Indeed you cannot have patience with me! (67)
وَكَيْفَ تَصْبِرُ عَلَىٰ مَا لَمْ تُحِطْ بِهِۦ خُبْرًا (٦٨)
And how can you have patience about something you do not comprehend?’ (68)
قَالَ سَتَجِدُنِىٓ إِن شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ صَابِرًا وَلَآ أَعْصِى لَكَ أَمْرًا (٦٩)
He said, ‘You will find me, God willing, to be patient, and I will not disobey you in any matter.’ (69)
قَالَ فَإِنِ ٱتَّبَعْتَنِى فَلَا تَسْـَٔلْنِى عَن شَىْءٍ حَتَّىٰٓ أُحْدِثَ لَكَ مِنْهُ ذِكْرًا (٧٠)
He said, ‘If you follow me, do not question me concerning anything until I myself first mention it for you.’ (70)

Commentary

Meeting the Great Teacher

When Moses and his traveling companion returned to the first place—that is, beside the rock and near the "junction of the two seas"—"they found a servant from among Our servants, whom We had granted mercy from Us and whom We had taught knowledge from Our presence" (فَوَجَدا عَبْداً مِنْ عِبادِنا آتَيْناهُ رَحْمَةً مِنْ عِنْدِنا وَ عَلَّمْناهُ مِنْ لَدُنَّا عِلْماً).

The expression "they found" (وجدا) indicates that they were searching for this learned man and finally found what they were looking for.

The expression "a servant from among Our servants" (عَبْداً مِنْ عِبادِنا) shows that the highest honor for a human being is to be a true servant of Allah, and it is this station of servitude (ubudiyyah) that makes a person eligible for Divine mercy and opens the gateways of knowledge to his heart.

The expression "from Our presence" (مِنْ لَدُنَّا) also indicates that the knowledge of that scholar was not ordinary knowledge, but rather an awareness of a portion of the secrets of this world and the mysteries of events that only Allah knows.

The use of the word "knowledge" (علماً) in its indefinite form (nakirah), which in such cases usually denotes greatness and magnificence, shows that the learned man had attained a significant portion of this knowledge.

Regarding what is meant by "mercy from Us" (رَحْمَةً مِنْ عِنْدِنا) in the above verse, commentators have mentioned various interpretations: some have interpreted it as the station of prophethood, and others as a long life. However, the possibility also exists that it refers to the remarkable talent, vast soul, and expansion of the chest (sharh al-sadr) that Allah had granted that man so he could receive Divine knowledge.

Whether this learned man's name was "Khidr" and whether he was a prophet or not will be discussed in the "Key Points" section.

At this moment, Moses, with the utmost politeness and in the form of a question, "said to him: May I follow you so that you may teach me of what you have been taught of guidance and rectitude?" (قالَ لَهُ مُوسى‌ هَلْ أَتَّبِعُكَ عَلى‌ أَنْ تُعَلِّمَنِ مِمَّا عُلِّمْتَ رُشْداً). From the expression "rectitude" (رشداً), it is inferred that knowledge is not the ultimate goal, but rather a means to find the path to the objective and to reach goodness and righteousness. Such knowledge is valuable, must be learned from a master, and is a source of pride.

But to his great surprise, the learned man "said to Moses: Indeed, you will never be able to have patience with me" (قالَ إِنَّكَ لَنْ تَسْتَطِيعَ مَعِيَ صَبْراً).

And he immediately stated the reason for it, saying: "And how can you be patient about that which you do not encompass in knowledge?" (وَ كَيْفَ تَصْبِرُ عَلى‌ ما لَمْ تُحِطْ بِهِ خُبْراً). As we shall see later, this learned man had mastery over branches of knowledge related to the inner secrets and the depths of events and phenomena, whereas Moses was neither commanded regarding the inner reality (batin) nor did he have much knowledge of it.

In such cases, it often happens that the outward appearance of events differs from what lies within their inner reality. It may be that the appearance is very unpleasant or seemingly foolish, while in reality, it is very sacred, calculated, and logical.

In such a situation, one who sees only the outward appearance loses the reins of patience and self-control and rises in protest and sometimes in aggression.

However, the master who is aware of the inner secrets and looks at the inner face of things continues his work with composure. He does not listen to the protests and shouts of the student; rather, he waits for a suitable opportunity to reveal the truth of the matter. Meanwhile, the student remains restless, but once the secrets are revealed to him, he becomes completely calm.

Upon hearing this, Moses perhaps became worried and feared that the grace of this great scholar's presence would be cut off from him. Therefore, he gave a commitment to be patient in the face of all events and "said: You will find me, if Allah wills, patient, and I will not disobey you in any matter" (قالَ سَتَجِدُنِي إِنْ شاءَ اللَّهُ صابِراً وَ لا أَعْصِي لَكَ أَمْراً). Once again, Moses reveals his utmost politeness in this phrase; he relies on the will of Allah. He does not say to the learned man "I am patient," but rather says, "If Allah wills, you will find me patient."
