However, since patience in the face of seemingly unpleasant events whose secrets one does not know is not an easy task, the learned man once again took a commitment from Moses and warned him: "He said: Then if you follow me, do not ask me about anything until I myself mention it to you!" (قالَ فَإِنِ اتَّبَعْتَنِي فَلا تَسْئَلْنِي عَنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ حَتَّى أُحْدِثَ لَكَ مِنْهُ ذِكْراً) [1]. Moses gave this renewed commitment and set out in the company of this master.

[1]: The expression "until I myself mention it to you" (أُحْدِثَ لَكَ مِنْهُ ذِكْراً), considering the word "uḥditha" (to initiate/bring about), means: I will initiate the speech myself and unveil the matter for the first time; you must say nothing.

Ayahs

فَٱنطَلَقَا حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا رَكِبَا فِى ٱلسَّفِينَةِ خَرَقَهَا ۖ قَالَ أَخَرَقْتَهَا لِتُغْرِقَ أَهْلَهَا لَقَدْ جِئْتَ شَيْـًٔا إِمْرًا (٧١)
So they went on and when they boarded the boat, he made a hole in it. He said, ‘Did you make a hole in it to drown its people? You have certainly done a monstrous thing!’ (71)
قَالَ أَلَمْ أَقُلْ إِنَّكَ لَن تَسْتَطِيعَ مَعِىَ صَبْرًا (٧٢)
He said, ‘Did I not say that you cannot have patience with me?’ (72)
قَالَ لَا تُؤَاخِذْنِى بِمَا نَسِيتُ وَلَا تُرْهِقْنِى مِنْ أَمْرِى عُسْرًا (٧٣)
He said, ‘Do not take me to task for my forgetting, and do not be hard upon me.’ (73)
فَٱنطَلَقَا حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا لَقِيَا غُلَـٰمًا فَقَتَلَهُۥ قَالَ أَقَتَلْتَ نَفْسًا زَكِيَّةًۢ بِغَيْرِ نَفْسٍ لَّقَدْ جِئْتَ شَيْـًٔا نُّكْرًا (٧٤)
So they went on until they came upon a boy, whereat he slew him. He said, ‘Did you slay an innocent soul, without [his having slain] anyone? You have certainly done a dire thing!’ (74)
۞ قَالَ أَلَمْ أَقُل لَّكَ إِنَّكَ لَن تَسْتَطِيعَ مَعِىَ صَبْرًا (٧٥)
He said, ‘Did I not tell you that you cannot have patience with me?’ (75)
قَالَ إِن سَأَلْتُكَ عَن شَىْءٍۭ بَعْدَهَا فَلَا تُصَـٰحِبْنِى ۖ قَدْ بَلَغْتَ مِن لَّدُنِّى عُذْرًا (٧٦)
He said, ‘If I question you about anything after this, do not keep me in your company. You already have enough excuse on my part.’ (76)
فَٱنطَلَقَا حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَآ أَتَيَآ أَهْلَ قَرْيَةٍ ٱسْتَطْعَمَآ أَهْلَهَا فَأَبَوْا۟ أَن يُضَيِّفُوهُمَا فَوَجَدَا فِيهَا جِدَارًا يُرِيدُ أَن يَنقَضَّ فَأَقَامَهُۥ ۖ قَالَ لَوْ شِئْتَ لَتَّخَذْتَ عَلَيْهِ أَجْرًا (٧٧)
So they went on until they came to the people of a town. They asked its people for food, but they refused to extend them any hospitality. There they found a wall which was about to collapse, so he erected it. He said, ‘Had you wished, you could have taken a wage for it.’ (77)
قَالَ هَـٰذَا فِرَاقُ بَيْنِى وَبَيْنِكَ ۚ سَأُنَبِّئُكَ بِتَأْوِيلِ مَا لَمْ تَسْتَطِع عَّلَيْهِ صَبْرًا (٧٨)
He said, ‘This is where you and I shall part. I will inform you about the interpretation of that over which you could not maintain patience. (78)

Commentary

A Divine Teacher and These Reprehensible Acts?!

Yes, "Moses set out accompanied by this divinely learned man until they embarked upon a ship" (فَانْطَلَقا حَتَّى إِذا رَكِبا فِي السَّفِينَةِ).

From here on, we observe that the Quran uses the dual pronoun in all instances, referring to Moses and that learned man. This indicates that the mission of Moses' companion, Joshua, ended there and he returned, or perhaps he was disregarded because he was not a central figure in this specific event, even if he were present. However, the first possibility seems stronger.

In any case, when the two embarked on the ship, "that learned man made a hole in the ship!" (خَرَقَها).

"Kharaq"—as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat—means tearing something apart out of corruption, without prior study or thought; and indeed, the outward appearance of this learned man's action seemed exactly like that.

Since Moses was, on one hand, a great Prophet of Allah and was duty-bound to protect the lives and property of the people and to enjoin good and forbid evil, and on the other hand, his human conscience would not allow him to remain silent in the face of such a wrong act, he forgot the commitment he had made to Khidr and spoke out in protest, "saying: 'Have you made a hole in it to drown its folk? Verily, you have done a dreadful thing!'" (قالَ أَ خَرَقْتَها لِتُغْرِقَ أَهْلَها لَقَدْ جِئْتَ شَيْئاً إِمْراً).

Undoubtedly, the learned man's goal was not to drown the passengers, but since the result of this action appeared to be nothing but drowning, Moses expressed it using the "Lam of consequence" (Lam al-Ghayah), which is used to state an objective.

This is exactly like when a person overeats excessively and we say, "Do you want to kill yourself?!" Certainly, he has no such intention, but the result of his action may be just that.

"Imr" (rhyming with Shimr) refers to a significant, astonishing, or very ugly matter.

And truly, the outward appearance of this act was astonishing and very bad; what act could be more dangerous than making a hole in a ship carrying numerous passengers?!

In some narrations, we read that the people on the ship soon noticed the danger and temporarily filled the gap with something, but that ship was no longer a sound vessel.

At this moment, the divinely learned man, with his characteristic composure, looked at Moses and "said: 'Did I not say that you could never have patience with me?'" (قالَ أَ لَمْ أَقُلْ إِنَّكَ لَنْ تَسْتَطِيعَ مَعِيَ صَبْراً).

Moses, who regretted his haste—which was naturally due to the gravity of the incident—and remembered his commitment, sought to apologize. Turning to the teacher, he "said: 'Call me not to account for what I forgot, and be not hard upon me for my fault'" (قالَ لا تُؤاخِذْنِي بِما نَسِيتُ وَ لا تُرْهِقْنِي مِنْ أَمْرِي عُسْراً). Meaning: it was a mistake, and whatever happened has passed; please overlook it with your nobility.

"La turhiqni" is from the root "irhaq," meaning to cover something by force or coercion, and sometimes it means to impose a task. In the above sentence, the meaning is: do not be hard on me, do not put me to trouble, and do not cut off your grace from me because of this act!

Their sea journey ended and they disembarked from the ship, "and they journeyed on until they met a lad, and he [the learned man] slew him!" (فَانْطَلَقا حَتَّى إِذا لَقِيا غُلاماً فَقَتَلَهُ).

Here, once again, Moses lost his composure. The horrific sight of killing an innocent child, and that too without any justification, was not something Moses could remain silent about. The fire of anger was kindled in his heart, and it was as if a veil of sorrow and dissatisfaction covered his eyes, such that he forgot his commitment once more. He spoke out in protest—a protest more intense and resonant than the first, because the incident was more horrific than the first—and "said: 'Have you slain an innocent and pure soul who has slain no one?!'" (قالَ أَ قَتَلْتَ نَفْساً زَكِيَّةً بِغَيْرِ نَفْسٍ).

"Verily, you have done a most wicked and horrible thing!" (لَقَدْ جِئْتَ شَيْئاً نُكْراً).

The word "ghulam" means a young youth, whether he has reached the age of maturity or not.

Regarding whether the youth whom the learned man killed here had reached the age of maturity or not, there is discussion among commentators. Some took the expression "nafsa zakiyyah" (a pure and innocent soul) as evidence that he was not mature.

Others took the expression "bi-ghayri nafsin" (without [having slain] a soul) as evidence that he was mature, because retaliation (qisas) is only permissible regarding a mature person. However, on the whole, one cannot judge with certainty in this regard based solely on the verse itself.

"Nukr" means ugly and abominable, and its resonance is stronger than the word "imr" used in the incident of making a hole in the ship. The reason for this is clear: the first act created a potential danger for a group who soon noticed and averted the danger, but in the second act, he had apparently committed a crime.

Again, that noble scholar, with the same special coolness, repeated the previous sentence and said: "Did I not say to you that you could never have patience with me?" (قالَ أَ لَمْ أَقُلْ لَكَ إِنَّكَ لَنْ تَسْتَطِيعَ مَعِيَ صَبْراً).

The only difference from the previous sentence is the addition of the word "laka" (to you), which is for further emphasis, meaning: I said this specifically to you.

Moses (peace be upon him) remembered his covenant—a realization accompanied by shame, for he had broken his promise twice, albeit out of forgetfulness. He gradually felt that the teacher's words might be true and that his actions were initially unbearable for Moses. Therefore, he once again began to apologize and said:

This time too, overlook my fault and ignore my forgetfulness, but "if I ask you about anything after this, keep me not in your company; indeed, you shall then have found an excuse in my case" (قالَ إِنْ سَأَلْتُكَ عَنْ شَيْ‌ءٍ بَعْدَها فَلا تُصاحِبْنِي قَدْ بَلَغْتَ مِنْ لَدُنِّي عُذْراً).

This sentence reflects the ultimate fairness and foresight of Moses, showing that he submitted to a reality, however bitter. In other words, after three trials, it would become clear to him that the missions of these two great men were separate, and as the saying goes, their waters do not flow in the same stream!

After this conversation and renewed commitment, "Moses set out with the teacher until they reached a village and asked its people for food, but they refused to entertain them as guests" (فَانْطَلَقا حَتَّى إِذا أَتَيا أَهْلَ قَرْيَةٍ اسْتَطْعَما أَهْلَها فَأَبَوْا أَنْ يُضَيِّفُوهُما).

Undoubtedly, Moses and Khidr were not the type of people who wanted to be a burden on the people of that land, but it appears they had lost or exhausted their provisions and travel expenses along the way, and for this reason, they wished to be guests of the local inhabitants. (There is also the possibility that the learned man intentionally made this proposal to them to teach Moses a new lesson).

It is also necessary to mention that "qaryah" in the language of the Quran has a general meaning and includes any kind of town or settlement, but here it specifically means a city, because a few verses later it is referred to as "al-madinah" (the city).

In any case, as to which city this was and where it was located, there is discussion among commentators: it is narrated from Ibn Abbas that it refers to "Antioch." [^1] Others have said it refers to Ayla, which is known today as the port of Eilat, located on the Red Sea near the Gulf of Aqaba.

Others believe it refers to the city of "Nazareth," located in northern Palestine, which was the birthplace of Prophet Jesus (peace be upon him).

The late Tabarsi narrates a hadith here from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) which supports the latter possibility.

Given what we said regarding the meaning of "Majma' al-Bahrayn" (the junction of the two seas)—that it refers to the meeting point of the "Gulf of Aqaba" and the "Gulf of Suez"—it becomes clear that the city of Nazareth and the port of Ayla are closer to this region than Antioch.

[^1]: "Antioch" is one of the ancient cities of Syria, 96 km from Aleppo and 59 km from Alexandretta, famous for its food grains and oilseeds. It has a port called Suwaydiyyah, 27 km away (Encyclopedia of Farid Wajdi, Vol. 1, p. 835).

In any case, from what happened to Moses and his teacher in this village, we understand that its inhabitants were stingy and base-minded. Thus, in a narration from the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), we read that he said regarding them: "They were the people of a village of ignoble ones" (kanu ahla qaryatin li'am). [^1] Then the Quran adds: "Yet they found therein a wall which was on the point of falling, so he [the learned man] set it up" and prevented its ruin (فَوَجَدا فِيها جِداراً يُرِيدُ أَنْ يَنْقَضَّ فَأَقامَهُ). [^2] Moses, who was presumably exhausted and hungry at that time, and most importantly, felt that his and his teacher's high status had been severely wounded by the improper behavior of the villagers, observed Khidr repairing a falling wall in the face of this disrespect, as if he wanted to reward them for their bad behavior. He thought that at the very least, it would have been good for the teacher to perform this work for a wage so that food could be procured.

Therefore, he completely forgot his commitment once more and spoke out in protest, but a protest milder and gentler than before, "saying: 'If you had wished, you could have taken a wage for it!'" (قالَ لَوْ شِئْتَ لَاتَّخَذْتَ عَلَيْهِ أَجْراً).

In reality, Moses thought it was far from justice for a person to make such a sacrifice for people who were so base; or in other words, kindness is good, but in its proper place.

It is true that returning good for evil has been the way of the men of God,

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse in question.

[^2]: Attributing "will" (iradah) to a "wall" (jidar) is certainly metaphorical, meaning it had become so weak that it was as if it had decided to fall.

but only where it does not encourage the evildoer in their wrongdoings.

It was here that the learned man spoke the final words to Moses, for from the sum of the past events, he became certain that Moses did not have the capacity to endure his actions. "He said: 'This is the parting between me and you! But I will soon tell you the interpretation of that which you could not find patience for'" (قالَ هذا فِراقُ بَيْنِي وَ بَيْنِكَ سَأُنَبِّئُكَ بِتَأْوِيلِ ما لَمْ تَسْتَطِعْ عَلَيْهِ صَبْراً).

Of course, Moses did not raise any objection to this statement, for it was exactly what he himself had proposed in the previous incident. That is, the reality had been proven to Moses himself that their waters do not flow in the same stream.

But in any case, the news of parting was like a sledgehammer blow to Moses' heart—parting from a teacher whose chest was a storehouse of secrets, whose companionship was a source of blessing, whose words were lessons, and whose behavior was inspiring. The light of Allah shone on his forehead, and the center of his heart was a treasury of divine knowledge.

Yes, separating from such a leader is very painful, but it was a bitter reality that Moses had to accept regardless.

The famous commentator Abu al-Futuh Razi says there is a report that Moses was asked to name the hardest of the difficulties he faced in his life. He said: "I saw many hardships (referring to the troubles during Pharaoh's time and the exhausting difficulties during the rule over the Israelites), but none affected my heart like the words of Khidr when he announced the parting and separation!" [^1]

[^1]: Tafsir Abu al-Futuh Razi, under the verse in question.

"Ta'wil" is from the root "awl" (rhyming with qawl), meaning to refer or return something. Therefore, any action or word that we bring to its original goal is called ta'wil. Likewise, lifting the veil from the secrets of something is also a type of ta'wil.

And if the interpretation of dreams is called ta'wil, it is for this same reason (as mentioned in Surah Yusuf, verse 100: هذا تَأْوِيلُ رُءْيايَ). [^1]

[^1]: For further explanation, refer to Volume 2 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 324, under verse 7 of Surah Al-Imran.

Ayahs

أَمَّا ٱلسَّفِينَةُ فَكَانَتْ لِمَسَـٰكِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ فَأَرَدتُّ أَنْ أَعِيبَهَا وَكَانَ وَرَآءَهُم مَّلِكٌ يَأْخُذُ كُلَّ سَفِينَةٍ غَصْبًا (٧٩)
As for the boat, it belonged to some poor people who work on the sea. I wanted to make it defective, for behind them was a king seizing every ship usurpingly. (79)
وَأَمَّا ٱلْغُلَـٰمُ فَكَانَ أَبَوَاهُ مُؤْمِنَيْنِ فَخَشِينَآ أَن يُرْهِقَهُمَا طُغْيَـٰنًا وَكُفْرًا (٨٠)
As for the boy, his parents were faithful [persons], and We feared he would overwhelm them with rebellion and unfaith. (80)
فَأَرَدْنَآ أَن يُبْدِلَهُمَا رَبُّهُمَا خَيْرًا مِّنْهُ زَكَوٰةً وَأَقْرَبَ رُحْمًا (٨١)
So We desired that their Lord should give them in exchange one better than him in respect of purity and closer in mercy. (81)
وَأَمَّا ٱلْجِدَارُ فَكَانَ لِغُلَـٰمَيْنِ يَتِيمَيْنِ فِى ٱلْمَدِينَةِ وَكَانَ تَحْتَهُۥ كَنزٌ لَّهُمَا وَكَانَ أَبُوهُمَا صَـٰلِحًا فَأَرَادَ رَبُّكَ أَن يَبْلُغَآ أَشُدَّهُمَا وَيَسْتَخْرِجَا كَنزَهُمَا رَحْمَةً مِّن رَّبِّكَ ۚ وَمَا فَعَلْتُهُۥ عَنْ أَمْرِى ۚ ذَٰلِكَ تَأْوِيلُ مَا لَمْ تَسْطِع عَّلَيْهِ صَبْرًا (٨٢)
As for the wall, it belonged to two boy orphans in the city. Under it there was a treasure belonging to them. Their father had been a righteous man. So your Lord desired that they should come of age and take out their treasure—as a mercy from your Lord. I did not do that out of my own accord. This is the interpretation of that over which you could not maintain patience.’ (82)

Commentary

The Inner Secrets of These Events

Once the parting and separation of Musa and Khidr became certain, it was necessary for this Divine teacher to recount the secrets of his actions, which Musa did not have the capacity to endure. In reality, Musa’s benefit from his companionship was understanding the mystery of these three strange incidents, which could serve as a key to many issues and an answer to various questions.

He began first with the story of the ship and said: "As for the ship, it belonged to a group of poor people who worked with it at sea; I intended to damage it because I knew that behind them was a tyrannical king who seized every sound ship by force" (أَمَّا السَّفِينَةُ فَكانَتْ لِمَساكِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ فِي الْبَحْرِ فَأَرَدْتُ أَنْ أَعِيبَها وَ كانَ وَراءَهُمْ مَلِكٌ يَأْخُذُ كُلَّ سَفِينَةٍ غَصْباً).

Thus, behind the seemingly repulsive act of piercing the ship, there existed an important goal: saving it from the clutches of a usurping king. This was because the king would never see damaged ships as suitable for his purposes and would overlook them. In short, this act was carried out to preserve the interests of a group of poor people and had to be done.

The word "wara’" (behind) certainly does not have a spatial connotation here; rather, it is an allusion to the fact that they were falling into the clutches of such an oppressor without being aware of it. Since a person cannot see events behind them, this expression is used here.

Furthermore, when a person is under pressure from an individual or a group, they use the expression "behind," for example, saying "the creditors are behind me" and will not let me go. In verse 16 of Surah Ibrahim, we read: مِنْ وَرائِهِ جَهَنَّمُ وَ يُسْقى‌ مِنْ ماءٍ صَدِيدٍ—as if Hell is pursuing these sinners, hence the expression "wara’" is used. [1] Additionally, the use of the term "masakin" (the poor) in this context indicates that a "miskin" is not necessarily someone who owns absolutely nothing; rather, it can also refer to those who possess some property or wealth, but it does not suffice for their needs.

There is also the possibility that calling them "miskin" was not due to financial poverty but due to a lack of power. This usage exists in the Arabic language and is consistent with the original root of the word "miskin," which implies stillness, weakness, and inability.

In Nahj al-Balagha, we read: "Poor son of Adam! ... a gnat annoys him, a sip of water chokes him, and sweat makes him malodorous" [1].

Then, he proceeded to explain the secret of the second incident, namely the killing of the youth, saying: "As for the youth, his parents were believers, and we did not want this youth to lead his parents away from the path of faith and drive them to rebellion and disbelief" (وَ أَمَّا الْغُلامُ فَكانَ أَبَواهُ مُؤْمِنَيْنِ فَخَشِينا أَنْ يُرْهِقَهُما طُغْياناً وَ كُفْراً).

A group of commentators has also mentioned the possibility that the meaning is not that the disbelieving and rebellious youth would lead his parents astray, but rather that he would cause his parents great suffering and distress due to his rebellion and disbelief [2]. However, the first interpretation seems closer to the text.

In any case, that learned man proceeded to kill this youth and cited the unfortunate event that would have occurred in the future for the believing parents—assuming he lived—as the reason.

By Allah's will, we will soon discuss all of Khidr’s actions from the perspective of Divine and logical laws in the section detailing the points of this eventful story, and we will answer the objection regarding "retribution before the crime."

The expression "khashina" (we feared that in the future such would happen...) is a very meaningful expression. This phrasing shows that this learned man also felt responsible for the future of the people and was not willing to let believing parents fall into misery because of their youth's deviation.

Additionally, the expression "khashina" (we feared) is used here in the sense of "we disliked," because for such a person with this level of knowledge, awareness, and ability, fear of such matters does not exist.

In other words, the goal is to avoid an unfortunate event that a person, based on the principle of love, wishes to prevent.

There is also the possibility that it means "alimna" (we knew), as narrated from Ibn Abbas; meaning, we knew that if he remained, such an unfortunate event would happen to his parents in the future.

As for why the plural first-person pronoun is used for a single individual, the answer is clear. This is not the first time we encounter such an expression in the Quran. Both in the Quran and in other words of the Arabic and non-Arabic languages, great personages sometimes use the "plural" pronoun when speaking of themselves. This is because they usually have subordinates under them whom they commission to perform tasks; Allah commands the angels, and humans command their subordinates.

And then he added: "We intended that their Lord should grant them in his place a child purer and more affectionate" (فَأَرَدْنا أَنْ يُبْدِلَهُما رَبُّهُما خَيْراً مِنْهُ زَكاةً وَ أَقْرَبَ رُحْماً). The expression "aradna" (we intended) and also "rabbuhuma" (their Lord) are also very meaningful here, and we will soon understand the secret behind them.

"Zakah" here means purity and cleanliness, and it has a broad concept that includes faith and righteous deeds, in both religious and material matters. Perhaps this expression was an answer to Musa’s objection when he said, "You killed a pure soul (nafsan zakiyyah)." He replies: "No, this one was not pure; we wanted God to give them a pure child in his place!"

In several Hadiths found in various Islamic sources, we read: "Allah replaced him with a daughter for them, from whose lineage seventy prophets were born" [1]!

In the final verse under discussion, the learned man lifts the veil from the secret of his third act, namely repairing the wall, and says: "As for the wall, it belonged to two orphan boys in the city, and beneath it was a treasure belonging to them, and their father was a righteous man" (وَ أَمَّا الْجِدارُ فَكانَ لِغُلامَيْنِ يَتِيمَيْنِ فِي الْمَدِينَةِ وَ كانَ تَحْتَهُ كَنْزٌ لَهُما وَ كانَ أَبُوهُما صالِحاً). "Your Lord intended that they should reach their maturity and extract their treasure" (فَأَرادَ رَبُّكَ أَنْ يَبْلُغا أَشُدَّهُما وَ يَسْتَخْرِجا كَنزَهُما).

"This was a mercy from your Lord" (رَحْمَةً مِنْ رَبِّكَ‌).

And I was commanded to build that wall for the sake of the righteousness of the parents of these two orphans, lest it collapse and the treasure be revealed and endangered.

Finally, to remove any doubt or suspicion from Musa, and so that he might know with certainty that all these actions were according to a specific plan and mission, he added: "And I did not do it of my own accord," but rather it was the command of Allah and the instruction of the Lord (وَ ما فَعَلْتُهُ عَنْ أَمْرِي‌).

Yes, "This was the explanation of those things over which you could not maintain patience" (ذلِكَ تَأْوِيلُ ما لَمْ تَسْطِعْ عَلَيْهِ صَبْراً).

Key Points

1- Was Khidr’s Mission within the Legislative System or the Generative System?!

The most important issue that has occupied great scholars in this story is the three events that this learned man performed in front of Musa. Because Musa was unaware of the inner reality of the matter, he began to object, but later, when he heard the teacher's explanations, he was convinced.

The question is: Can one really damage someone's property without their permission to prevent a usurper from destroying it?

And can a youth be punished for an act he will perform in the future?! And is it necessary for us to work for free and perform forced labor to preserve someone's property?! Faced with these questions, we have two paths before us:

The first is to align these with jurisprudential standards and the laws of Sharia, as a group of commentators has done.

They have aligned the first incident with the law of "the more important and the important" (al-ahamm wa al-muhimm) and said that preserving the entire ship was certainly a more important task, while preserving it from minor damage was not much. Or in other words, Khidr here "repelled a greater evil with a lesser evil," especially since the inner satisfaction of the ship owners could have been predicted had they been aware of the situation (and in jurisprudential terms, Khidr had "implied permission" for this act).

Regarding the youth, this group of commentators insists that he must have been an adult, and an apostate or even a "corrupter on earth" (mufsid), and thus was liable to be killed for his current actions. If Khidr attributes his act to the youth's future crimes, it is because he wants to say that this criminal is not only currently engaged in this but will also commit greater crimes in the future; thus, killing him according to the standards of Sharia was permissible based on his current actions.

As for the third case, no one can object to someone for performing an act of sacrifice and altruism for another and preserving their property from being wasted through their own labor. This altruism may not be obligatory, but it is certainly a good deed and worthy of praise; rather, in some cases, it may reach the level of obligation, such as when vast properties of an orphan child are at risk of being lost, and with minor effort, one can prevent their destruction. It is not unlikely that in such a case, this act would be obligatory.

The second path is based on the fact that while the above explanations are convincing regarding the treasure and the wall, they do not align well with the literal meaning of the verse regarding the youth who was killed. This is because the justification for his killing was apparently his future action, not his current ones.

Regarding the ship, it is also somewhat debatable: can we preemptively damage the house, property, and life of anyone we are certain will be usurped in the future without their knowledge so they may escape danger? Do jurists truly accept such a ruling?! Therefore, another path must be taken, which is as follows:

In this world, we have two systems: the "Generative System" (Nizam al-Takwin) and the "Legislative System" (Nizam al-Tashri'). Although these two systems are harmonious in general principles, they sometimes diverge in details.

For example, Allah tests His servants by afflicting them with "fear" (insecurity), "loss of wealth and fruits," and the loss of lives and loved ones, so it may be known who is patient and steadfast in the face of these events.

Can any jurist or even a prophet initiate such an act—that is, destroy property, lives, fruits, and security so that people may be tested?

Or we see that Allah would afflict some of His prophets and righteous servants with great calamities as a warning and for discipline due to a "leaving of the better" (tark al-awla), such as the calamity of Ya’qub عليه السلام due to a lack of attention to some of the poor, or the distress of Yunus عليه السلام due to a small "leaving of the better."

Does anyone have the right to initiate such an act as a punishment or penalty?

Or we see that sometimes Allah takes away a blessing from a person due to ingratitude; for example, they do not give thanks for their wealth, so their property sinks in the sea, or they do not give thanks for health, so Allah takes health away from them. From the perspective of jurisprudence and legislative laws, can anyone destroy another's property or turn health into illness because of ingratitude?

Examples like these are numerous, and collectively they show that the world of creation, especially the creation of man, is established upon this "Best System" (Nizam al-Ahsan). For man to traverse the path of perfection, Allah has placed generative laws and regulations for him, the violation of which has various reactions.

Meanwhile, from the perspective of the law of Sharia, we cannot fit all of them into the framework of these laws.

For instance, a doctor can amputate a person's finger to prevent venom from reaching their heart, but can anyone amputate a person's finger to cultivate patience and steadfastness in them or because of ingratitude for a blessing?! (While certainly Allah can do such a thing because it accords with the Best System).

Now that it is proven we have two systems and Allah is the Ruler over both, there is no obstacle to Allah commissioning a group to implement the Legislative System and commissioning a group of angels or some humans (such as Khidr) to implement the Generative System (pay close attention).

From the perspective of the Divine Generative System, there is no obstacle to Allah afflicting even an underage child with an accident in which they lose their life, because their future existence might bring about great dangers, just as sometimes the survival of such persons contains benefits like testing and trial and the like.

And there is also no obstacle to Allah afflicting me today with a severe illness such that I cannot leave the house, because He knows that if I leave the house, a dangerous accident will occur, and He deems me worthy of being saved from that danger.

In other words, a group of Allah's agents in this world are commissioned with the inner reality (batin) and a group are commissioned with the outer appearance (zahir). Those commissioned with the inner reality have their own specific criteria and program principles, just as those commissioned with the outer appearance have their own specific principles and criteria.

It is true that the general line of both these programs leads man toward perfection, and in this regard, they are harmonious, but they sometimes diverge in details like the examples above.

Of course, without a doubt, in neither of the two lines can anyone act arbitrarily; rather, they must be authorized by the True Owner and Ruler. Therefore, Khidr explicitly stated this truth and said: "ما فَعَلْتُهُ عَنْ أَمْرِي‌" (I never did this of my own accord); rather, I walk exactly according to a Divine program and a criterion and line given to me.

In this way, the contradiction is resolved.

And the reason we see that Musa could not endure Khidr’s actions was because his line of mission was separate from Khidr’s line of mission. Therefore, every time he observed a step contrary to the outward appearances of the law of Sharia, his cry of protest rose. But Khidr continued his path with composure, and since these two great Divine leaders could not live together forever due to their different missions, he said: "هذا فِراقُ بَيْنِي وَ بَيْنِكَ‌" (This is the parting between me and you).

2- Who Was Khidr?

As we have seen, the name "Khidr" is not explicitly mentioned in the Holy Quran. Musa’s companion or teacher is mentioned only as "عَبْداً مِنْ عِبادِنا آتَيْناهُ رَحْمَةً مِنْ عِنْدِنا وَ عَلَّمْناهُ مِنْ لَدُنَّا عِلْماً" (a servant from among Our servants whom We had granted mercy from Us and whom We had taught knowledge from Our presence), which indicates his status of servitude and his specific knowledge. Therefore, we have also mostly referred to him as "the learned man."

However, in numerous narrations, this learned man is identified by the name "Khidr." From some narrations, it is understood that his original name was "Balya ibn Malkan," and Khidr was his title, because wherever he stepped, the ground would become green from his footsteps.

Some have also suggested that the name of this learned man was "Ilyas," and from here the notion arose that "Ilyas" and "Khidr" might be the names of the same person. However, the first view is the most famous and well-known among commentators and narrators of Hadith.

Obviously, whatever this man's name was is not important; what is important is that he was a Divine scholar, encompassed by the specific mercy of the Lord, commissioned with the inner reality and the generative system of the world, aware of some secrets, and in one respect, the teacher of Musa ibn 'Imran, even though Musa was superior to him in some respects.

As for whether he was a prophet or not, we again have various narrations.

In the first volume of Usul al-Kafi, several narrations are cited indicating that this learned man was not a prophet but a scholar like "Dhu al-Qarnayn" and "Asif ibn Barkhiya" [1]. Meanwhile, from some other narrations, it is understood that he held the rank of prophethood, and the appearance of some expressions in the above verses also suggests this, for in one instance he says, "I did not do this of my own accord," and in another place, he says, "We intended such and such."

And from some narrations, it is understood that he enjoyed a very long life.

Here a question arises: does the story of Musa and this great scholar also exist in Jewish and Christian sources?

The answer to the question is that if the "Scriptures" (Torah and Gospel) are meant, no, it is not in them. However, from some books of Jewish scholars compiled in the eleventh century AD, a story is narrated that bears a relatively strong resemblance to the account of Musa and the scholar of his time, even though the hero of that story is "Ilyas" and "Joshua ben Levi," who were commentators of the "Talmud" in the third century AD, and it differs from the account of Musa and Khidr in various respects. That story is as follows:

"Joshua" asks God to meet "Ilyas," and when his prayer is answered and he is honored to meet Ilyas, he asks him to be informed of some secrets. Ilyas tells him, "You do not have the strength to endure," but Joshua insists, and Ilyas grants his request on the condition that he asks no questions about whatever he sees, and if Joshua violates this, Ilyas will leave him. With this agreement, Joshua and Ilyas become traveling companions.

During their journey, they first enter a house where the owner welcomes them warmly. The family living in this house owned only one cow from all the world's possessions, and they made a living by selling its milk. Ilyas orders the owner to kill that cow, and Joshua is deeply astonished and shocked by this act and asks him the reason. Ilyas reminds him of the agreement and threatens him with separation; consequently, Joshua remains silent.

From there, both travel to another village and enter the house of a wealthy man. In this house, Ilyas begins working with clay and repairs a wall that was on the verge of collapse.

In another village, several people from that village were gathered in a place and did not welcome these two well. Ilyas prays for them that they all attain leadership.

In the fourth village, they are welcomed warmly. Ilyas prays that only one of them attains leadership! Finally, "Joshua ben Levi" cannot endure it and asks about the four events. Ilyas says: In the first house, the owner's wife was ill, and if that cow had not been sacrificed as charity, that woman would have died, and the loss for the owner would have been more than the loss resulting from slaughtering the cow.

In the second house, beneath the wall was a treasure that had to be preserved for an orphan child.

For the people of the third village, I wished leadership for all so that their affairs would fall into distress and disorder; conversely, the people of the fourth village will have the reins of their affairs in the hands of one person, and their matters will be organized and settled [1]. Let there be no mistake; we do not mean to say these two stories are one and the same; rather, the point is that the story narrated by Jewish scholars might be a similar story or a distortion of the original account of Musa and Khidr that has changed over a long period and taken this form.

3- Fabricated Legends

The account of Musa and Khidr has its foundation and basis in what is mentioned in the Quran. Unfortunately, however, many legends have been crafted around it, which sometimes give it a superstitious appearance when added to this account! It must be known that this is not the only story to suffer this fate; other real stories have not remained exempt from this issue.

To understand the reality, one must take the above twenty-three verses as the standard, and even Hadiths can only be accepted if they agree with them. If a Hadith contradicts them, it is certainly unacceptable, and fortunately, we do not have such Hadiths in the authentic narrations.

4- Is Forgetfulness Possible for Prophets?!

In the above event, we repeatedly encountered the issue of Musa’s forgetfulness: once regarding the fish they had prepared for food, and three other times in connection with the commitment his learned friend had taken from him.

Now the question arises: is forgetfulness possible for prophets?

A group believes that the occurrence of such forgetfulness from prophets is not far-fetched, as it relates neither to the foundation of the prophetic call, nor its branches, nor the propagation of the message, but rather to a purely ordinary matter related to daily life. What is certain is that no prophet ever falls into error or mistake regarding the prophetic call and its details, and the station of infallibility (Ismah) protects him from such things.

But what is the obstacle to Musa, because he was eagerly and hurriedly following this learned man, forgetting his food, which was an ordinary matter?

And also, what is the obstacle to the magnitude of events such as breaking the ship, killing a youth, and the groundless repair of a wall in a city of stingy people, making him so agitated that he forgot his personal commitment to his learned friend? This is neither far-fetched for a prophet nor does it conflict with the station of infallibility.

Some commentators have also suggested that forgetfulness here might have a metaphorical meaning—that is, "leaving"—because when a person leaves something, it is similar to having forgotten it. But why did Musa leave his food? Because he was indifferent to such a matter. And regarding his commitment to his learned friend, it was because for him, who looked at events through the window of outward appearance, it was not acceptable at all for a person to groundlessly harm people's property or lives; therefore, he saw himself obligated to protest and thought this was not the place for that commitment.

But it is clear that such interpretations cannot be consistent with the literal meaning of the verses.

5- Why Did Musa Hasten to Meet Khidr?!

In a Hadith from Ibn Abbas from Ubayy ibn Ka’b, we read that he narrates from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله: One day Musa was busy delivering a sermon among the Children of Israel. Someone asked him, "Who on earth is the most knowledgeable?" Musa said, "I know no one more knowledgeable than myself." At this moment, it was revealed to Musa: "We have a servant at the 'Junction of the Two Seas' who is more knowledgeable than you." Here, Musa requested from Allah to be granted a meeting with this learned man, and Allah showed him the way to reach this goal [1]. A similar Hadith has been narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام as well [2]. In truth, this was a warning to Musa that despite all his knowledge, he should never consider himself the most superior person.

But here the question arises: shouldn't an Arch-Prophet (Ulul 'Azm) and possessor of a message be the most knowledgeable person of his time?

In response, we say: he must be the most knowledgeable regarding the domain of his mission, namely the Legislative System, and Musa was so. However, as we explained in the first key points, the domain of his learned friend's mission was a separate domain that had no connection to the world of legislation. In other words, that learned man was aware of secrets upon which the prophetic call did not depend.

Incidentally, in a Hadith narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we explicitly read that Musa was more knowledgeable than Khidr—meaning in the knowledge of Sharia [1]. Perhaps the failure to find an answer to this question, as well as the question regarding forgetfulness, has caused some not to consider this Musa as Musa ibn 'Imran and to identify him as another person. But with the resolution of this problem, no room remains for that claim.

From a Hadith narrated from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام, this point is also understood: that the domain of the mission of these two great personages was different from each other, and each was more knowledgeable than the other in his own work [2]. It is also interesting to mention that in a Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it is stated:

"When Musa met Khidr, a bird appeared before them and took a drop of water with its beak. Khidr said to Musa, 'Do you know what the bird is saying?' Musa said, 'What is it saying?' Khidr said: It says: 'My knowledge and the knowledge of Musa compared to the knowledge of Allah is only like what my beak has taken from the water'" [3].

6- What Was That Treasure?

Among the other questions that have arisen around this story is: what essentially was the treasure that Musa’s learned friend insisted on hiding? Furthermore, why had that man of faith, the father of the orphans, amassed such a treasure?! Some have said that this treasure, in reality, had a spiritual aspect more than a material one. According to many narrations from both Shi'a and Sunni sources, this treasure was a tablet upon which wise words were inscribed.

And as for what these wise words were, there is discussion among commentators.

In the book al-Kafi, it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that he said: "This treasure was not gold or silver; it was only a tablet on which four sentences were recorded: 'There is no god but Allah; whoever is certain of death does not laugh (vainly); whoever is certain of the Account (and is thinking of his responsibilities) does not let his heart rejoice; and whoever is certain of Divine Decrees fears none but Allah.'" [1] However, in some other narrations, it is stated it was a tablet of gold, and it seems these two do not contradict each other, because the goal of the first narration is that it was not a heap of dirhams and dinars as the concept of treasure usually brings to mind.

And even if we take the literal meaning of the word "kanz" (treasure) and interpret it as a hoard of gold and silver, it still does not create a problem. This is because the forbidden treasure is when a person hoards a significant amount of valuable property for a long time while there is a great need for it in society. But if, for example, to preserve property—property that is in circulation for trade—they bury it underground for one or a few days (as was common in past times due to insecurity, where they would sometimes bury their property even for one night) and then the owner passes away due to an accident, such a treasure can never be subject to objection.

7- Lessons from This Story

A- Finding a learned leader and benefiting from the light of his knowledge is so important that even an Arch-Prophet like Musa travels all this way to follow him. This is a model for all humans at any level of knowledge and in any circumstances, age, or year.

B- The essence of Divine knowledge stems from servitude and worship of Allah, as we read in the above verses: "عَبْداً مِنْ عِبادِنا ...عَلَّمْناهُ مِنْ لَدُنَّا عِلْماً" (a servant from among Our servants... We taught him knowledge from Our presence).

C- Knowledge should always be learned for the sake of action, as Musa says to his learned friend: "مِمَّا عُلِّمْتَ رُشْداً" (teach me of the knowledge you have been taught for guidance)—meaning, I do not want knowledge only for itself, but I seek it to reach the goal.

D- One should not rush in affairs, for many matters require the appropriate opportunity (al-umuru marhunatun bi-awqatiha), especially in matters of great importance. For this reason, this learned man explained the secret of his actions to Musa at an appropriate opportunity.

E- The outer face and the inner face of things and events is another important lesson this story teaches us. We should not judge hastily regarding unpleasant events that occur in our lives. How many events are there that we dislike, but later it becomes clear that they were among the hidden graces of Allah.

This is the same as what the Quran says elsewhere: "عَسى‌ أَنْ تَكْرَهُوا شَيْئاً وَ هُوَ خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ وَ عَسى‌ أَنْ تُحِبُّوا شَيْئاً وَ هُوَ شَرٌّ لَكُمْ وَ اللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ وَ أَنْتُمْ لا تَعْلَمُونَ‌" (Perhaps you dislike a thing and it is good for you, and perhaps you love a thing and it is bad for you, and Allah knows and you do not know)! [1] Attention to this reality causes a person not to immediately despair when unpleasant events occur. Here we read an interesting Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام where the Imam said to the son of Zurarah (the man who was considered among the greats, jurists, and traditionists of his time, and who had great love for the Imam, and the Imam also had great love for him):

"Convey my greetings to your father and say: If I speak ill of you in some gatherings, it is because our enemies are watching to see whom we show love to, so they may harass him because of the love we have for him. Conversely, if we criticize someone, they praise him. If I sometimes speak ill of you behind your back, it is because you have become famous among the people for your loyalty and love for us, and for this reason, our opponents criticize you. I wished to place a fault upon you to repel their evil, just as Allah says through the tongue of Musa’s learned friend: أَمَّا السَّفِينَةُ فَكانَتْ لِمَساكِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ فِي الْبَحْرِ فَأَرَدْتُ أَنْ أَعِيبَها وَ كانَ وَراءَهُمْ مَلِكٌ يَأْخُذُ كُلَّ سَفِينَةٍ غَصْباً ... Understand this parable correctly. But by Allah, you are the most beloved of people to me, and you are the most beloved of my father's companions, whether living or dead. You are the best of ships in this turbulent sea, and behind you is a tyrannical, usurping king who closely watches for the passage of sound ships that cross this ocean of guidance, so he may seize them. May Allah's mercy be upon you in life and after death" [2].

F- Confessing to realities and taking a stance consistent with them is another lesson we learn from this story. When Musa was caught three times unintentionally in breaking his covenant with his learned friend, he realized well that he could no longer accompany him. Although the parting from this teacher was very difficult for him, he did not show stubbornness in the face of this bitter reality and fairly gave the right to that learned man. He parted from him sincerely and took up his own program of work, while he had amassed great treasures of truth from this short friendship.

A person should not spend their entire life testing themselves and turn life into a laboratory for a future that never comes. When one has tested a matter several times, they must submit to its result.

G- The effects of the fathers' faith for the children—Khidr took upon himself the protection of the children in that part which he could, for the sake of a righteous and upright father. That is, a child can become prosperous in the light of the father's faith and trustworthiness, and the good result of it can reach his child as well. In some narrations, we read that the righteous man was not the immediate father of the orphans but was considered one of their distant ancestors (yes, such is the effect of righteous action) [1]. Among the signs of this father's righteousness is that he left a treasure of spirituality and wise advice as a legacy for his children.

H- Shortening of life due to harassing parents—If a child deserves death because in the future he will harass his parents and rebel against them and be ungrateful, or lead them away from the Divine path, what is the state of a child who is currently engaged in this sin? What is their status before Allah?

In Islamic narrations, a close link is mentioned between the shortening of life and the abandonment of "maintaining family ties" (silat al-rahim), especially harassing parents, which we referred to in part under verse 23 of Surah al-Isra' [1].

I- People are enemies of what they do not know! It often happens that someone does good to us, but because we do not know the inner reality of the act, we perceive it as enmity and become agitated. Especially regarding what we do not know, we are impatient and restless. Of course, it is a natural thing for a person to be impatient regarding matters of which they see only one side or one angle. But the above story tells us that one should not rush in judgment; one must examine the various dimensions of every subject.

In a Hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, we read: "People are enemies of what they do not know" [2]. Therefore, the more the level of people's awareness rises, the more logical their encounter with issues will become. In other words, the foundation of "patience" is awareness!

Of course, Musa had a right to be upset from one perspective, because he saw that in these three incidents, almost the greater part of the Sharia was endangered: in the first incident, the immunity of people's property; in the second incident, the immunity of people's lives; and in the third incident, legal matters, or in other words, the logical treatment of people's rights. Therefore, it is no surprise that he became so upset that he forgot his solemn covenant with that great scholar. But as soon as he became aware of the inner reality of the matter, he became calm and no longer protested. This itself expresses how much the lack of information about the inner reality of events is worrying.

J- Etiquette of the Student and Teacher—In the dialogues that took place between Musa and that Divine learned man, interesting points regarding the etiquette of the student and teacher are observed, such as:

1- Musa introduces himself as a follower of Khidr (أَتَّبِعُكَ‌). 2- Musa expresses his followership in the form of requesting permission from him (هَلْ أَتَّبِعُكَ‌). 3- He confesses his need for learning and attributes knowledge to his teacher (عَلى‌ أَنْ تُعَلِّمَنِ‌). 4- In a state of humility, he describes the teacher's knowledge as vast and himself as seeking to learn a portion of his knowledge (mimma). 5- He refers to the teacher's knowledge as a Divine knowledge (ullimta). 6- He seeks guidance and direction from him (rushda). 7- He subtly reminds him that just as Allah has been kind to you and taught you, you too should show this kindness to me (تُعَلِّمَنِ مِمَّا عُلِّمْتَ‌). 8- The phrase "hal attabi'uka" also conveys the reality that the student must follow the teacher; it is not the teacher's duty to follow the student (except in specific cases). 9- Musa, with that great status he had (an Arch-Prophet and possessor of a message and a book), shows all this humility; meaning, whoever you are and whatever status you have, you must be humble in the pursuit of knowledge. 10- In stating his commitment to the teacher, he did not use a definitive expression but said: سَتَجِدُنِي إِنْ شاءَ اللَّهُ صابِراً (If Allah wills, you will find me patient), which is both etiquette before the Lord and before the teacher, so that if a violation occurs, there is no breach of respect toward the teacher.

It is also necessary to mention that this God-conscious scholar showed the utmost forbearance and patience in the station of teaching and discipline. Whenever Musa, due to agitation, forgot his commitment and began to protest, he would only say calmly in the form of a question: "Did I not say that you cannot maintain patience with my actions?"

Ayahs

وَيَسْـَٔلُونَكَ عَن ذِى ٱلْقَرْنَيْنِ ۖ قُلْ سَأَتْلُوا۟ عَلَيْكُم مِّنْهُ ذِكْرًا (٨٣)
They question you concerning Dhul Qarnayn. Say, ‘I will relate to you an account of him.’ (83)
إِنَّا مَكَّنَّا لَهُۥ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ مِن كُلِّ شَىْءٍ سَبَبًا (٨٤)
Indeed We had granted him power in the land and given him the means to all things. (84)
فَأَتْبَعَ سَبَبًا (٨٥)
So he directed a means. (85)
حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا بَلَغَ مَغْرِبَ ٱلشَّمْسِ وَجَدَهَا تَغْرُبُ فِى عَيْنٍ حَمِئَةٍ وَوَجَدَ عِندَهَا قَوْمًا ۗ قُلْنَا يَـٰذَا ٱلْقَرْنَيْنِ إِمَّآ أَن تُعَذِّبَ وَإِمَّآ أَن تَتَّخِذَ فِيهِمْ حُسْنًا (٨٦)
When he reached the place where the sun sets, he found it setting over a warm sea, and by it he found a people. We said, ‘O Dhul Qarnayn! You will either punish them, or treat them with kindness.’ (86)
قَالَ أَمَّا مَن ظَلَمَ فَسَوْفَ نُعَذِّبُهُۥ ثُمَّ يُرَدُّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهِۦ فَيُعَذِّبُهُۥ عَذَابًا نُّكْرًا (٨٧)
He said, ‘As for him who is a wrongdoer, we will punish him. Then he shall be returned to his Lord and He will punish him with a dire punishment. (87)
وَأَمَّا مَنْ ءَامَنَ وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا فَلَهُۥ جَزَآءً ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ ۖ وَسَنَقُولُ لَهُۥ مِنْ أَمْرِنَا يُسْرًا (٨٨)
But as for him who has faith and acts righteously, he shall have the best reward, and we will assign him easy tasks under our command.’ (88)
ثُمَّ أَتْبَعَ سَبَبًا (٨٩)
Thereafter he directed another means. (89)
حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا بَلَغَ مَطْلِعَ ٱلشَّمْسِ وَجَدَهَا تَطْلُعُ عَلَىٰ قَوْمٍ لَّمْ نَجْعَل لَّهُم مِّن دُونِهَا سِتْرًا (٩٠)
When he reached the place where the sun rises, he found it rising on a people for whom We had not provided any shield against it. (90)
كَذَٰلِكَ وَقَدْ أَحَطْنَا بِمَا لَدَيْهِ خُبْرًا (٩١)
So it was, and We were fully aware of whatever [means] he possessed. (91)

Commentary

The Extraordinary Story of Dhul-Qarnayn

At the beginning of the discussion regarding the People of the Cave (Ashab al-Kahf), we mentioned that a group from the Quraysh intended to "test" the Prophet of Islam. After consulting with the Jews of Medina, they proposed three questions: first, the history of the People of the Cave; second, the nature of the Soul (Ruh); and third, the story of Dhul-Qarnayn. The answer regarding the Soul appeared in Surah al-Isra, while the answers to the other two questions are found in this very Surah, al-Kahf.

Now it is time for the story of Dhul-Qarnayn:

As previously noted, Surah al-Kahf refers to three stories which, though seemingly different, share a common thread (the stories of the People of the Cave, Moses and Khidr, and Dhul-Qarnayn). All three involve matters that take us beyond the boundaries of ordinary life, demonstrating that the universe and its realities are not limited to what we see and are accustomed to.

The story of Dhul-Qarnayn concerns an individual who has occupied the thoughts of philosophers and researchers from ancient times until today, and much effort has been spent to identify him.

We will first proceed with the commentary on the verses related to Dhul-Qarnayn—totaling 16 verses—which, regardless of his historical identity, is in itself a highly instructive lesson full of key points.

Subsequently, we will enter the discussion to identify him using the clues available in these verses, narrations, and the accounts of historians.

In other words, we will first speak of his "personality," and what is of primary importance from the perspective of the Quran is this first subject.

The first verse says: "They ask you about Dhul-Qarnayn" (وَ يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنْ ذِي الْقَرْنَيْنِ).

"Say: I will soon recite to you a mention of his story" (قُلْ سأَتْلُوا عَلَيْكُمْ مِنْهُ ذِكْراً). The use of the expression "sa-atlu" (I will soon recite), considering that the prefix "sin" usually denotes the near future, while in this case the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله spoke of Dhul-Qarnayn immediately, may be for the sake of literary etiquette—an etiquette mixed with the avoidance of haste and impulsiveness, implying the reception of the word from Allah and then its delivery to the people.

In any case, the beginning of this verse indicates that the story of Dhul-Qarnayn was already a topic of discussion among the people, though it was shrouded in disagreements or ambiguities; for this reason, they sought the necessary clarifications from the Prophet صلي الله عليه وآله on this matter.

Then it adds: "Indeed, We established him upon the earth" (We granted him power, stability, strength, and governance) (إِنَّا مَكَّنَّا لَهُ فِي الْأَرْضِ). "And We gave him the means to everything" (وَ آتَيْناهُ مِنْ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ سَبَباً). Although some commentators have sought to limit the concept of "sabab"—which originally means a rope used to climb palm trees and was later applied to any kind of means—to a specific meaning, it is evident that the verse is entirely absolute and has a broad concept. It shows that Allah had placed the means to reach everything at the disposal of Dhul-Qarnayn: sufficient intellect and insight, sound management, power and strength, armies and manpower, and material resources. In short, whatever spiritual and material means were necessary for progress and achieving goals, We placed at his disposal.

"So he followed a way" (using those means) (فَاتَّبَعَ سَبَباً).

"Until, when he reached the setting-place of the sun" (حَتَّى إِذا بَلَغَ مَغْرِبَ الشَّمْسِ).

"There he perceived it setting in a dark and muddy spring or sea" (وَجَدَها تَغْرُبُ فِي عَيْنٍ حَمِئَةٍ) [^1] "and there he found a people" (who were a mixture of good and bad individuals) (وَ وَجَدَ عِنْدَها قَوْماً).

"We said: O Dhul-Qarnayn! Either you shall punish them or adopt a way of kindness among them" (قُلْنا يا ذَا الْقَرْنَيْنِ إِمَّا أَنْ تُعَذِّبَ وَ إِمَّا أَنْ تَتَّخِذَ فِيهِمْ حُسْناً) [^2]. Some commentators wish to infer his prophethood from the expression "qulna" (We said to Dhul-Qarnayn), but the possibility also exists that this phrase refers to a heart-felt inspiration (ilham), which exists for non-prophets as well. However, it cannot be denied that this expression more readily brings the concept of prophethood to mind.

Dhul-Qarnayn "said: As for him who has done wrong, we shall punish him" (قالَ أَمَّا مَنْ ظَلَمَ فَسَوْفَ نُعَذِّبُهُ).

[^1]: "Hami'ah" originally means foul-smelling black mud, or in other words, "silt." This indicates that the land Dhul-Qarnayn reached contained many marshlands, such that at sunset, he perceived the sun as sinking into those marshes, just as all sea travelers and coastal dwellers have such a perception of the sun setting into the sea or rising from it.

[^2]: The phrase "إِمَّا أَنْ تُعَذِّبَ ..." appears to be an interrogative sentence, even though its outward form is declarative.

"Then he will be returned to his Lord, and He will punish him with a terrible punishment" (ثُمَّ يُرَدُّ إِلى‌ رَبِّهِ فَيُعَذِّبُهُ عَذاباً نُكْراً) [^1]. These wrongdoers and oppressors will taste both the punishment of this world and the torment of the Hereafter.

"But as for him who believes and does righteous deeds, he will have a goodly reward" (وَ أَمَّا مَنْ آمَنَ وَ عَمِلَ صالِحاً فَلَهُ جَزاءً الْحُسْنى‌). "And we will give him an easy command" (وَ سَنَقُولُ لَهُ مِنْ أَمْرِنا يُسْراً).

We will both treat him with kind words and will not place hard or heavy burdens upon his shoulders, nor will we take heavy tribute or taxes from him.

It seems Dhul-Qarnayn's purpose in this statement was to point out that people would be divided into two groups regarding his call to monotheism, faith, and the struggle against oppression, polytheism, and corruption: those who submit to this constructive divine program will certainly have a good reward and live in security and peace of mind.

But those who take a hostile stance against this call and continue in polytheism, oppression, and corruption will be punished.

Furthermore, from the contrast between "him who has done wrong" (man zalama) and "him who believes and does righteous deeds" (man amana wa 'amila salihan), it becomes clear that "wrong" (zulm) here means polytheism and unrighteous action, which are the bitter fruits of the ill-omened tree of polytheism.

Dhul-Qarnayn concluded his journey to the West and then resolved toward the East, as

[^1]: "Nukr" is from the root "munkar," meaning unknown; that is, an unknown punishment that he would never have believed.

the Quran says: "Then he followed another way (using the means at his disposal)" (ثُمَّ أَتْبَعَ سَبَباً).

"And he continued his way until he reached the rising-place of the sun" (حَتَّى إِذا بَلَغَ مَطْلِعَ الشَّمْسِ). "There he found it rising upon a people for whom We had provided no shelter against the sun" (وَجَدَها تَطْلُعُ عَلى‌ قَوْمٍ لَمْ نَجْعَلْ لَهُمْ مِنْ دُونِها سِتْراً).

This population was at a very low stage of human life, to the extent that they lived naked, or had very little covering that did not shield their bodies from the sun.

Some commentators have also considered it not unlikely that they had no houses or dwellings to shelter them from the sun's rays. [^1] Another possibility mentioned in the commentary of this phrase is that their land was a desert lacking mountains, trees, and shelters, and there was nothing in that desert to cover them from the sun and provide shade. [^2] At the same time, these interpretations are not mutually exclusive.

Indeed, "Thus it was (the work of Dhul-Qarnayn), and We knew well what means he had (to advance his goals)" (كَذلِكَ وَ قَدْ أَحَطْنا بِما لَدَيْهِ خُبْراً).

Some commentators have suggested the possibility that the above sentence is an allusion to Divine guidance toward Dhul-Qarnayn in his programs and efforts. [^1]

[^1]: In some narrations reaching us from the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, the first interpretation is mentioned, and in others, the second; yet they are not contradictory. (Refer to Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 306).

[^2]: Tafsir Fi Zilal and Fakhr Razi under the verse in question.

[^1]: Al-Mizan, Vol. 13, p. 391.

Ayahs

ثُمَّ أَتْبَعَ سَبَبًا (٩٢)
Thereafter he directed another means. (92)
حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا بَلَغَ بَيْنَ ٱلسَّدَّيْنِ وَجَدَ مِن دُونِهِمَا قَوْمًا لَّا يَكَادُونَ يَفْقَهُونَ قَوْلًا (٩٣)
When he reached [the place] between the two barriers, he found between them a people who could hardly understand a word [of his language]. (93)
قَالُوا۟ يَـٰذَا ٱلْقَرْنَيْنِ إِنَّ يَأْجُوجَ وَمَأْجُوجَ مُفْسِدُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَهَلْ نَجْعَلُ لَكَ خَرْجًا عَلَىٰٓ أَن تَجْعَلَ بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَهُمْ سَدًّا (٩٤)
They said, ‘O Dhul Qarnayn! Indeed Gog and Magog are causing disaster in this land. Shall we pay you a tribute on condition that you build a barrier between them and us?’ (94)
قَالَ مَا مَكَّنِّى فِيهِ رَبِّى خَيْرٌ فَأَعِينُونِى بِقُوَّةٍ أَجْعَلْ بَيْنَكُمْ وَبَيْنَهُمْ رَدْمًا (٩٥)
He said, ‘What my Lord has furnished me is better. Yet help me with some strength, and I will make a bulwark between you and them. (95)
ءَاتُونِى زُبَرَ ٱلْحَدِيدِ ۖ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا سَاوَىٰ بَيْنَ ٱلصَّدَفَيْنِ قَالَ ٱنفُخُوا۟ ۖ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَعَلَهُۥ نَارًا قَالَ ءَاتُونِىٓ أُفْرِغْ عَلَيْهِ قِطْرًا (٩٦)
Bring me pieces of iron!’ When he had levelled up between the flanks, he said, ‘Blow!’ When he had turned it into fire, he said, ‘Bring me molten copper to pour over it.’ (96)
فَمَا ٱسْطَـٰعُوٓا۟ أَن يَظْهَرُوهُ وَمَا ٱسْتَطَـٰعُوا۟ لَهُۥ نَقْبًا (٩٧)
So they could neither scale it, nor could they make a hole in it. (97)
قَالَ هَـٰذَا رَحْمَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّى ۖ فَإِذَا جَآءَ وَعْدُ رَبِّى جَعَلَهُۥ دَكَّآءَ ۖ وَكَانَ وَعْدُ رَبِّى حَقًّا (٩٨)
He said, ‘This is a mercy from my Lord. But when the promise of my Lord is fulfilled, He will level it; and my Lord’s promise is true.’ (98)

Commentary

How was the Barrier of Dhul-Qarnayn Built?

The above verses refer to another of Dhul-Qarnayn's journeys, stating:

"After this incident, he again utilized the important means at his disposal" (ثُمَّ أَتْبَعَ سَبَباً).

"He continued on his way until he reached between two mountains, and there he found a group—

distinct from the previous two groups—who understood almost no speech" (حَتَّى إِذا بَلَغَ بَيْنَ السَّدَّيْنِ وَجَدَ مِنْ دُونِهِما قَوْماً لا يَكادُونَ يَفْقَهُونَ قَوْلًا).

This indicates that he reached a mountainous region and observed a population there (other than the two populations he had found in the East and West) who were at a very low level of civilization; for one of the clearest signs of human civilization is speech.

Some have also suggested the possibility that the phrase "understood almost no speech" (لايَكادُونَ يَفْقَهُونَ قَوْلًا) does not mean they were unfamiliar with known languages, but rather that they did not comprehend the content of speech—meaning they were intellectually very underdeveloped.

As for where these two mountains were located—like other historical and geographical aspects of this story—we shall discuss it at the end of the commentary.

At this time, that population, who were suffering at the hands of bloodthirsty and relentless enemies named Gog and Magog (Ya’juj and Ma’juj), took advantage of the arrival of Dhul-Qarnayn, who possessed great power and resources. They appealed to him and "said: O Dhul-Qarnayn! Indeed, Gog and Magog are spreading corruption in this land; may we then pay you a tribute on the condition that you set up a barrier between us and them?" (قالُوا يا ذَا الْقَرْنَيْنِ إِنَّ يَأْجُوجَ وَ مَأْجُوجَ مُفْسِدُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ فَهَلْ نَجْعَلُ لَكَ خَرْجاً عَلى‌ أَنْ تَجْعَلَ بَيْنَنا وَ بَيْنَهُمْ سَدًّا).

This speech of theirs, despite the fact that they did not understand at least the language of Dhul-Qarnayn, might have been through signs and gestures, or a very deficient dialect that cannot be formally accounted for.

It has also been suggested that the understanding between them was facilitated by certain translators—

or through Divine inspiration, similar to the speech of certain birds with Solomon (Sulayman) عليه السلام.

In any case, it is inferred from this sentence that the population was in a good state regarding economic resources, but they were weak in terms of industry, intellect, and planning. Therefore, they were willing to bear the cost of this important barrier provided that Dhul-Qarnayn accepted its design and construction.

Regarding "Gog" and "Magog," we shall speak at the end of this discussion, Allah willing.

However, in response to them, Dhul-Qarnayn "declared that what my Lord has established for me (of what you wish to provide) is better," and I have no need for your financial assistance (قالَ ما مَكَّنِّي فِيهِ رَبِّي خَيْرٌ). "Help me with strength (manpower), so that I may create a formidable rampart between you and these two corrupt nations" (فَأَعِينُونِي بِقُوَّةٍ أَجْعَلْ بَيْنَكُمْ وَ بَيْنَهُمْ رَدْماً).

"Radm" (رَدْم) originally means filling a gap with stones, but it was later used in a broader sense to include any kind of barrier, and even the patching of clothes.

A group of commentators believe that "Radm" refers to a firm and powerful barrier [1], and according to this interpretation, Dhul-Qarnayn promised them that he would build something beyond what they expected.

Additionally, it should be noted that Sadd (with a short 'a') and Sudd (with a short 'u') carry the same meaning, which is a partition created between two things. However, according to Raghib in Al-Mufradat, some have distinguished between the two, considering the former to be man-made and the latter to be natural barriers.

[1]: Alusi in Ruh al-Ma'ani, Fayd Kashani in Tafsir al-Safi, and Fakhr Razi in Al-Tafsir al-Kabir.

Then he commanded: "Bring me large blocks of iron" (آتُونِي زُبَرَ الْحَدِيدِ).

"Zubar" (زُبَر) is the plural of "Zubrah," meaning large and thick pieces of iron.

When the pieces of iron were ready, he issued the order to stack them upon one another "until he had completely leveled the gap between the two mountain cliffs" (حَتَّى إِذا ساوى‌ بَيْنَ الصَّدَفَيْنِ‌).

"Sadaf" (صَدَف) here means the side of a mountain. From this expression, it becomes clear that there was a gap between the two mountain sides through which Gog and Magog would enter, and Dhul-Qarnayn intended to fill it.

In any case, the third command of Dhul-Qarnayn was that "he said: Bring combustible materials (firewood and the like) and place them on both sides of this barrier, and with the tools you have," blow into it until the pieces of iron became red and molten (قالَ انْفُخُوا حَتَّى إِذا جَعَلَهُ ناراً).

In reality, he wanted to join the pieces of iron together through this method to create a unified barrier. With this extraordinary plan, he performed the same task that is done today through welding; that is, he applied so much heat to the irons that they softened slightly and fused together!

Finally, he issued the last command: "He said: Bring me molten copper so that I may pour it over this barrier" (قالَ آتُونِي أُفْرِغْ عَلَيْهِ قِطْراً).

In this way, he covered the entire iron barrier with a layer of copper, protecting it from air penetration and corrosion!

Some commentators have also stated that modern science has proven that adding a certain amount of copper to iron significantly increases its resistance. Dhul-Qarnayn—

since He was aware of this fact, proceeded with such an action.

Furthermore, the famous meaning of "Qitr" (قِطْر) is what we mentioned (molten copper), though some commentators have interpreted it as "molten zinc," which is contrary to the well-known view.

Ultimately, this barrier became so powerful and firm that "that corrupt group was unable to scale it, nor were they able to pierce a hole in it" (فَمَا اسْطاعُوا أَنْ يَظْهَرُوهُ وَ مَا اسْتَطاعُوا لَهُ نَقْباً).

At this point, although Dhul-Qarnayn had performed a very significant task—and according to the way of the arrogant, he should have boasted about it, felt proud, or placed a favor upon that group—because he was a man of God, he "declared with utmost humility: This is a mercy from my Lord" (قالَ هذا رَحْمَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّي‌). If I possess knowledge and insight and can take such an important step by means of it, it is from Allah; and if I have power and influence of speech, that too is from Him.

And if such materials were placed at my disposal, that too is by the blessing of the vast mercy of the Lord. I have nothing of my own to boast about, and I have done no great deed to place a favor upon the servants of Allah! Then he added this sentence: Do not think that this is an eternal and everlasting barrier; no, "when the promise of my Lord comes to pass, He will level it to the ground and turn it into a flat and even land!" (فَإِذا جاءَ وَعْدُ رَبِّي جَعَلَهُ دَكَّاءَ).

"And the promise of my Lord is true" (وَ كانَ وَعْدُ رَبِّي حَقًّا).

In this statement, Dhul-Qarnayn refers to the issue of the annihilation of the world and the collapse of its structure on the threshold of the Resurrection.

However, some commentators consider the "promise of God" to be an allusion to human scientific progress, whereby an impassable barrier no longer has meaning; aerial means, such as airplanes, helicopters, and the like, remove all such obstacles.

But this interpretation seems unlikely.

Key Points

1- Instructive Points of This Historical Story

Of course, as to who Dhul-Qarnayn was, how his journeys to the East and West took place, where the barrier he built is located, and similar matters, we will discuss them later, Allah willing. However, regardless of the historical comparative aspects, this story itself contains many instructive points that require attention more than anything else and, in fact, constitute the primary goal of the Quran.

1- The first lesson it teaches us is that in the world, no task is possible without resorting to means (asbab). Therefore, for the progress of Dhul-Qarnayn's work, Allah gave him the "means" of progress and victory (وَ آتَيْناهُ مِنْ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ سَبَباً), and he utilized these means well (فاتبع سببا). Therefore, those who expect to achieve victory without preparing the necessary means will get nowhere, even if they were Dhul-Qarnayn!

2- Although the setting of the sun in a muddy spring was certainly an optical illusion, it nonetheless shows that the sun, with all its greatness, can be covered by a muddy spring, just as a great human being and a high-ranking personality can sometimes fall completely due to a single slip, and their personality will set in the eyes of others.

3- No government can achieve victory without encouraging the servants and punishing the wrongdoers. This is the same principle that Dhul-Qarnayn utilized well, saying: "We shall punish those who have committed oppression, and we shall reward those who have faith and do righteous deeds in a worthy and good manner."

Ali عليه السلام, in his famous command to Malik al-Ashtar—which is a comprehensive manual for statecraft—says:

"The doer of good and the doer of evil must not hold an equal position in your sight, for that would discourage the doers of good from their goodness and train the evildoers in their evil." [1]

4- A burdensome task is never suitable for a government of Divine justice. For this reason, after Dhul-Qarnayn explicitly stated that he would punish the oppressors and reward the righteous, he added: "I will propose an easy and simple program for them" (so that they may have the ability to perform it out of desire, inclination, and enthusiasm).

5- A comprehensive government cannot be indifferent to the differences and diversity of people's lives and their various conditions. For this reason, Dhul-Qarnayn, who held a Divine government, acted appropriately when encountering various nations, each of whom had their own specific way of life, and took them all under his wing.

6- Dhul-Qarnayn did not even overlook the population who, according to the Quran, understood almost no speech (لا يَكادُونَ يَفْقَهُونَ قَوْلًا). He listened to their grievances by every possible means and fulfilled their needs, building a firm barrier between them and their relentless enemies. Despite the fact that it does not seem such an underdeveloped population—

[1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Letter 53.

would have any benefit for the government, he proceeded to reform their affairs without any expectation of reward.

In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read:

"Making the deaf hear without showing annoyance is a pleasant charity." [1]

7- Security is the first and most important condition for a healthy social life. For this reason, to provide it for a nation that was under threat, Dhul-Qarnayn undertook the most laborious tasks and used the strongest barriers to prevent the corruptors—a barrier that has become a proverb in history and a symbol of strength, durability, and permanence. They say, "Like the barrier of Alexander!" (even though Dhul-Qarnayn was not Alexander). Fundamentally, unless the corruptors are stopped with decisiveness and by means of the strongest barriers, society will not see happiness.

For this reason, when building the Kaaba, the first thing Abraham عليه السلام requested from Allah for that land was the blessing of security (رَبِّ اجْعَلْ هَذَا الْبَلَدَ آمِناً) [2]. Also for this reason, the harshest punishments in Islamic jurisprudence are considered for those who endanger the security of society (refer to the commentary of Surah Al-Ma'idah, verse 33).

8- Another lesson that can be learned from this historical event is—

[1]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 2, under the word "Samam." [2]: Surah Ibrahim, Verse 35.

that the primary owners of the problem must be partners in the execution of the work, for "the sigh of the one in pain has an effect." Therefore, Dhul-Qarnayn first commanded the group complaining of the invasion of savage nations to bring pieces of iron; then he commanded the lighting of fire around the iron barrier for welding; and after that, he commanded the preparation of molten copper to cover the iron with a layer of copper. Fundamentally, work that proceeds with the participation of the primary owners of the problem both helps the emergence of their talents and causes them to value the resulting outcome and strive to preserve it, as they endured much suffering in its construction.

Furthermore, it becomes clearly evident that even an underdeveloped nation, when provided with correct planning and management, can undertake such an important and miraculous task.

9- A Divine leader must be indifferent to wealth and material things and be content with what Allah has placed at his disposal. Thus, we see that Dhul-Qarnayn, contrary to the way of monarchs who have an extraordinary greed for accumulating wealth from everywhere and everyone, did not accept the offer of wealth and said: "What my Lord has established for me is better" (ما مَكَّنِّي فِيهِ رَبِّي خَيْرٌ). In the Holy Quran, we repeatedly read in the stories of the Prophets that one of their most fundamental statements was: "We never demand any wage, reward, or wealth from you in exchange for our call."

In eleven instances in the Holy Quran, this matter is seen regarding the Prophet of Islam or previous Prophets; sometimes it is accompanied by the phrase "my reward is only upon Allah," and sometimes without it. Occasionally, they mentioned the love of their household—which was itself a foundation for future leadership—as a reward: "Say: I do not ask of you any reward for it except love for my near relatives" (قُلْ لا أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْراً إِلَّا الْمَوَدَّةَ فِي الْقُرْبى‌).

10- "Thoroughness in work" from every aspect is another lesson of this story. In building the barrier, Dhul-Qarnayn used large pieces of iron, and to ensure these pieces were completely fused together, he melted them in fire. To ensure the barrier had a long life and resisted the effects of air, moisture, and rain, he covered it with a layer of copper to prevent the iron from corroding.

11- No matter how strong, powerful, capable, and influential a human becomes, and no matter how great the tasks he accomplishes, he must never boast or become arrogant. This is another lesson that Dhul-Qarnayn teaches everyone. Everywhere, he relied on the power of the Lord. After completing the barrier, he said: "This is a mercy from my Lord" (هذا رَحْمَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّي‌). When they offered him financial help, he said: "What my Lord has established for me is better" (ما مَكَّنِّي فِيهِ رَبِّي خَيْرٌ). Finally, when he spoke of the annihilation of this firm barrier, his reliance was again upon the promise of his Lord.

12- Everything is subject to vanishing, and the firmest structures of this world will eventually suffer decay, even if they are made of a single piece of iron and steel. This is the final lesson in this event—a lesson for all those who practically consider the world eternal, striving so unconditionally and greedily in accumulating wealth and acquiring status as if death and annihilation never exist. Yet, let alone the barrier of Dhul-Qarnayn, the sun with all its greatness will eventually perish and be extinguished, and the mountains with all their solidity will be shattered and fall apart. Man is the most vulnerable of all in this regard.

Is not reflection upon this reality enough to stop self-will and tyranny?

2- Who was Dhul-Qarnayn?

As to who the Dhul-Qarnayn mentioned in the Holy Quran was historically, and which of the famous men of history he corresponds to, there is much discussion among commentators. Various opinions have been expressed in this regard, the most important of which are the following three theories:
