First: Some believe he is none other than "Alexander the Macedonian." Therefore, some call him Alexander Dhul-Qarnayn and believe that after his father's death, he gained control over the lands of Rome, the West, and Egypt, and built the city of Alexandria. Then he brought Syria and Jerusalem under his sway, went from there to Armenia, conquered Iraq and Iran, then intended for "India" and "China," and from there returned to Khorasan, built many cities, came to Iraq, and afterwards fell ill in the city of "Zur" and passed away. According to some, he did not live more than 36 years. His body was taken to Alexandria and buried there. [1]

Second: A group of historians believe Dhul-Qarnayn was one of the kings of "Yemen" (the kings of Yemen were called "Tubba'," the plural of which is "Tababi'ah"). Among them, Asma'i in the history of the Arabs before Islam, Ibn Hisham in his famous history titled Sirah, and Abu Rayhan al-Biruni in Al-Athar al-Baqiyah can be named as defenders of this theory.

[1]: Tafsir Fakhr Razi under the verses in question and Kamil of Ibn Athir, Vol. 1, p. 287. Some believe the first person to express this theory was Sheikh Abu Ali Sina in the book Al-Shifa.

Even in the poems of the "Himyarites" (who were from the tribes of Yemen) and some pre-Islamic poets, verses are seen in which they take pride in the existence of "Dhul-Qarnayn." [1] According to this hypothesis, the barrier built by Dhul-Qarnayn is the same as the famous "Ma'rib Barrier."

The third theory, which is also considered the most recent, is the one proposed by the famous Islamic scholar "Abul Kalam Azad"—who was once the Minister of Culture of India—in the scholarly book he wrote on this subject. [2] According to this theory, Dhul-Qarnayn is "Cyrus the Great," the Achaemenid king.

Since the first and second theories have almost no significant historical evidence, and beyond that, neither Alexander the Macedonian possesses the attributes the Quran describes for Dhul-Qarnayn, nor do any of the kings of Yemen.

Furthermore, "Alexander the Macedonian" did not build a famous barrier, and the "Ma'rib Barrier" in "Yemen" is a barrier that does not correspond to any of the attributes the Quran mentions for the barrier of Dhul-Qarnayn; for the barrier of Dhul-Qarnayn, according to the Quran, was made of iron and copper and was to prevent the invasion of savage tribes, whereas the Ma'rib Barrier was made of ordinary materials for the purpose of collecting water and preventing floods, the description of which the Quran provides in Surah Saba.

For this reason, we focus the discussion more on the third theory, and here—

[1]: Al-Mizan, Vol. 13, p. 414. [2]: This book has been translated into Persian and published under the title Dhul-Qarnayn or Cyrus the Great, and many contemporary commentators and historians have detailed this theory with a favorable tone in their books.

we deem it necessary to pay close attention to several matters:

A: The first thing that attracts attention here is why "Dhul-Qarnayn" (Owner of Two Horns/Two Centuries) was called by this name?

Some believe this naming is because he reached the East and West of the world, which the Arabs refer to as the "two horns of the sun" (qarnay al-shams).

Others believe this name was because he lived or ruled for two centuries, and they have differing opinions on the length of a century.

Some say there were specific protrusions on the two sides of his head, and because of that, he became known as Dhul-Qarnayn.

And finally, some are of the opinion that his specific crown had two horns.

There are other opinions, the citation of all of which would be lengthy, and as we shall see, the innovator of the third theory, "Abul Kalam Azad," made extensive use of this title to prove his theory.

B: It is well-inferred from the Holy Quran that Dhul-Qarnayn possessed distinguished attributes:

  • Allah placed the means of victories at his disposal.

  • He had three major military expeditions: first to the West, then to the East, and finally to a region where a mountain pass existed. In each of these journeys, he encountered nations whose attributes were described in the commentary of the previous verses.

  • He was a man of faith, a monotheist, and kind, and he did not deviate from the path of justice and equity; for this reason, he was subject to the special grace of the Lord. He was a helper of the righteous and an enemy of the oppressors and tyrants, and he had no interest in the wealth and riches of the world.

  • He believed in both Allah and the Day of Resurrection.

  • He is the builder of one of the most important and powerful barriers—a barrier in which iron and copper were used instead of brick and stone (and if other materials were used in its construction, they were overshadowed by these metals)—and his goal in building this barrier was to help an oppressed group against the oppression of the Gog and Magog tribes.

  • He was someone whose name was famous among a group of people before the revelation of the Quran; hence the Quraysh or the Jews asked the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله about him, as the Quran says: "They ask you about Dhul-Qarnayn" (يَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنْ ذِي الْقَرْنَيْنِ‌).

However, nothing explicitly indicating he was a Prophet is inferred from the Quran, although there are expressions in the Quran that allude to this meaning, as mentioned in the commentary of the previous verses.

From many Islamic narrations narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, we also read: "He was not a Prophet but a righteous servant." [1]

C: The basis of the third view (that Dhul-Qarnayn was Cyrus the Great) is very concisely founded on two principles:

First: Those who questioned the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله about this matter, according to the narrations regarding the occasion of revelation, were the Jews, or the Quraysh at the instigation of the Jews. Therefore, the root of this matter must be found in the books of the Jews.

Among the famous books of the Jews, we return to the Book of Daniel, Chapter 8. There we read:

"In the year of the reign of Belshazzar, a vision appeared to me, Daniel, after the vision that appeared to me at first. And I saw in the vision, and it happened while I was looking—

[1]: Refer to Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, pp. 294 and 295.

that I was in the palace of Shushan, which is in the province of Elam; and I saw in the vision that I was by the river Ulai. Then I lifted up my eyes and saw, and behold, there stood before the river a ram which had two horns, and the horns were high... I saw the ram pushing westward, northward, and southward, so that no animal could withstand him; nor was there any that could deliver from his hand, but he did according to his will and became great..." [1]

After that, in the same book, it is narrated from Daniel: "Gabriel appeared to him and interpreted his dream thus:

The ram which you saw, having two horns, they are the kings of Media and Persia."

The Jews understood from the glad tidings of Daniel's vision that the period of their captivity would end with the uprising of one of the kings of Media and Persia and his victory over the kings of Babylon, and they would be freed from the clutches of the Babylonians.

Not long after, "Cyrus" appeared on the scene of Iranian government and unified the countries of Media and Persia, creating a great kingdom from the two. Just as Daniel's vision had said that the ram pushes its horns to the West, East, and South, Cyrus also achieved great conquests in all three directions.

He freed the Jews and gave them permission to return to Palestine.

Interestingly, in the Torah, in the Book of Isaiah, Chapter 44, Verse 28, we read: "Who says of Cyrus, 'He is My shepherd, and he shall perform all My pleasure,' saying to Jerusalem, 'You shall be built.'"

This sentence is also noteworthy: in some expressions of the Torah, Cyrus—

[1]: Book of Daniel, Chapter 8, Verses 1-4.

is referred to as the "eagle of the East" and the "man of counsel" who will be called from a far country (Book of Isaiah, Chapter 46, Verse 11).

Second: In the 19th century AD, near Istakhr by the Murghab River, a statue of Cyrus was discovered that is approximately the height of a human. it shows Cyrus in a form where two wings like those of an eagle are opened from his two sides, and he wears a crown on his head in which two horns like those of a ram are seen.

This statue, which is a very valuable example of ancient masonry art, so attracted the attention of scholars that a group of German scientists traveled to Iran just to view it.

From the correspondence of the contents of the Torah with the characteristics of this statue, the probability grew very strong in the view of this scholar as to the root from which the naming of "Cyrus" as "Dhul-Qarnayn" (Owner of Two Horns) originated, and also why the stone statue of Cyrus has wings like those of an eagle. Thus, it became certain to a group of scholars that the historical personality of Dhul-Qarnayn has been fully revealed through this path.

What supports this theory are the moral descriptions written in history for Cyrus.

Herodotus, the Greek historian, writes: "Cyrus" commanded his soldiers not to draw swords except against combatants, and not to kill any enemy soldier who lowers his spear. The army of Cyrus obeyed his command such that the mass of the nation did not feel the calamities of war.

Also, Herodotus writes about him: Cyrus was a noble, generous, very mild, and kind king. Unlike other kings, he was not greedy for accumulating wealth—

but was greedy for generosity and giving. He provided justice to the oppressed and loved whatever involved the most good.

Another historian, Xenophon, writes: Cyrus was a wise and kind king, and the greatness of monarchs was combined in him with the virtues of sages. He had a superior ambition and a dominant existence. His motto was the service of humanity, and his nature was the distribution of justice; humility and magnanimity took the place of pride and vanity in his being.

Interestingly, these historians who described Cyrus in this way were foreign historians, not from his own nation or sons of his homeland; rather, they were Greeks, and we know that the people of Greece did not look upon Cyrus with friendship, for with the conquest of "Lydia" by Cyrus, a great defeat was brought upon the Greek nation.

Proponents of this view say the attributes mentioned in the Holy Quran regarding Dhul-Qarnayn correspond with the attributes of Cyrus.

Beyond all this, Cyrus undertook journeys to the East, West, and North, which are detailed in his life history and are applicable to the three journeys mentioned in the Quran:

The first military expedition of Cyrus was to the country of "Lydia," located in the northern part of Asia Minor, and this country was to the West relative to the center of Cyrus's government.

Whenever we place the map of the western coast of Asia Minor before us, we will see that the greater part of the coast is submerged in small inlets, especially near "Izmir," where the gulf takes the form of a spring.

The Quran says Dhul-Qarnayn, in his western journey, felt the sun setting in a muddy spring.

This scene was the same scene that Cyrus observed when the solar disk was setting (in the view of the observer) in the coastal inlets.

The second military expedition of Cyrus was toward the East, as Herodotus says:

This eastern invasion of Cyrus took place after the conquest of "Lydia," especially the rebellion of some savage desert tribes prompted Cyrus to this attack.

The Quranic expression "until he reached the rising of the sun, he found it rising on a people for whom We had provided no shelter against it" (حَتَّى إِذا بَلَغَ مَطْلِعَ الشَّمْسِ وَجَدَها تَطْلُعُ عَلى‌ قَوْمٍ لَمْ نَجْعَلْ لَهُمْ مِنْ دُونِها سِتْراً) refers to Cyrus's journey to the far East, where he observed the sun rising on a people who had no shelter against its rays—indicating that the people were nomads and desert-dwellers.

Cyrus had a third military expedition toward the North, toward the Caucasus Mountains, until he reached the pass between two mountains. To prevent the invasion of savage tribes, at the request of the people there, he built a firm barrier across the pass.

This pass is currently called the "Darial" Pass, shown on existing maps between "Vladikavkaz" and "Tbilisi." In that very place, an ancient iron wall still exists. This wall is the same barrier that Cyrus built, for the attributes the Quran describes for the barrier of Dhul-Qarnayn correspond perfectly to it.

This was a summary of what has been stated in support of the third theory. [1] It is true that there are points of ambiguity in this theory as well, but for now, it can be named as the best theory regarding the identification of Dhul-Qarnayn with famous historical figures.

[1]: For further explanation, refer to the book Dhul-Qarnayn or Cyrus the Great and also Farhang-e Qisas-e Quran.

3- Where is the Barrier of Dhul-Qarnayn?

Although some wish to identify this barrier with the famous Great Wall of China, which still stands and continues for hundreds of kilometers, it is clear that the Great Wall of China is neither made of iron and copper, nor is it in a narrow mountain pass; rather, it is a wall built of ordinary materials, and as we said, its length is hundreds of kilometers and it still exists.

Others insist that it is the "Ma'rib" Barrier in the land of Yemen, while the Ma'rib Barrier, although built in a mountain pass, was for preventing floods and for the purpose of storing water, and its construction is not of iron and copper.

However, according to the testimony of scholars—as we also indicated above—in the land of the Caucasus, between the Caspian Sea and the Black Sea, there is a range of mountains like a wall that separates the North from the South. The only pass that exists in these wall-like mountains is the famous "Darial" Pass, and in that very place, an ancient iron wall is still visible. For this reason, many believe that the barrier of Dhul-Qarnayn is this very barrier.

Interestingly, nearby is a river named "Syrus," which means "Cyrus" (the Greeks called Cyrus "Syrus").

In ancient Armenian works, this wall is mentioned by the name "Bhag Gurai," and the meaning of this word is "Cyrus's Pass" or "Cyrus's Passage." This document shows that he was the builder of this barrier. [1]

[1]: Again, for further explanation, please refer to the book Dhul-Qarnayn or Cyrus the Great and Farhang-e Qisas-e Quran.

4- Who are Gog and Magog?

In the Holy Quran, Gog and Magog are mentioned in two Surahs: one in the verses under discussion and the other in Surah Al-Anbiya, verse 96.

The Quranic verses clearly testify that these two names belonged to two savage, bloodthirsty tribes that caused severe harassment to the inhabitants around their center of residence.

In the Torah, in the Book of Ezekiel, Chapters 38 and 39, and in the Book of Revelation of John, Chapter 20, they are mentioned as "Gog" and "Magog," the Arabicized forms of which are Ya'juj and Ma'juj.

According to the great commentator, Allamah Tabataba'i in Al-Mizan, it is inferred from the sum of the Torah's statements that Magog, or Gog and Magog, were a large group or groups living in the most distant point of North Asia; they were a warlike and predatory people. [1]

Some believe these two words are Hebrew, but originally transferred from the Greek language to Hebrew. In the Greek language, they were pronounced "Gag" and "Magag," which was transferred in the same form to other European languages.

There are many historical evidences that in the northeastern region of the earth, in the areas of Mongolia, in past times, there was seemingly a bubbling spring of humans. The people of this region reproduced rapidly, and after increasing in number, they would pour toward the East or South, covering these lands like a flowing flood and gradually settling there.

For the flood-like movement of these tribes, various periods are mentioned in history—

[1]: Al-Mizan, Vol. 13, p. 411.

one of which was the period of the invasion of these savage tribes in the 4th century AD under the leadership of "Attila," who destroyed the civilization of the Roman Empire.

Another period, which is also considered the last period of their invasion, took place in the 12th century AD under the leadership of Genghis Khan, who invaded Islamic and Arab countries and destroyed many cities, including Baghdad.

In the era of Cyrus as well, an invasion occurred from their direction, which was around the year 500 BC. But at this date, the unified government of Media and Persia came into existence, the situation changed, and Western Asia was relieved from the attacks of these tribes.

In this way, it seems close to the truth that Gog and Magog were these very savage tribes from whom the people of the Caucasus requested protection during Cyrus's journey to that region, and he proceeded to build the famous barrier of Dhul-Qarnayn. [1]

[1]: For further explanation, refer to the same source.

Ayahs

۞ وَتَرَكْنَا بَعْضَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَمُوجُ فِى بَعْضٍ ۖ وَنُفِخَ فِى ٱلصُّورِ فَجَمَعْنَـٰهُمْ جَمْعًا (٩٩)
That day We shall let them surge over one another, the Trumpet will be blown, and We shall gather them all, (99)
وَعَرَضْنَا جَهَنَّمَ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَرْضًا (١٠٠)
and on that day We shall bring hell into view visibly for the faithless. (100)
ٱلَّذِينَ كَانَتْ أَعْيُنُهُمْ فِى غِطَآءٍ عَن ذِكْرِى وَكَانُوا۟ لَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ سَمْعًا (١٠١)
—Those whose eyes were blind to My remembrance and who could not hear. (101)
أَفَحَسِبَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَن يَتَّخِذُوا۟ عِبَادِى مِن دُونِىٓ أَوْلِيَآءَ ۚ إِنَّآ أَعْتَدْنَا جَهَنَّمَ لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ نُزُلًا (١٠٢)
Do the faithless suppose that they have taken My servants for guardians in My stead? Indeed, We have prepared hell for the hospitality of the faithless. (102)

Commentary

The Abode of the Faithless

In connection with the previous discussion regarding the barrier of Ya’juj and Ma’juj and its destruction on the threshold of the Resurrection, the verses under discussion continue with matters related to the rising of the Day of Judgment, stating: "On that day when the world comes to an end, We shall leave them to surge like waves against one another" (وَ تَرَكْنا بَعْضَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَمُوجُ فِي بَعْضٍ‌).

The expression "surge" (yamuj) is used either because of the immense multitude of humans in that arena—just as in common parlance we say a crowd was "surging"—or because of the anxiety and trembling that will seize the bodies of humans on that day, as if their frames are shaking like the waves of the sea.

Of course, these two interpretations are not contradictory; it is possible that both aspects are intended by this expression.

Then it adds: "Then the Trumpet will be blown, and We shall grant them a new life and gather them all together" (وَ نُفِخَ فِي الصُّورِ فَجَمَعْناهُمْ جَمْعاً).

Undoubtedly, all human beings will be gathered in that arena, and no one is exempt from this law; the expression "We shall gather them in a [total] gathering" (فَجَمَعْناهُمْ جَمْعاً) also points to this very reality.

From the totality of Quranic verses, it is clearly understood that at the end of the world and the beginning of the next world, two great revolutionary transformations occur in the universe: the first transformation is the annihilation of creatures and humans in a sudden event, and the second program—the interval of which from the first is unknown—is the raising of the dead, also through another sudden event. These two programs are referred to in the Quran as the "Blowing of the Trumpet" (Nafkh al-Sur), and God willing, we will explain its details under the commentary of verse 68 of Surah Az-Zumar.

Here, a narration is cited from Asbagh bin Nubata from Ali عليه السلام where the Imam, in interpreting the phrase "We shall leave them to surge like waves against one another," says it refers to the Day of Resurrection [1]. It might be imagined that this narration contradicts what we mentioned above in the interpretation of the verse, as we interpreted it as the stage of the world's annihilation (as suggested by the context of the preceding and succeeding verses).

However, paying attention to one point resolves this objection: sometimes the "Day of Resurrection" is used in a broad sense that includes its preliminaries, and we know that one of its preliminaries is the sudden annihilation of the world.

Then, it proceeds to describe the state of the disbelievers, mentioning both the consequence of their deeds and the attributes that lead to that fate, saying: "And We shall present Hell on that day to the disbelievers, a [full] presentation" (وَ عَرَضْنا جَهَنَّمَ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلْكافِرِينَ عَرْضاً).

Hell, with its various torments and different painful punishments, will become completely visible and manifest before them. This very observation and its appearance before them is itself a painful and soul-crushing torment, let alone when they actually fall into it.

Who are the disbelievers and why do they meet such a fate? In one short sentence, it introduces them: "Those whose eyes were under a veil from My remembrance" (الَّذِينَ كانَتْ أَعْيُنُهُمْ فِي غِطاءٍ عَنْ ذِكْرِي‌), meaning they were unable to see the face of truth and remember Me.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Ayyashi (as quoted in Tafsir al-Mizan under the verse in question).

"...and those who had ears but were unable to hear" (وَ كانُوا لا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ سَمْعاً).

In reality, they had disabled the most important means of seeking truth and perceiving realities—those things which are the factors of human felicity or misery. That is, they had plunged their seeing eyes and hearing ears into a thick and heavy veil due to wrong thoughts, prejudices, animosities, and other vile attributes.

It is interesting that regarding the eye, it says: their eyes were in a covering away from My remembrance, indicating that because they were in the covering and veil of negligence, they did not see the signs of Allah; and because they did not see the truth, they followed myths and forgot Allah.

Yes, the face of truth is manifest, and everything in this world speaks to man; only a seeing eye and a hearing ear are required, and nothing more.

In other words, the remembrance of Allah is not something seen with the eye; what is seen are His signs, but His signs are the cause of His remembrance.

The next verse points to a point of their intellectual deviation which was the primary foundation for their other deviations, saying: "Do the disbelievers think that they can take My servants as protectors and guardians instead of Me?" (أَ فَحَسِبَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَنْ يَتَّخِذُوا عِبادِي مِنْ دُونِي أَوْلِياءَ).

Do these servants who were made objects of worship—such as the Messiah and the angels—no matter how high their status, possess anything of their own to be able to protect others? Or on the contrary, did they themselves receive whatever they had from Allah, and were even themselves in need of His guidance? This is a reality they forgot and thus sank into polytheism.

At the end of the verse, for further emphasis, He says: "Indeed, We have prepared Hell as an abode for the disbelievers, and We shall entertain them in this lodging!" (إِنَّا أَعْتَدْنا جَهَنَّمَ لِلْكافِرِينَ نُزُلًا).

"Nuzul" (on the pattern of 'rusul') has been used to mean both a "lodging/abode" and "that which is prepared for the entertainment of a guest." Some have said it refers to the first thing with which a guest is welcomed, such as a drink or fruit brought for the guest upon their initial arrival.

Ayahs

قُلْ هَلْ نُنَبِّئُكُم بِٱلْأَخْسَرِينَ أَعْمَـٰلًا (١٠٣)
Say, ‘Shall we inform you who are the biggest losers in their works? (103)
ٱلَّذِينَ ضَلَّ سَعْيُهُمْ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَهُمْ يَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُمْ يُحْسِنُونَ صُنْعًا (١٠٤)
Those whose efforts are misguided in the life of the world, while they suppose they are doing good.’ (104)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ رَبِّهِمْ وَلِقَآئِهِۦ فَحَبِطَتْ أَعْمَـٰلُهُمْ فَلَا نُقِيمُ لَهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ وَزْنًا (١٠٥)
They are the ones who deny the signs of their Lord and encounter with Him. So their works have failed. On the Day of Resurrection We will not give them any weight. (105)
ذَٰلِكَ جَزَآؤُهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ بِمَا كَفَرُوا۟ وَٱتَّخَذُوٓا۟ ءَايَـٰتِى وَرُسُلِى هُزُوًا (١٠٦)
That is their requital—hell—because of their unfaith and for deriding My signs and apostles. (106)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ كَانَتْ لَهُمْ جَنَّـٰتُ ٱلْفِرْدَوْسِ نُزُلًا (١٠٧)
As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, they shall have the gardens of Firdaws for abode, (107)
خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا لَا يَبْغُونَ عَنْهَا حِوَلًا (١٠٨)
to remain in them [forever]; they will not seek to leave it for another place. (108)

Commentary

The Most Destitute of People

These verses, and the subsequent ones until the end of this Surah, while providing an explanation of the characteristics of the faithless, serve as a sort of conclusion to all the discussions presented in this Surah—especially the accounts related to the People of the Cave, Moses and Khidr, and Dhul-Qarnayn, and their struggles against their opponents.

First, it proceeds to identify the most loss-stricken of human beings and the most wretched of individuals. However, to stimulate the curiosity of the listeners regarding such an important matter, it presents it in the form of a question and commands the Prophet: "Say: Shall We inform you of those who are the greatest losers in respect of their deeds?" (قُلْ هَلْ نُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِالْأَخْسَرِينَ أَعْمالًا).

Immediately, He provides the answer Himself so that the listener does not remain in suspense for long: "Those whose efforts are lost and wasted in the life of this world, while they think they are doing good deeds!" (الَّذِينَ ضَلَّ سَعْيُهُمْ فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَ هُمْ يَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُمْ يُحْسِنُونَ صُنْعاً).

Certainly, the concept of "loss" (khusran) does not merely mean that a person misses out on profits; rather, true loss is when one loses the original capital itself. What capital is greater and higher than intellect, intelligence, God-given faculties, life, youth, and health? These are the very things whose product is human actions, and our deeds are the manifestation of our strengths and powers.

When these powers are transformed into destructive or futile actions, it is as if they have all been lost and annihilated. It is exactly like a person who takes a vast capital with him to the market but loses it midway and returns empty-handed.

Of course, if a person knows they have lost their capital, although they have suffered a loss, it is not a "dangerous" loss, because this loss teaches him lessons for the future that are sometimes equivalent to or greater than that capital; thus, in reality, he has not lost anything.

However, the real loss and the "double loss" occur when a person loses his material and spiritual capitals in a wrong and deviant path while imagining he has done something good. He has neither gained a result from these efforts, nor learned a lesson from his loss, nor is he safe from repeating this mistake.

It is interesting that the expression used here is "أَخْسَرِينَ أَعْمالًا" (greatest losers in deeds - plural) whereas it could have been "أَخْسَرِينَ عَمَلًا" (greatest losers in deed - singular, as the tamyiz is usually singular). This phrasing may indicate that they are not trapped in loss in just one "market of action," but rather their "compounded ignorance" (jahl-e murakkab) has caused loss in all their life programs and all their deeds and works.

In other words, sometimes a person suffers a loss in one line of trade but profits in another, and at the end of the year, calculating them together, he finds he hasn't lost much. But the misery is when a person suffers a loss in every single field in which he has invested.

Furthermore, the expression "being lost" (dalla) likely refers to the fact that human deeds do not vanish from the universe in any case—just as matter and energy in the world, though constantly changing form, do not disappear—but they sometimes become "lost" because their effects are not visible and no use is made of them, similar to lost capitals that are out of our reach and useless.

Regarding why a person falls into such a psychological state, we will discuss this in the "Key Points."

The following verses proceed to introduce the characteristics and beliefs of this group of losers, stating several traits that are the root of all their misfortunes: First, it says: They are those who disbelieved in the signs of their Lord (أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِآياتِ رَبِّهِمْ).

Signs that make the eyes and ears see and hear; signs that tear through the veils of arrogance and manifest the face of reality before man; and finally, signs that are light and illumination, leading man out of the darkness of illusions and imaginations toward the land of truths.

Secondly, after forgetting God, they disbelieved in the "Resurrection" and the "Meeting with Allah" (وَ لِقائِهِ).

Indeed, until faith in the "Resurrection" is placed alongside faith in the "Origin" (Mabda'), and man does not feel that a Power is monitoring his actions and preserving them all for a great, precise, and rigorous court, he will not maintain a correct account of his deeds and will not be reformed.

Then it adds: "Because of this disbelief in the Origin and the Resurrection, their deeds have become null and void" (فَحَبِطَتْ أَعْمالُهُمْ).

Just like ashes before a mighty storm.

And since they have no deed that is measurable or valuable, "We shall not assign any weight or balance for them on the Day of Resurrection" (فَلا نُقِيمُ لَهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ وَزْناً).

For weighing and measuring pertain to where there is something of substance; those who have nothing of substance, how can they have any weighing or measurement?!

Then, while stating their punishment, it mentions the third factor of their deviation, misfortune, and loss, saying: "Their recompense is Hell because they disbelieved and took My signs and My messengers in mockery and ridicule" (ذلِكَ جَزاؤُهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ بِما كَفَرُوا وَ اتَّخَذُوا آياتِي وَ رُسُلِي هُزُواً) [1]. In this way, they have denied the three fundamental principles of religious belief (Origin, Resurrection, and the Mission of the Prophets) or, even worse than denial, they have subjected them to mockery.

The characteristics of the disbelievers and the most loss-stricken people, as well as their ultimate end, have been well understood. Now we turn to the believers and their destiny, so that through the principle of contrast, the situation of both sides becomes completely clear. The Quran says in this regard: "Indeed, those who believed and did righteous deeds, the Gardens of Firdaus (Paradise) shall be their hospitality" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ كانَتْ لَهُمْ جَنَّاتُ الْفِرْدَوْسِ نُزُلًا).

[1]: There is a discussion among commentators regarding the syntax of the sentence "ذلِكَ جَزاؤُهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ": Some consider "ذلِكَ" as the subject (mubtada'), "جَزاؤُهُمْ" as the predicate (khabar), and "جَهَنَّمُ" as an appositive (badal) for dhalika. While others consider the subject to be omitted and "ذلِكَ" as its predicate, and "جَزاؤُهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ" as another subject and predicate, making the implied meaning: "The matter is this: their recompense is Hell." However, it is clear that the first explanation is more appropriate.

"Firdaus," as some great commentators have stated, is a garden that encompasses all blessings and all necessary bounties; thus, "Firdaus" is the best and highest of the gardens of Paradise.

And since the perfection of a blessing is realized only when the hand of cessation does not reach it, it immediately adds: "They shall abide therein forever" (خالِدِينَ فِيها). And despite the fact that the hard-to-please and change-seeking nature of man constantly demands transformation, variety, and change, "the inhabitants of Firdaus will never desire any transfer or change from it" (لا يَبْغُونَ عَنْها حِوَلًا).

This is because everything they desire is there, even variety and perfection, as will be explained in the "Key Points."

Key Points

1- Who are the "Greatest Losers in Respect of Their Deeds"?

We have often seen in our own lives and the lives of others that sometimes a person performs a wrong action while thinking they have done something good and significant. This kind of "compounded ignorance" (jahl-e murakkab) may last for a moment, a year, or even a lifetime; and truly, no misfortune greater than this can be imagined.

If we see the Quran naming such people as the "greatest losers," the reason is clear: those who commit a sin but know they are wrongdoers usually set a limit for their wrongdoing and at least do not gallop headlong into it. Often, they come to their senses and seek repentance and righteous deeds to compensate. But those who are sinners and yet perceive their sin as worship, their evil deeds as righteous, and their deviations as correctness, will not only fail to seek compensation but will continue their work with the utmost intensity, even employing all the capitals of their existence in this path. What a striking expression the Quran uses for them: (بِالْأَخْسَرِينَ أَعْمالًا).

In Islamic narrations, various interpretations have been given for the "greatest losers," each pointing to a clear instance of this broad concept without limiting it. In a hadith from Asbagh ibn Nubatah, we read that a person asked Imam Ali عليه السلام for the interpretation of this verse. The Imam replied: "It refers to the Jews and Christians; they were initially on the truth, then they introduced innovations into their religion, and while these innovations led them to the path of deviation, they imagined they were doing good" [1]. In another hadith from the same Imam عليه السلام, we read that after mentioning the above, he said: "The Kharijites of Nahrawan were not far from them either!" [2]. In another hadith, specific reference is made to "monks" (men and women who renounce the world) and groups of innovators among Muslims [3]. In some narrations, it is interpreted as "those who deny the Wilayah (guardianship) of Amir al-Mu'minin Ali عليه السلام" [4].

Monks who spend a lifetime in the corners of monasteries, subjecting themselves to various deprivations, forgoing marriage, giving up good clothing and food, and prioritizing monastic life over everything, imagining that these deprivations cause them to draw near to God—are they not instances of the "greatest losers in deeds"?

Is it possible for any divine religion or creed to invite a social human being to isolation, contrary to the laws of reason and nature, and consider this the source of proximity to God?

Likewise, those who introduced innovations into God's religion, replacing Monotheism with the Trinity, and presenting Christ, the servant of God, as the son of God, and other such superstitions into the pure divine creed, thinking they were doing a service—are such individuals not among the most loss-stricken of people? The "Kharijites of Nahrawan," that hollow-brained and ignorant group who considered the greatest sins (such as killing Ali عليه السلام and a group of true Muslims and the elite of Islam) as a means of proximity to God and even assumed Paradise was exclusively theirs—were they not among the greatest losers? In short, the verse has such a broad meaning that it encompasses many peoples in the past, present, and future.

Now the question arises: what is the source of this dangerous deviant state? Certainly, intense prejudices, vanities, arrogance, self-centeredness, and self-love are among the most important factors in the emergence of such false imaginations.

Sometimes the flattery and sycophancy of others, and sometimes sitting in isolation and being the sole judge of one's own case, causes this state to arise, beautifying all of a person's deviant and ugly deeds and thoughts in his own eyes—to the extent that instead of feeling shame and disgrace for these uglinesses, he feels pride, honor, and boastfulness. As the Quran states elsewhere: "Is he, then, to whom the evil of his deed has been made fair-seeming, so that he considers it good...?" (أَ فَمَنْ زُيِّنَ لَهُ سُوءُ عَمَلِهِ فَرَآهُ حَسَناً) (Fatir, Verse 8).

In some other verses of the Quran, the agent of this "beautifying of ugliness" is identified as Satan; and certainly, Satan's tools within the human being are those very ugly and deviant traits and dispositions: "And [remember] when Satan made their deeds seem fair to them and said, 'No one can overcome you today from among the people, and indeed, I am your neighbor [protector]!'" (وَ إِذْ زَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الشَّيْطانُ أَعْمالَهُمْ وَ قالَ لا غالِبَ لَكُمُ الْيَوْمَ مِنَ النَّاسِ وَ إِنِّي جارٌ لَكُمْ) (Surah Al-Anfal, Verse 48).

After mentioning the story of Pharaoh's famous tower, the Quran says: "Thus was the evil of his deed made fair-seeming to Pharaoh" (وَ كَذلِكَ زُيِّنَ لِفرْعَوْنَ سُوءُ عَمَلِهِ) (so that he engaged in such foolish and ridiculous acts to combat God and imagined he had done something significant) (Mu'min, 36-37).

[1], [2], [3]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, p. 312. [4]: Ibid, p. 311.

2- What is "Meeting with Allah" (Liqa' Allah)?

Although some pseudo-scholars of superstition have inferred from such verses that God can be seen in the other world, interpreting "meeting" as a sensory encounter.

However, it is obvious that a sensory meeting necessitates physicality, and physicality necessitates being limited, being in need, and being perishable; and every rational person knows that God cannot possess such attributes.

Therefore, without doubt, the meaning of "meeting" (liqa') or "vision" (ru'yah) in various verses of the Quran when attributed to God is not a sensory meeting but rather an "inner witnessing" (shuhud-e batini).

That is, in the Resurrection, because man observes the signs of God more and better than at any other time, he sees Him clearly with the eye of the heart, and his faith in God becomes a "witnessing faith." For this reason, according to the verses of the Quran, even the most stubborn deniers of God will confess on the Day of Resurrection because they see no way to deny [1]. A group of commentators has also understood the concept of this expression to be the observation of blessings and rewards, as well as divine punishments and retributions, effectively implying the words "blessing," "reward," or "recompense."

Of these two interpretations, while they are not mutually exclusive, the first interpretation seems clearer.

3- The Weighing of Deeds

We do not need to interpret the issue of weighing deeds on the Day of Resurrection through the "embodiment of deeds" and say that human action there turns into a physical object possessing weight, because "weighing" (tauzin) has a broad meaning and encompasses any kind of measurement. For example, regarding people without character, we say they are "weightless" people or they are "light," while the intention is their lack of character, not physical weight.

Interestingly, in the verses above regarding the group of "greatest losers in deeds," it says: "We shall not assign any weight for them on the Day of Resurrection." Does this contradict the verses that say: "And the weighing on that Day is the truth" (وَ الْوَزْنُ يَوْمَئِذٍ الْحَقُّ) (Al-A'raf, 8)? Certainly not, because weighing is for those who have performed an action capable of being measured. But someone whose entire existence, thoughts, and deeds do not have the weight of even a fly's wing, what need is there for measurement?! Hence, in a famous narration from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read:

"Indeed, a great, fat man will be brought on the Day of Resurrection who will not weigh as much as a gnat's wing" [2]! This is because in this world, their deeds, their thoughts, and their character were all hollow and empty.

From this, it becomes clear that people there are of several groups: 1. A group so burdened with good deeds and righteous actions that there is no need for weighing or accounting in their case, and they enter Paradise without an account. 2. Another group whose deeds have become so nullified and void, or who completely lack righteous deeds, that they also have no need for weighing; they too enter Hell without an account! 3. But the third group are those who possess both good and evil deeds; their case will be subject to weighing and accounting, and perhaps the majority of people fall into this third category.

[1]: Refer to Surah Al-Mu'minun, Verse 106. [2]: Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses in question.

4- Interpretation of the Phrase "لا يَبْغُونَ عَنْها حِوَلًا"

"Hiwal" (on the pattern of milal) has an infinitive meaning and signifies "transformation" and "transfer of place." As we said in the commentary of the verses, "Firdaus" is a garden of Paradise where the best blessings and divine bounties are gathered, and for this reason, it is the best spot in that world; therefore, its inhabitants never desire to move from it.

It might be asked: then life there will be monotonous and stagnant, and this itself is a great flaw.

In response, we say there is no obstacle to transformation and perfection continuing in that very region; that is, the means of perfection are gathered there, and human beings—in the light of the deeds they performed in this world and the bounties God has bestowed upon them in that world—constantly move forward on the path of perfection.

God willing, under appropriate verses, we will explain the discussion regarding human perfection even in Paradise more elaborately.

5- Who is Firdaus for?

Another question that arises here is that "Firdaus," as mentioned, is the highest place in Paradise [1], and in the verses above we read that Firdaus is the dwelling place of those who believe and perform righteous deeds. If this is so, other regions of Paradise should have no inhabitants, because a non-believer has no way into Paradise.

In response to this question, it can be said that the above verses do not refer to every individual who has faith and righteous deeds, but rather set a high degree of faith and righteous action as the criterion for entering Firdaus. Although the appearance of the verse is absolute, consideration of the concept of the word "Firdaus" qualifies it.

Hence, in Surah Al-Mu'minun, which describes the qualities of the inheritors of Firdaus, it mentions a high level of believers' attributes that are not present in all individuals, and this itself is an indication that the inhabitants of Firdaus must possess distinguished qualities in addition to faith and righteous deeds.

Also for this reason, in the hadith we previously quoted from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that he says: "Whenever you ask God for Paradise, specifically ask for Firdaus, for it is the most comprehensive and perfect of the dwellings of Paradise," indicating that the ambition of believers must be high in everything and at all times; even in desiring Paradise, one should not be content with lower stages, even though the lower stages are also immersed in blessing.

Obviously, someone who makes such a request from God must surely prepare himself to reach such a station; he must surely exert the maximum effort and endeavor to acquire the best human traits and perform the most righteous deeds.

Therefore, those who say, "May God grant that we enter Paradise even if we are in its lowest stages," are individuals who do not possess a sufficient share of the high ambition of true believers.

[1]: Some have said this word is originally from the Roman language, and some consider its root to be from the Ethiopic language, which then transferred to Arabic (Tafsir Fakhr Razi and Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan).

Ayahs

قُل لَّوْ كَانَ ٱلْبَحْرُ مِدَادًا لِّكَلِمَـٰتِ رَبِّى لَنَفِدَ ٱلْبَحْرُ قَبْلَ أَن تَنفَدَ كَلِمَـٰتُ رَبِّى وَلَوْ جِئْنَا بِمِثْلِهِۦ مَدَدًا (١٠٩)
Say, ‘If the sea were ink for the words of my Lord, the sea would be spent before the words of my Lord are finished, though We replenish it with another like it.’ (109)
قُلْ إِنَّمَآ أَنَا۠ بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُكُمْ يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَىَّ أَنَّمَآ إِلَـٰهُكُمْ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ ۖ فَمَن كَانَ يَرْجُوا۟ لِقَآءَ رَبِّهِۦ فَلْيَعْمَلْ عَمَلًا صَـٰلِحًا وَلَا يُشْرِكْ بِعِبَادَةِ رَبِّهِۦٓ أَحَدًۢا (١١٠)
Say, ‘I am just a human being like you. It has been revealed to me that your God is the One God. So whoever expects to encounter his Lord—let him act righteously, and not associate anyone with the worship of his Lord.’ (110)

Circumstances of Revelation

Regarding the circumstances of the revelation of this verse, it is narrated from Ibn Abbas that when the Jews heard this Divine statement from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله: "وَمَا أُوتِيتُمْ مِنَ الْعِلْمِ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا" (And you have not been given of knowledge except a little), they said: "How can such a thing be correct? While we have been given the Torah, and whoever has been given the Torah has been given abundant good." At this moment, the first of the above verses was revealed (providing a depiction of God's infinite knowledge and the insignificance of our knowledge compared to His).

Some say the Jews said to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "Allah has given you wisdom, and 'وَمَنْ يُؤْتَ الْحِكْمَةَ فَقَدْ أُوتِيَ خَيْراً كَثِيراً' (And whoever is given wisdom has indeed been given much good), yet when we ask you about the 'Spirit' (Ruh), you give a vague answer?!" Then the above verse was revealed (showing that no matter how much knowledge a human possesses, it is very insignificant compared to the endless knowledge of Allah). [^1]

Commentary

Those Who Hope for the Meeting with Allah

While the above verses pursue an independent discussion, they are connected to the overall themes of this Surah. Each of the three important stories in this Surah pulled back the curtain on new and wondrous matters. It is as if the Quran, in these verses, intends to say that awareness of the story of the People of the Cave, Moses and Khidr, and Dhul-Qarnayn is not a significant matter compared to the infinite knowledge of Allah, for His knowledge encompasses all of creation and the universe—past, present, and future.

In any case, in the first verse under discussion, the Quran tells the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "Say: If the sea were ink for writing the words of my Lord, the sea would surely be exhausted before the words of my Lord were exhausted, even if We brought the like of it to replenish it" (قُلْ لَوْ كانَ الْبَحْرُ مِداداً لِكَلِماتِ رَبِّي لَنَفِدَ الْبَحْرُ قَبْلَ أَنْ تَنْفَدَ كَلِماتُ رَبِّي وَ لَوْ جِئْنا بِمِثْلِهِ مَدَداً).

"Midad" (ink) refers to ink or the colored substance used for writing. Originally, it is derived from "madd" (extension), because through its extension, lines become visible. [^1] "Kalimat" (Words) is the plural of "Kalimah" (word), which originally means the utterances spoken, or in other words, a term that signifies a meaning. However, since every being in this world is a proof of the knowledge and power of the Lord, sometimes the term "Kalimat Allah" (Word of Allah) is applied to every creature, especially to significant and magnificent beings.

The Holy Quran says regarding Jesus Christ عليه السلام: "إِنَّمَا الْمَسِيحُ عِيسَى ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ رَسُولُ اللَّهِ وَ كَلِمَتُهُ أَلْقاها إِلى‌ مَرْيَمَ" (The Messiah, Jesus, the son of Mary, was but a messenger of Allah and His word which He directed to Mary) (Surah An-Nisa, 171).

In the verse under discussion, "word" also carries this meaning; it refers to the beings of the universe, each of which narrates the various attributes of the Lord.

In truth, the Quran in this verse draws attention to the reality that you should not imagine the universe is limited to what you see, know, or perceive. Rather, it is so vast and magnificent that if the seas were to become ink and they wished to write the names, attributes, and characteristics of these beings, the seas would be exhausted before they could enumerate the creatures of the universe.

It is also necessary to note that "sea" here represents the genus (all seas), and similarly, the word "like" in the phrase "even if We brought the like of it to replenish it" also conveys the genus, indicating that no matter how many similar seas are added, the Divine words will not end.

For this reason, the above verse does not contradict a similar verse in Surah Luqman which states: "وَلَوْ أَنَّ مَا فِي الْأَرْضِ مِنْ شَجَرَةٍ أَقْلَامٌ وَالْبَحْرُ يَمُدُّهُ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ سَبْعَةُ أَبْحُرٍ مَا نَفِدَتْ كَلِمَاتُ اللَّهِ" (And if whatever trees upon the earth were pens and the sea [was ink], replenished thereafter by seven [more] seas, the words of Allah would not be exhausted) (Luqman, 27).

This means these pens would break and those inks would be finished to the last drop, yet the description of the creatures and the secrets and realities of the universe would remain.

An important point to consider here is that while the above verse visualizes the infinite expansion of the universe in the past, present, and future, it is also a depiction of the unlimited knowledge of Allah. For we know that Allah has encompassing knowledge of all that has been and will be in the expanse of existence; rather, His knowledge, by virtue of being "Knowledge by Presence" (al-ilm al-huduri), is inseparable from the existence of these beings (reflect deeply).

Thus, in other words, it can be said: if all the oceans on earth became ink and all the trees became pens, they would never be able to record what is in the knowledge of Allah.

A Depiction of Infinity

To visualize the concept of infinity and to bring the meaning of Allah's endless knowledge and the extraordinary vastness of the universe closer to our minds, the Holy Quran employs a very eloquent and expressive style here, using "living and vibrant numbers."

Do numbers have "living" and "dead" forms?

Yes, the numbers used in mathematics, obtained by multiplying "zeros" to the right of integers, are in reality "dead" numbers; they can never truly visualize the greatness of something.

Those who deal with mathematics know that a number "one" with a kilometer of zeros to its right is an extraordinarily massive and dizzying number, the magnitude of which is truly difficult to conceive. But for whom? For mathematicians. For the masses, however, it can never provide a visualization of greatness.

A "living number" is one that takes our thoughts along with it as far as it goes and visualizes reality as it is; it must have a soul, greatness, and a voice.

Instead of saying that Allah's creatures in the expanse of existence are far more than a number with a hundred kilometers of zeros beside it, the Quran says: If all the trees on earth were pens, and all the seas were ink, all these pens would break and perish, and all these inks would be exhausted to the last drop, yet the realities of the universe, its secrets and mysteries, the beings of this world, and the knowledge of the Lord would not end.

Think carefully: how much writing power does one pen have? Then think: how many pens can be made from one small tree branch? After that, how many thousands or millions of pens can be prepared from a large tree with its massive trunk? Then consider the total number of trees on earth in all forests and gardens, and calculate the pens made from them! On the other hand, think about how many words can be written with one drop of ink?

Then multiply that by a pool, a lake, a sea, an ocean, and finally all the seas on earth. What an amazing number it forms!! The greatness of this statement becomes even clearer when we realize that the number "seven" (sab'a) here is not a number of "count" but a number of "multiplicity." In other words, its meaning is that even if many seas were added to that sea, the words of Allah would not end.

Think about how living and vibrant this number is; it is a number that pulls human thought along and advances it toward infinity. It is a number whose greatness anyone—whether a mathematician or illiterate—can grasp and become acquainted with its vastness and majesty.

Yes, the knowledge of Allah is even higher than this number. It is a knowledge that is unlimited and infinite. It is a knowledge whose domain is the entire universe and encompasses the past and future of the world's history and all secrets, mysteries, and realities.

The second verse, which is the final verse of Surah Al-Kahf, is a collection of the fundamental principles of religious belief—namely Monotheism (Tawhid), the Resurrection (Ma'ad), and the Mission of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. In fact, it is the same theme with which Surah Al-Kahf began, as the beginning also spoke of Allah, revelation, the reward of deeds, and the Day of Judgment. In one sense, it is an essence and summary of the entire Surah, a significant part of which revolved around these three subjects.

Since the issue of Prophethood throughout history has been accompanied by various forms of exaggeration (ghuluw), the verse expresses it as follows:

"Say: I am only a human being like you; the only distinction is that revelation is sent down to me" (قُلْ إِنَّما أَنَا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُكُمْ يُوحى‌ إِلَيَ‌).

With this expression, a red line is drawn through all polytheistic imaginary distinctions that elevate prophets from the stage of humanity to the stage of divinity.

Then, from among all the matters revealed, it places its finger on the issue of Monotheism and says: "It is revealed to me that your God is only one God" (أَنَّما إِلهُكُمْ إِلهٌ واحِدٌ).

Why is only this issue mentioned? Because Monotheism is the essence of all beliefs and all individual and social programs that bring happiness to humanity. We have stated elsewhere that Monotheism is not just one principle among the principles of religion, but rather the leaven of all the principles and branches of Islam.

If, in a simple example, we were to liken the teachings of religion—from principles to branches—to pearl beads, Monotheism should be likened to the string that connects these beads and creates a valuable and beautiful necklace from the whole. Or, if each of the religious teachings were likened to a limb of the human body, we must say that Monotheism is the spirit breathed into the entirety of this body.

In the discussions of Resurrection and Prophethood, the reality has been established that they are not separate from Monotheism. That is, when we know Allah with all His attributes, we know that such a God must send prophets, and His wisdom and justice necessitate the existence of a court of justice and a resurrection. Social issues, the entire human society, and everything related to it must be a reflection of Monotheism and unity in order to be organized.

For this reason, we read in the Hadiths that the phrase "La ilaha illa Allah" is the strong fortress of the Lord, and whoever enters it is safe from Divine punishment. We have also all heard that at the beginning of Islam, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله would say: "If you seek salvation, come under the banner of Monotheism" (Say: there is no god but Allah, and you will succeed).

The third sentence of this verse refers to the issue of Resurrection and links it to the issue of Monotheism with the "fa" of derivation (fa al-ta'riq), saying: "So whoever hopes for the meeting with his Lord, let him do righteous work" (فَمَنْ كانَ يَرْجُوا لِقاءَ رَبِّهِ فَلْيَعْمَلْ عَمَلًا صالِحاً).

The "meeting with the Lord," which is the inner observation of His Pure Essence with the eye of the heart and internal insight, is possible for true believers even in this world. However, since this matter becomes universal and public on the Day of Judgment due to the observation of more numerous, clearer, and more explicit signs, this expression in the language of the Quran is usually used regarding the Day of Resurrection. On the other hand, it is natural that if a person expects and hopes for something, they must prepare themselves to receive it.

One who claims "I am waiting for something" while no effect is visible in their actions is, in reality, nothing more than a false claimant. For this reason, in the above verse, "let him do righteous work" (فَلْيَعْمَلْ عَمَلًا صالِحاً) is expressed in the imperative form—a command that is a necessary consequence of the hope and expectation of meeting the Lord.

In the final sentence, it describes the reality of righteous work in a short statement: "and let him not associate anyone in the worship of his Lord" (وَ لا يُشْرِكْ بِعِبادَةِ رَبِّهِ أَحَداً).

In clearer terms, until the reality of purity and sincerity (ikhlas) enters the action, it will not take on the color of a "righteous work." It is the Divine and Godly motive that gives depth to human action, grants it luminosity, and provides the correct direction. When sincerity is lost, the action becomes more superficial, leans toward personal interests, and loses its depth, authenticity, and correct direction.

In truth, this righteous work, which has originated from and been mixed with Divine motive and sincerity, is the passport for meeting the Lord!

And as we have previously indicated, "righteous work" has such a broad meaning that it includes every useful and constructive individual and social program in all fields of life.

Sincerity: The Spirit of Righteous Work

In Islamic narrations, extraordinary importance has been given to the issue of "intention" (niyyah). In fact, the program of Islam is that it accepts every action based on the color of its intention and motive. The famous Hadith of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, "No action is [accepted] except by intention," which we have all heard, expresses this very reality.

After the issue of intention, emphasis is placed on "sincerity" (ikhlas); if it exists, the action is extraordinarily valuable, otherwise, it lacks value. Sincerity means that a person's motive is pure from any non-Divine taint. It can be called "Monotheism of Intention"—meaning thinking only of the Lord and His pleasure in all programs.

Interestingly, regarding the circumstances of the revelation of the above verse, it is narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that a person came to him and said: "O Messenger of Allah! I spend in the way of Allah and maintain family ties, and I perform these deeds only for the sake of Allah. However, when people talk about these deeds of mine and praise them, I feel delighted and happy. How are these deeds of mine?"

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله remained silent and said nothing until the above verse was revealed and answered this question (that only the deed accompanied by complete sincerity is acceptable in the sight of Allah). [^1] Undoubtedly, this narration does not refer to that involuntary state of joy, but rather to a state that becomes the motive for a person's action or indicates a lack of sincerity in intention.

Pure action is of such importance in Islam that we read in a Hadith from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "Whoever remains sincere to Allah for forty days, Allah will cause the springs of wisdom and knowledge to flow from his heart to his tongue." [^2]

O Lord! Make our intentions so pure in all our deeds that we think of none but You, and take no step for other than You; rather, whatever we want other than You and whomever we want besides You, let it be for the sake of Your pleasure and its connection and relationship to You. "Ameen, O Lord of the Worlds."

End of Surah Al-Kahf and end of Volume 12. 1st of Jumada al-Thani, 1402 (March 27, 1982).
